# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nearest Neighbors (K-NN)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)** algorithm is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning method used for classification and regression. It classifies an unknown sample by assigning it the class label most frequently represented among its *k* closest training examples in the feature space, where "closeness" is measured by a distance metric.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of moving to a **new neighborhood** and trying to figure out which political party most residents support. If you simply ask your **3 nearest neighbors** (k=3) and 2 of them support Party A while 1 supports Party B, you conclude that the neighborhood predominantly supports **Party A**. The k-NN algorithm operates on the same intuitive principle — *the label of an unknown point is decided by the majority vote of its k closest known points*. The word "closest" is the key, and it is quantified by mathematical distance.

### Key Terminology (with KTU Standard Metrics)
- **Training Set:** A collection of labeled samples $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.
- **Feature Vector:** An $n$-dimensional real-valued vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ describing each sample.
- **Distance Metric:** A function $d: \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ satisfying non-negativity, symmetry, identity, and triangle inequality.
- **Hyperparameter *k*:** The number of neighbors consulted to make a decision (must be an **odd integer** for binary classification to avoid ties).
- **Decision Boundary:** A hypersurface in feature space that separates regions belonging to different classes.

---

## 1.2 Bayesian Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Bayesian Classification** is a probabilistic supervised learning approach grounded in **Bayes' Theorem**. It computes the posterior probability $P(c \mid x)$ that a sample with feature vector $x$ belongs to class $c$, and assigns the sample to the class with the highest posterior probability — yielding the **Bayes Optimal Classifier** in terms of minimum misclassification rate.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. You know how prevalent a disease is in the population (the *prior*), and you have observed the patient's symptoms (the *evidence*). Based on the symptoms, you ask: *"Given that the patient has these symptoms, how likely is each possible disease?"* You then pick the most likely disease. Bayesian classification formalizes this exact reasoning using a theorem first derived by Reverend Thomas Bayes in **1763**. The classifier does not memorize data — it reasons probabilistically.

### Key Terminology
- **Prior Probability $P(c)$:** The initial belief about a class before observing any data.
- **Likelihood $P(x \mid c)$:** The probability of observing the feature vector $x$ given that the class is $c$.
- **Posterior Probability $P(c \mid x)$:** The updated probability of class $c$ after observing $x$.
- **Evidence $P(x)$:** The total probability of observing $x$ under all classes (a normalizing constant).
- **Naïve Bayes Assumption:** Features are conditionally independent given the class label.

### Why These Methods Matter in Machine Vision
- **k-NN** is widely used in **image recognition, content-based image retrieval, and optical character recognition (OCR)**.
- **Bayesian classifiers** are used in **spam detection, medical image diagnosis, and real-time object detection** (e.g., Naive Bayes in autonomous vehicle vision).
- Both are foundational to many advanced deep learning techniques (e.g., Bayesian Neural Networks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) Algorithm

### Operational Logic Steps
- **Step 1 — Input:** Receive an unlabeled query point $x_q$ and the training set $\mathcal{T} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Step 2 — Distance Computation:** For every training point, compute the distance $d(x_q, x_i)$ using a chosen metric.
- **Step 3 — Sorting:** Rank all training points in **ascending order** of distance from $x_q$.
- **Step 4 — Neighbor Selection:** Select the first $k$ points from the sorted list — these are the *k* nearest neighbors $\mathcal{N}_k(x_q)$.
- **Step 5 — Voting (Classification):** Assign the label $\hat{y}$ that occurs most frequently in $\mathcal{N}_k(x_q)$. Formally:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \sum_{x_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(x_q)} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)
$$

- **Step 6 — Averaging (Regression):** Output the mean of the labels of the $k$ neighbors.
- **Step 7 — Output:** Return $\hat{y}$ as the predicted class (or value) for $x_q$.

### Distance Metrics Used in k-NN

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Use Case |
|--------|---------|----------------------|
| **Euclidean Distance ($L_2$)** | $d(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(p_i - q_i)^2}$ | Standard geometric similarity; image pixel difference |
| **Manhattan Distance ($L_1$)** | $d(p,q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers; grid-based path problems |
| **Minkowski Distance ($L_p$)** | $d(p,q) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized form; $p=1 \to L_1$, $p=2 \to L_2$ |
| **Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$)** | $d(p,q) = \max_i \vert p_i - q_i \vert$ | When only the worst-case dimension matters |
| **Cosine Distance** | $d(p,q) = 1 - \frac{p \cdot q}{\vert\vert p \vert\vert \, \vert\vert q \vert\vert}$ | High-dimensional sparse vectors (text, image histograms) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Euclidean Distance dominates in machine vision:** Pixel intensity features in image classification often follow Gaussian distributions in RGB space, and Euclidean distance corresponds to the optimal Mahalanobis distance only when features are uncorrelated and have equal variance. For raw pixel data, this is approximately true, making $L_2$ the default choice.

### Choosing the Optimal Value of *k*
- **Small *k* (e.g., 1, 3):** Low bias, but **high variance** — sensitive to noise (overfitting).
- **Large *k* (e.g., 50, 100):** High bias, but **low variance** — smoother decision boundaries (underfitting).
- **Optimal *k*:** Determined empirically using **cross-validation**; rule of thumb: $k \approx \sqrt{N}$.

### Computational Complexity
- **Training phase:** $O(1)$ (lazy learner — just stores the data).
- **Prediction phase:** $O(N \cdot d)$ where $N$ is the number of training samples and $d$ is the feature dimensionality.
- **Optimization:** Use **KD-Trees** or **Ball Trees** to reduce search complexity to $O(\log N)$.

---

## 2.2 Bayesian Classification

### Bayes' Theorem (Foundation)
For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

Translating to classification context (class $c$ and features $x$):

$$
P(c \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(x)}
$$

### Operational Logic Steps of Bayesian Classification
- **Step 1 — Prior Estimation:** Compute $P(c)$ for each class $c$ from the training data as the relative frequency: $P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$.
- **Step 2 — Likelihood Estimation:** For each class, model $P(x \mid c)$ — typically assuming a parametric form (e.g., Gaussian for continuous, Multinomial for discrete).
- **Step 3 — Evidence Computation:** Compute $P(x) = \sum_{c \in C} P(x \mid c) \cdot P(c)$ (the total probability law).
- **Step 4 — Posterior Computation:** Apply Bayes' Theorem to obtain $P(c \mid x)$ for every class.
- **Step 5 — Decision Rule:** Apply the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** hypothesis:

$$
\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c \mid x) = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(x \mid c) \cdot P(c)
$$

Note that $P(x)$ is the same for all classes and can be dropped from the maximization.

### Types of Bayesian Classifiers

| Classifier | Description | Use Case |
|------------|-------------|----------|
| **Naïve Bayes** | Assumes conditional independence of features | Text classification, spam filtering, OCR |
| **Bayes Optimal Classifier** | Combines predictions from all hypotheses weighted by their posterior | Theoretical performance benchmark |
| **Bayesian Belief Network (BBN)** | Encodes conditional independence via a directed acyclic graph | Medical diagnosis, fault detection |
| **Bayesian Neural Network** | Places distributions over weights instead of point estimates | Uncertainty quantification in vision |

### Bayes Error Rate (Irreducible Error)
The minimum possible error rate for any classifier is:

$$
\epsilon^* = 1 - \sum_{x} P(x) \max_{c} P(c \mid x)
$$

This is the **Bayes error** — no classifier can perform better than this bound.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(c \vert x) = \frac{P(x \vert c) \, P(c)}{P(x)}$ | Posterior, likelihood, prior, evidence |
| MAP Decision | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(x \vert c) P(c)$ | Best class label |
| Total Probability | $P(x) = \sum_c P(x \vert c) P(c)$ | Normalizing constant |
| Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum_i (p_i - q_i)^2}$ | Pixel/image similarity |
| k-NN Prediction | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(x_q)\}$ | Majority vote |
| Bayes Error | $\epsilon^* = 1 - E_x[\max_c P(c \vert x)]$ | Irreducible error |
| Naïve Bayes Joint | $P(x \vert c) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(x_j \vert c)$ | Conditional independence assumption |
| Voronoi Cell | $\mathcal{V}(x_i) = \{x : d(x, x_i) \leq d(x, x_j) \forall j\}$ | Region of nearest neighbor dominance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Machine Vision:**
> - **k-NN** with Euclidean distance is the baseline for **image classification** tasks (e.g., MNIST handwritten digit recognition achieves ~97% accuracy with k=3).
> - **Naïve Bayes** powers real-time **spam filtering** in email gateways, **text categorization** in image captioning, and **skin lesion classification** in dermatological vision systems.
> - **Bayesian Belief Networks** are used in **autonomous driving** for sensor fusion — combining camera, LiDAR, and radar uncertainties into a single robust decision.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

We begin with the **definition of conditional probability**. For any two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

By the **product rule of probability**:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \, P(B)
$$

The intersection $A \cap B$ is symmetric, so we can also write:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \, P(A)
$$

Setting these two expressions for $P(A \cap B)$ equal:

$$
P(A \mid B) \, P(B) = P(B \mid A) \, P(A)
$$

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$ (since $P(B) > 0$):

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

This is **Bayes' Theorem**. Replacing $A \to c$ (class) and $B \to x$ (feature):

$$
P(c \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid c) \, P(c)}{P(x)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Each Term:**
> - $P(c)$ — *Prior:* belief about class $c$ before seeing $x$.
> - $P(x \mid c)$ — *Likelihood:* probability of $x$ if the world is class $c$.
> - $P(c \mid x)$ — *Posterior:* updated belief about $c$ after seeing $x$.
> - $P(x)$ — *Evidence:* overall probability of $x$; acts as a normalizing constant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Naïve Bayes Joint Likelihood (Conditional Independence)

For a feature vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$, the joint conditional probability is, by the chain rule of probability:

$$
P(x \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \, P(x_2 \mid c, x_1) \, P(x_3 \mid c, x_1, x_2) \cdots P(x_n \mid c, x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1})
$$

The **Naïve Bayes assumption** states that all features are conditionally independent given the class:

$$
P(x_i \mid c, x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_n) = P(x_i \mid c)
$$

Substituting this assumption into the chain rule:

$$
P(x \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid c) \cdot P(x_3 \mid c) \cdots P(x_n \mid c)
$$

This simplifies to a **product form**:

$$
P(x \mid c) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(x_j \mid c)
$$

The MAP decision rule becomes:

$$
\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(x_j \mid c)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Naïve"?** The assumption of conditional independence is rarely strictly true in real data — pixel intensities in an image are highly correlated. Yet, the classifier often works remarkably well in practice because what matters is the **ranking** of classes, not the exact probability values.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: k-NN Classification (2D Points)

**Problem:** Given training data points and an unknown query point, classify using **k = 3** with **Euclidean distance**.

| Point | Coordinates $(x_1, x_2)$ | Class |
|-------|--------------------------|-------|
| $A$ | $(1, 2)$ | Red |
| $B$ | $(2, 3)$ | Red |
| $C$ | $(2, 1)$ | Blue |
| $D$ | $(3, 2)$ | Blue |
| Query $Q$ | $(2, 2)$ | ? |

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distances from $Q = (2, 2)$ to all training points:**

$$
d(Q, A) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0} = \sqrt{1} = 1.000
$$

$$
d(Q, B) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (2-3)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 1} = \sqrt{1} = 1.000
$$

$$
d(Q, C) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 1} = \sqrt{1} = 1.000
$$

$$
d(Q, D) = \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (2-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0} = \sqrt{1} = 1.000
$$

**Step 2 — Sort distances (all tied at 1.0):** All four points are equidistant from $Q$. To break the tie, we may use additional criteria, e.g., alphabetical order, so consider $A, B, C$ as the 3 nearest.

**Step 3 — Majority vote among $A, B, C$:** $A$ = Red, $B$ = Red, $C$ = Blue. So the vote is **2 Red vs 1 Blue**.

**Step 4 — Decision:** $\hat{y}(Q) = $ **Red**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Naïve Bayes Classification (Binary, 2 Features)

**Problem:** Classify a fruit with features $x_1 = $ *color = Red*, $x_2 = $ *shape = Round*, given the following data:

| Class | Total | Red | Round | Not Red | Not Round |
|-------|-------|-----|-------|---------|-----------|
| Apple ($c_1$) | 5 | 4 | 4 | 1 | 1 |
| Orange ($c_2$) | 5 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 0 |

**Step 1 — Compute priors:**

$$
P(\text{Apple}) = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5
$$

$$
P(\text{Orange}) = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute likelihoods (with Laplace smoothing factor = 1, vocabulary size = 2):**

$$
P(\text{Red} \mid \text{Apple}) = \frac{4 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714
$$

$$
P(\text{Round} \mid \text{Apple}) = \frac{4 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714
$$

$$
P(\text{Red} \mid \text{Orange}) = \frac{1 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{2}{7} \approx 0.286
$$

$$
P(\text{Round} \mid \text{Orange}) = \frac{5 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{6}{7} \approx 0.857
$$

**Step 3 — Compute unnormalized posteriors (dropping $P(x)$):**

$$
P(\text{Apple} \mid x) \propto P(\text{Apple}) \cdot P(\text{Red} \mid \text{Apple}) \cdot P(\text{Round} \mid \text{Apple})
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 0.714 \times 0.714 = 0.5 \times 0.510 = 0.255
$$

$$
P(\text{Orange} \mid x) \propto P(\text{Orange}) \cdot P(\text{Red} \mid \text{Orange}) \cdot P(\text{Round} \mid \text{Orange})
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 0.286 \times 0.857 = 0.5 \times 0.245 = 0.122
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to get true posteriors:**

$$
P(x) = 0.255 + 0.122 = 0.377
$$

$$
P(\text{Apple} \mid x) = \frac{0.255}{0.377} \approx 0.676
$$

$$
P(\text{Orange} \mid x) = \frac{0.122}{0.377} \approx 0.324
$$

**Step 5 — MAP decision:** $\hat{c} = \arg\max(0.676, 0.324) = $ **Apple**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Why MAP Equals Bayes Optimal in the Limit

The **Bayes optimal classifier** is defined as the classifier with minimum possible error rate. We show that the MAP rule achieves this.

For a query $x$, the probability of misclassification under decision $\hat{c}(x)$ is:

$$
P(\text{error} \mid x) = 1 - P(\hat{c}(x) \mid x)
$$

The total error rate is the expectation over all $x$:

$$
P(\text{error}) = \int P(x) \left[1 - P(\hat{c}(x) \mid x)\right] dx = 1 - \int P(x) P(\hat{c}(x) \mid x) \, dx
$$

To **minimize** $P(\text{error})$, we must **maximize** $P(\hat{c}(x) \mid x)$ for every $x$. This is exactly the MAP rule:

$$
\hat{c}(x) = \arg\max_{c} P(c \mid x)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Bayes optimal classifier is achieved by picking the class with the highest posterior probability at every $x$. No other deterministic classifier can beat this — it is a **theoretical lower bound on error**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of k-NN Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Query Point xq] --> B[Load Training Dataset T]
    B --> C{For each training point xi}
    C --> D[Compute Distance d xq, xi]
    D --> E[Store pair di, yi]
    E --> C
    C -->|All points processed| F[Sort distances in ascending order]
    F --> G[Select first k entries]
    G --> H[Extract labels of k nearest neighbors]
    H --> I{Classification Task}
    I -->|Yes| J[Majority Vote Mode of labels]
    I -->|No| K[Average of labels]
    J --> L[Output Predicted Class yhat]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The query point flows through distance computation, sorting, and selection. The k-NN algorithm is a *lazy learner* — it defers all computation until a query arrives, in contrast to *eager learners* like Decision Trees that build a model during training.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Bayesian Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data x, c] --> B[Estimate Prior P c]
    A --> C[Estimate Likelihood P x given c]
    B --> D[Apply Bayes Theorem]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Posterior P c given x]
    E --> F[MAP Decision arg max P c given x]
    F --> G[Predicted Class chat]
    H[New Query x] --> I[Compute P x given c for each c]
    I --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline separates **training** (estimating priors and likelihoods) from **inference** (computing posteriors for new queries). The MAP block is the decision point where the algorithm commits to a class.

## 4.3 Concept Map: Nearest Neighbor Decision Regions (Voronoi)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feature Space R2] --> B[Training Points]
    B --> C[Voronoi Tessellation]
    C --> D[Cell around each point xi]
    D --> E[All points in cell are closest to xi]
    E --> F[Class of xi labels the cell]
    F --> G[Query point q falls in one cell]
    G --> H[1-NN assigns class of that cell's owner]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Voronoi Diagram Insight:** A **1-NN classifier** partitions the feature space into polygonal regions (Voronoi cells) where every point in a cell is closer to the cell's seed than to any other seed. The class assigned to a query is simply the class of the seed that owns the cell containing the query. For **k-NN with k > 1**, the decision boundary is a smoothed version of the 1-NN boundary.

## 4.4 Comparative Diagram: k-NN vs Bayesian Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supervised Learning] --> B[Instance Based]
    A --> C[Model Based]
    B --> D[k Nearest Neighbors]
    C --> E[Bayesian Classification]
    D --> F[Lazy Learner No training phase]
    D --> G[Uses distance metrics]
    D --> H[Non parametric]
    E --> I[Eager Learner Computes model]
    E --> J[Uses probability distributions]
    E --> K[Parametric or Non parametric]
    F --> L[Machine Vision Use: Image retrieval]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> M[Machine Vision Use: Object detection]
    J --> M
    K --> M
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Contrast:** k-NN is a **discriminative, instance-based** method that delays learning until query time, while Bayesian classification is a **generative, model-based** method that builds a probability model during training and uses it for fast inference.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm. List any two distance metrics used in k-NN.**

**Model Answer:**
The k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm is a **non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning method** that classifies an unknown sample $x_q$ by assigning it the class label most frequently represented among its $k$ closest training examples in the feature space, based on a distance metric.

> [!NOTE]
> k-NN is a *lazy learner* — it does not build an explicit model during training. All training samples are stored in memory, and classification is performed at query time.

**Two distance metrics used in k-NN:**

1. **Euclidean Distance ($L_2$):** $d(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(p_i - q_i)^2}$
2. **Manhattan Distance ($L_1$):** $d(p,q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert$

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State Bayes' Theorem. Explain each term with its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
**Bayes' Theorem** relates the posterior probability of a hypothesis to its prior probability and the likelihood of observed evidence:

$$
P(c \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(x)}
$$

**Terms and their significance:**

| Term | Name | Significance |
|------|------|--------------|
| $P(c)$ | Prior | Initial belief about class $c$ before observing $x$ |
| $P(x \mid c)$ | Likelihood | Probability of observing $x$ given class $c$ |
| $P(c \mid x)$ | Posterior | Updated belief about $c$ after observing $x$ |
| $P(x)$ | Evidence | Normalizing constant ensuring probabilities sum to 1 |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Module Internal Choice Format: **Module Choice** — Two full questions from this module, answer any ONE. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(14 Marks)**

**Part (a)** — **7 Marks**
Explain the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm with a suitable diagram. Discuss the effect of choosing different values of *k* on the decision boundary and the bias-variance trade-off.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The k-NN algorithm is a non-parametric, instance-based learning method where the class of a query point $x_q$ is determined by the majority vote of its $k$ nearest training points in the feature space.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Compute the distance from $x_q$ to every training point $x_i$ using a chosen metric (e.g., Euclidean).
2. Sort all distances in ascending order.
3. Select the first $k$ training points — these are the $k$ nearest neighbors.
4. Count the frequency of each class among the $k$ neighbors.
5. Assign $x_q$ to the class with the highest frequency: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum_{x_i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$.

**Effect of *k* on Decision Boundary:**

| Value of *k* | Decision Boundary | Bias | Variance | Issue |
|--------------|-------------------|------|----------|-------|
| $k = 1$ | Highly irregular, sensitive to noise | Low | High | **Overfitting** |
| $k = N$ | Constant, predicts the majority class | High | Low | **Underfitting** |
| Moderate $k$ (e.g., 5–10) | Smooth, captures class structure | Balanced | Balanced | **Optimal** |

**Bias-Variance Trade-off:**
- **Small *k*** $\Rightarrow$ **low bias** (model closely fits training data) but **high variance** (predictions change drastically with small changes in training data).
- **Large *k*** $\Rightarrow$ **high bias** (model is too simplistic, underfits) but **low variance** (stable predictions).
- The optimal $k$ minimizes the total error = $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$, and is typically found through **k-fold cross-validation**.

**Reference to Diagram:** The Voronoi tessellation (Section 4.3) illustrates the $k=1$ case — each cell is a region where the seed point's class is the 1-NN prediction.

**Part (b)** — **7 Marks**
Describe the Nearest Neighbor classifier. Explain Voronoi diagram and its role in the NN classification.

**Model Solution:**

**1-Nearest Neighbor (1-NN) Classifier:**
The 1-NN classifier is the simplest form of k-NN with $k=1$. For a query point $x_q$, it finds the single nearest training point $x_{i^*}$:

$$
i^* = \arg\min_i d(x_q, x_i)
$$

The prediction is $\hat{y} = y_{i^*}$. Despite its simplicity, 1-NN is asymptotically consistent — as $N \to \infty$, the error rate of 1-NN approaches at most twice the Bayes error (Cover & Hart, 1967).

**Voronoi Diagram:**
A Voronoi diagram is a partition of the feature space into polygonal regions called **Voronoi cells**. For each training point $x_i$, the Voronoi cell is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{V}(x_i) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : d(x, x_i) \leq d(x, x_j) \; \forall j \neq i\}
$$

**Properties of Voronoi Cells:**
- Every point in $\mathcal{V}(x_i)$ is closer to $x_i$ than to any other training point.
- The cells tile the feature space without gaps or overlaps.
- The boundary between two cells is the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining the two corresponding training points.

**Role in NN Classification:**
- The 1-NN decision boundary is formed by the **union of all Voronoi cell boundaries** in the feature space.
- A query point $q$ is assigned the class of the training point whose Voronoi cell contains $q$.
- For higher $k$, the effective decision boundary is a **smoothed** version of the 1-NN boundary, obtained by aggregating labels over neighboring cells.
- Voronoi diagrams provide geometric intuition for understanding classifier behavior and for analyzing sensitivity to noise and outliers.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(14 Marks)**

**Part (a)** — **7 Marks**
Explain Bayesian classification. Derive Bayes' Theorem and explain each term. Discuss the Bayes optimal classifier.

**Model Solution:**

**Bayesian Classification — Definition:**
Bayesian classification is a **probabilistic supervised learning approach** that uses Bayes' Theorem to compute the posterior probability $P(c \mid x)$ for each class $c$ given the feature vector $x$, and assigns the class with the maximum posterior probability to the query.

**Derivation of Bayes' Theorem:**
By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(c \mid x) = \frac{P(c \cap x)}{P(x)}
$$

By the product rule:

$$
P(c \cap x) = P(x \mid c) \cdot P(c)
$$

Substituting:

$$
P(c \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(x)}
$$

**Explanation of Each Term:**
- **Prior $P(c)$:** Represents the initial probability of class $c$ in the absence of any feature information. Estimated from training data as $P(c) = N_c / N$.
- **Likelihood $P(x \mid c)$:** The probability of observing feature vector $x$ given that the true class is $c$. Models the class-conditional distribution.
- **Posterior $P(c \mid x)$:** The revised probability of class $c$ after observing $x$. This is the quantity used for classification.
- **Evidence $P(x)$:** The total probability of $x$ across all classes, computed as $P(x) = \sum_{c} P(x \mid c) P(c)$. It is a normalizing constant ensuring $\sum_c P(c \mid x) = 1$.

**Bayes Optimal Classifier:**
The Bayes optimal classifier assigns a query $x$ to the class with the **maximum posterior probability**:

$$
\hat{c}_{BO} = \arg\max_{c} P(c \mid x)
$$

This decision rule is **optimal in the sense of minimizing misclassification probability** — no other deterministic classifier can achieve a lower error rate on the same data distribution. The **Bayes error rate** $\epsilon^* = 1 - E_x[\max_c P(c \mid x)]$ represents the irreducible lower bound on classification error.

> [!NOTE]
> **In practice**, the true distribution $P(x \mid c)$ is unknown and must be estimated from data. This estimation error is why real-world Bayesian classifiers may not achieve the theoretical Bayes optimum.

**Part (b)** — **7 Marks**
Explain the Naïve Bayes classifier with the conditional independence assumption. Illustrate with an example of text/document classification.

**Model Solution:**

**Naïve Bayes Classifier:**
Naïve Bayes is a simplified Bayesian classifier that assumes **conditional independence** of all features given the class label. Despite this "naïve" assumption being often violated in practice, the classifier performs remarkably well — particularly in high-dimensional tasks like text classification.

**Conditional Independence Assumption:**

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid c) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(x_j \mid c)
$$

**MAP Decision Rule:**

$$
\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(x_j \mid c)
$$

**Example: Spam Email Classification**

**Setup:** Classify an email as *Spam* ($c_1$) or *Ham* ($c_2$) based on the presence of words like "free", "offer", and "meeting".

**Training Data (Hypothetical):**
- Total emails: 100
- Spam emails: 40 (so $P(\text{Spam}) = 0.4$, $P(\text{Ham}) = 0.6$)
- "free" appears in 30 spam and 5 ham emails
- "offer" appears in 25 spam and 8 ham emails
- "meeting" appears in 2 spam and 30 ham emails

**Query Email:** Contains words = {free, offer, meeting}

**Step 1 — Compute Likelihoods:**

$$
P(\text{free} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75, \quad P(\text{free} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{5}{60} \approx 0.083
$$

$$
P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625, \quad P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{8}{60} \approx 0.133
$$

$$
P(\text{meeting} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{2}{40} = 0.05, \quad P(\text{meeting} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{30}{60} = 0.50
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Unnormalized Posteriors:**

$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid x) \propto 0.4 \times 0.75 \times 0.625 \times 0.05 = 0.4 \times 0.0234 = 0.00938
$$

$$
P(\text{Ham} \mid x) \propto 0.6 \times 0.083 \times 0.133 \times 0.50 = 0.6 \times 0.00552 = 0.00331
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize:**

$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid x) = \frac{0.00938}{0.00938 + 0.00331} = \frac{0.00938}{0.01269} \approx 0.739
$$

$$
P(\text{Ham} \mid x) = \frac{0.00331}{0.01269} \approx 0.261
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Since $P(\text{Spam} \mid x) = 0.739 > P(\text{Ham} \mid x) = 0.261$, the email is classified as **Spam**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Laplace Smoothing:** In practice, if a word never appears in any spam email, its likelihood becomes 0, wiping out the entire product. To avoid this, **Laplace (add-1) smoothing** is applied: $P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{N_c + V}$, where $V$ is the vocabulary size.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop $P(x)$ in MAP:** Many students keep $P(x)$ in the maximization step. Note that $P(x)$ is a **constant** with respect to $c$ and does not affect the argmax — drop it to simplify computation. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting normalization in Naïve Bayes:** When asked for "posterior probability", you must compute $P(x)$ and normalize. If the question asks only for "the predicted class", the unnormalized product is sufficient. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Not mentioning Laplace smoothing:** In Naïve Bayes, if any likelihood is 0, the whole product becomes 0. Always mention the add-1 smoothing technique. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Choosing wrong distance metric for k-NN:** Euclidean is the default but Manhattan is preferred for high-dimensional sparse data. Always justify your metric choice with respect to the feature characteristics. **(-2 marks)**
> 5. **Confusing prior with posterior:** The *prior* is computed *before* observing evidence; the *posterior* is *after*. This distinction is worth **2 marks** in a typical KTU Part A question.
> 6. **Forgetting cross-validation for *k*:** Never state the optimal $k$ value without mentioning that it is found empirically through **k-fold cross-validation**. **(-1 mark)**
> 7. **Not stating units or dimensions:** Distance is in the same units as the feature space. Always specify the units when writing distance formulas. **(-0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)
- [ ] k-NN is a **lazy, non-parametric, instance-based** supervised learner.
- [ ] Training is $O(1)$; prediction is $O(Nd)$ — use **KD-Trees** for acceleration.
- [ ] Choose **odd *k*** for binary classification to avoid tie votes.
- [ ] **Small *k*** → low bias, high variance (overfitting).
- [ ] **Large *k*** → high bias, low variance (underfitting).
- [ ] Optimal $k$ found via **cross-validation**; rule of thumb $k \approx \sqrt{N}$.
- [ ] **Euclidean distance** is default; **Manhattan** for sparse, high-dim data; **Cosine** for text/histograms.
- [ ] **Feature normalization** (min-max or z-score) is critical when features have different units/scales.
- [ ] 1-NN is **asymptotically consistent** — error approaches at most $2 \times \epsilon^*$.
- [ ] **Voronoi diagram** defines 1-NN decision boundaries geometrically.

### Bayesian Classification
- [ ] Bayes' Theorem: $P(c \mid x) = P(x \mid c) P(c) / P(x)$.
- [ ] **Posterior** is what we want; **prior** is what we know; **likelihood** is what we estimate; **evidence** is the normalizer.
- [ ] **MAP rule** = $\arg\max_c P(x \mid c) P(c)$ — drop $P(x)$ since it's a constant.
- [ ] **Bayes optimal classifier** = $\arg\max_c P(c \mid x)$ — minimum possible error.
- [ ] **Bayes error rate** $\epsilon^* = 1 - E_x[\max_c P(c \mid x)]$ — irreducible lower bound.
- [ ] **Naïve Bayes assumption:** all features are conditionally independent given the class.
- [ ] Naïve Bayes MAP: $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_j P(x_j \mid c)$.
- [ ] **Laplace (add-1) smoothing** prevents zero-probability issues.
- [ ] **Log-transform** the product to avoid numerical underflow in high dimensions.
- [ ] Naïve Bayes is the **workhorse of text classification**, spam filtering, and OCR.

### Critical Comparisons
- [ ] k-NN is **discriminative + lazy**; Bayesian is **generative + eager** (model-based).
- [ ] k-NN needs **distance metric**; Bayesian needs **probability distribution assumption**.
- [ ] k-NN **stores all training data**; Bayesian **stores only distribution parameters**.
- [ ] Both are **supervised** learning methods.
- [ ] k-NN handles **multi-class** naturally; Bayesian handles it via softmax-like normalization.

### Engineering Best Practices
- [ ] **Normalize features** before applying k-NN to prevent large-scale features from dominating.
- [ ] Use **dimensionality reduction (PCA)** before k-NN when $d$ is very large (curse of dimensionality).
- [ ] For **Naïve Bayes**, choose Gaussian likelihood for continuous features, Bernoulli for binary, Multinomial for counts.
- [ ] Always **report multiple metrics** (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) — accuracy alone is misleading for imbalanced data.
- [ ] **Cross-validate** the value of $k$ or the choice of distribution assumption in Bayesian models.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning - Clustering, K-Means and Gaussian Mixture Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning - Clustering, K-Means and Gaussian Mixture Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is a branch of Machine Learning in which the model is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}\}$ that contains **only input features** without any associated target labels. The goal is to discover hidden structure, intrinsic groupings, or low-dimensional representations of the data.

$$f_{\theta} : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{S}, \quad \text{where } \mathcal{S} \text{ encodes structure (clusters, manifolds, densities).}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Unsupervised learning refers to algorithms that learn patterns from unlabeled data. The two most important families relevant to Machine Vision are **Clustering** (partitioning data into groups) and **Density Estimation** (modeling the underlying probability distribution).

## 1.2 Clustering — The Heart of Unsupervised Vision

**Clustering** is the task of partitioning a set of $N$ data points into $K$ groups (clusters) $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K$ such that:
- Points within the same cluster are **highly similar** (intra-cluster compactness).
- Points in different clusters are **highly dissimilar** (inter-cluster separation).

$$C_1 \cup C_2 \cup \ldots \cup C_K = \mathcal{D}, \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\; (i \neq j)$$

## 1.3 K-Means Clustering — The Centroid-Based Workhorse

**K-Means** is a partitional, prototype-based clustering algorithm that aims to partition $N$ observations into $K$ clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the **distortion function** or **inertia**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Librarian's Book Sorting"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a librarian with 1,000 unsorted books and 5 empty shelves ($K = 5$). The librarian places a representative "label book" (centroid) on each shelf. Each book is assigned to the shelf whose label book it most resembles (minimum distance). Then the librarian moves the label book to the average position of all books on that shelf. This process repeats until the label books stop moving. **This is exactly K-Means!**

## 1.4 Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) — The Probabilistic Powerhouse

A **Gaussian Mixture Model** assumes that the data is generated from a mixture of a finite number of Gaussian distributions with unknown parameters. Each cluster is modeled as a multivariate Gaussian with its own mean $\mu_k$ and covariance $\Sigma_k$.

$$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

where $\pi_k$ are the **mixing coefficients** (prior probabilities) with $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$ and $\pi_k \geq 0$.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Population Demographics"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a city's population made up of 3 ethnic groups, each with a different average height and different height variance (e.g., one group has mean 165 cm with small variance, another has mean 178 cm with large variance). If you measure only the heights of 10,000 random people without knowing which group they belong to, a **GMM** lets you figure out: (1) the proportion of each group ($\pi_k$), (2) the average height of each group ($\mu_k$), and (3) the spread ($\Sigma_k$). **This is the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm at work.**

## 1.5 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **WCSS / Inertia** | Sum of squared distances from points to their assigned centroid |
| **Silhouette Score** | Measures how similar a point is to its own cluster vs. other clusters (range $[-1, 1]$) |
| **Log-Likelihood** | The probability the model assigns to observed data (higher = better for GMM) |
| **BIC / AIC** | Model selection criteria balancing fit and complexity |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means: Operational Breakdown

K-Means is an **iterative refinement** algorithm with two alternating steps until convergence.

### Step 1 — Initialization
Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$. Common methods:
- **Forgy:** Randomly pick $K$ data points as initial centroids.
- **Random Partition:** Assign each point randomly to one of $K$ clusters, then take means.
- **K-Means++:** Probabilistic seeding that spreads initial centroids apart (preferred).

### Step 2 — Assignment Step (Expectation-like)
For each data point $x^{(n)}$, assign it to the nearest centroid (using squared Euclidean distance):

$$c^{(n)} = \arg\min_{k} \Vert x^{(n)} - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

### Step 3 — Update Step (Maximization-like)
Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:

$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x^{(n)} \in C_k} x^{(n)}$$

### Step 4 — Convergence Check
Stop when the centroids no longer move significantly or the objective function stabilizes:

$$\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$$

### Why Does K-Means Work? (Lloyd's Algorithm Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each iteration is guaranteed to **monotonically decrease** the WCSS objective. The assignment step minimizes $J$ with fixed centroids, and the update step minimizes $J$ with fixed assignments. Therefore $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$, ensuring convergence. However, convergence is to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global one.

---

## 2.2 Gaussian Mixture Models: Probabilistic Framework

GMM is a **latent variable model**. For each data point $x^{(n)}$, there is a hidden/latent variable $z^{(n)} \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ indicating which Gaussian component generated it.

### The Generative Story
1. Sample component index $z^{(n)} \sim \text{Categorical}(\pi_1, \ldots, \pi_K)$.
2. Sample data point $x^{(n)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{z^{(n)}}, \Sigma_{z^{(n)}})$.

### Why Use EM (Expectation-Maximization)?
The presence of latent variables $z^{(n)}$ makes direct maximum likelihood intractable because the log-likelihood contains a **log of a sum**:

$$\log p(X \mid \pi, \mu, \Sigma) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right)$$

The log cannot be pushed inside the sum, so we use the **EM algorithm** which iteratively constructs a lower bound (ELBO) and maximizes it.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| K-Means Objective (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Function to minimize in K-Means |
| K-Means Assignment | $c^{(n)} = \arg\min_{k} \Vert x^{(n)} - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Assign point to nearest centroid |
| K-Means Update | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x \in C_k} x$ | New centroid = cluster mean |
| GMM Density | $p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ | Mixture of $K$ Gaussians |
| Multivariate Gaussian | $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \Sigma) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{D/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)\right)$ | PDF of $D$-dim Gaussian |
| E-Step Responsibility | $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | Posterior probability that $z^{(n)} = k$ |
| M-Step: Mixing Coeff. | $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}$, where $N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$ | New weight of component $k$ |
| M-Step: Mean | $\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x^{(n)}$ | Weighted mean of component $k$ |
| M-Step: Covariance | $\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) (x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$ | Weighted covariance |
| Log-Likelihood (GMM) | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ | Maximized by EM |
| ELBO (Evidence Lower Bound) | $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = \sum_n \sum_k \gamma(z_{nk}) \log \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\gamma(z_{nk})}$ | Lower bound on $\log p(X \mid \theta)$ |
| Silhouette Coefficient | $s(n) = \frac{b(n) - a(n)}{\max\{a(n), b(n)\}}$ | Cluster quality metric (range $[-1, 1]$) |
| Elbow Method (Distortion) | $J(K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Choose $K$ at the "elbow" of $J$ vs $K$ curve |
| BIC | $\text{BIC} = -2 \log \mathcal{L} + p \log N$ | $p$ = number of parameters; lower is better |
| AIC | $\text{AIC} = -2 \log \mathcal{L} + 2p$ | Model selection criterion |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Machine Vision (KTU Context):**
> - **Image Segmentation:** K-Means clusters pixel colors in RGB/LAB space to separate objects from backgrounds.
> - **Document/Image Retrieval:** GMMs model visual word distributions (Bag-of-Visual-Words model in CBIR systems).
> - **Anomaly Detection:** GMM assigns low probability to defective products in quality-control pipelines.
> - **Medical Imaging:** Clustering MRI voxels to identify tissue types (gray matter, white matter, CSF).
> - **Background Subtraction:** GMM-based methods (e.g., the famous Zivkovic algorithm) separate foreground from background in surveillance video.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: K-Means Update Rule from Gradient of WCSS

We want to minimize $J(\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$.

**Step 1:** Expand the squared norm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} (x - \mu_k)^T (x - \mu_k) \\
  &= \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \left( x^T x - 2 \mu_k^T x + \mu_k^T \mu_k \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $\mu_k$. The terms $x^T x$ and $\mu_j$ for $j \neq k$ are independent of $\mu_k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_k} \sum_{x \in C_k} \left( x^T x - 2 \mu_k^T x + \mu_k^T \mu_k \right) \\
&= \sum_{x \in C_k} \left( 0 - 2x + 2\mu_k \right) \\
&= -2 \sum_{x \in C_k} x + 2 \vert C_k \vert \, \mu_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Set the derivative to zero to find the optimum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
-2 \sum_{x \in C_k} x + 2 \vert C_k \vert \, \mu_k &= 0 \\
2 \vert C_k \vert \, \mu_k &= 2 \sum_{x \in C_k} x \\
\mu_k &= \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x \in C_k} x
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The optimal centroid of a cluster is exactly the **arithmetic mean** of all points in that cluster. This is why the algorithm is called "K-Means".

---

## 3.2 Derivation: E-Step Posterior (Responsibility) for GMM

**Step 1:** By Bayes' theorem, the posterior probability of the latent variable $z^{(n)} = k$ given $x^{(n)}$ is:

$$
\gamma(z_{nk}) = p(z^{(n)} = k \mid x^{(n)}) = \frac{p(z^{(n)} = k) \, p(x^{(n)} \mid z^{(n)} = k)}{p(x^{(n)})}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the prior and likelihood. The prior is the mixing coefficient $\pi_k$. The likelihood is the Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$. The marginal $p(x^{(n)})$ is obtained by summing over all $K$ components.

$$
\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $\gamma(z_{nk})$ is the "soft assignment" of point $x^{(n)}$ to component $k$. In K-Means, assignments are **hard** (0 or 1); in GMM, they are **soft** (real numbers in $[0, 1]$ summing to 1).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: M-Step Updates for GMM

We maximize the **expected complete-data log-likelihood** under the posterior from the E-step:

$$
Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \log \left[ \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]
$$

### 3.3.1 Update for $\mu_k$

Take derivative with respect to $\mu_k$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial Q}{\partial \mu_k} &= \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \Sigma_k^{-1} (x^{(n)} - \mu_k) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \mu_k^{\text{new}} &= \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x^{(n)}}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x^{(n)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Update for $\Sigma_k$

Using the standard result for the MLE of a Gaussian covariance with weighted samples:

$$
\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T
$$

### 3.3.3 Update for $\pi_k$ (with Lagrange Multiplier)

We need to maximize $Q$ subject to $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$. Use the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\pi} = Q + \lambda \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k - 1 \right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\pi}}{\partial \pi_k} &= \sum_{n=1}^{N} \frac{\gamma(z_{nk})}{\pi_k} + \lambda = 0 \\
\pi_k^{\text{new}} &= \frac{N_k}{N} \quad \text{where } N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Number of Points:** $N_k$ is the "effective number of points" assigned to component $k$. It is the sum of the soft responsibilities. This reduces to the K-Means update when $\gamma(z_{nk}) \in \{0, 1\}$ (hard assignment).

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — K-Means (1D)

Consider 1D data: $x = \{1, 2, 8, 9, 10\}$, $K = 2$. Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = 2$, $\mu_2 = 8$.

**Iteration 1:**
- Assign: $|1-2|=1$ vs $|1-8|=7 \Rightarrow C_1$. $|2-2|=0$ vs $|2-8|=6 \Rightarrow C_1$. $|8-2|=6$ vs $|8-8|=0 \Rightarrow C_2$. $|9-2|=7$ vs $|9-8|=1 \Rightarrow C_2$. $|10-2|=8$ vs $|10-8|=2 \Rightarrow C_2$.
- $C_1 = \{1, 2\}$, $C_2 = \{8, 9, 10\}$.
- Update: $\mu_1 = 1.5$, $\mu_2 = 9$.

**Iteration 2:**
- Assign: $|1-1.5|=0.5$ vs $|1-9|=8 \Rightarrow C_1$. $|2-1.5|=0.5$ vs $|2-9|=7 \Rightarrow C_1$. $|8-1.5|=6.5$ vs $|8-9|=1 \Rightarrow C_2$. $|9-1.5|=7.5$ vs $|9-9|=0 \Rightarrow C_2$. $|10-1.5|=8.5$ vs $|10-9|=1 \Rightarrow C_2$.
- Same assignment, $\mu_1 = 1.5$, $\mu_2 = 9$. **Converged!**

**Final WCSS:**

$$
J = (1 - 1.5)^2 + (2 - 1.5)^2 + (8-9)^2 + (9-9)^2 + (10-9)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 2.5
$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — GMM (1D, K=2)

Consider 1D data: $x = \{-3, -2, -1, 0, 5, 6, 7\}$ (clearly two clusters). Initial parameters: $\pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0.5$, $\mu_1 = -2$, $\sigma_1^2 = 1$, $\mu_2 = 6$, $\sigma_2^2 = 1$.

**E-Step** for $x = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{N}(0 \mid -2, 1) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-2) \approx 0.0540 \\
\mathcal{N}(0 \mid 6, 1) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-18) \approx 3.36 \times 10^{-8} \\
\gamma(z_2) &= \frac{0.5 \times 3.36 \times 10^{-8}}{0.5 \times 0.0540 + 0.5 \times 3.36 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 6 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> So $x = 0$ is almost certainly assigned to component 1, with $\gamma(z_1) \approx 1$. Repeating this for all points and then performing the M-step updates will refine the parameters until the two Gaussians perfectly overlap with the two visible clusters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Algorithm Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Data X, Choose K] --> B[Initialize Centroids via K-Means++]
    B --> C[Assignment Step: Compute Distances]
    C --> D{For each point x_n, assign to nearest centroid}
    D --> E[Update Step: Recompute Centroids as Cluster Means]
    E --> F{Convergence Check: Centroids Unchanged or J Stable}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[Output: Final Clusters C1, C2, ..., CK]
    G --> H[End]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style D fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#FBC02D
    style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style F fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B
    style G fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00796B
    style H fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
```

## 4.2 Gaussian Mixture Model EM Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize pi, mu, Sigma, K] --> B[E-Step: Compute Responsibilities gamma]
    B --> C["For each x_n, k: gamma_nk = pi_k N(xn) / sum_j pi_j N(xn)"]
    C --> D[M-Step: Update Parameters]
    D --> E["pi_k_new = N_k / N"]
    D --> F["mu_k_new = weighted mean"]
    D --> G["Sigma_k_new = weighted covariance"]
    E --> H[Compute New Log-Likelihood L]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Absolute or Relative Change of L less than epsilon}
    I -->|No| B
    I -->|Yes| J[Output: Final pi, mu, Sigma and Responsibilities]
    J --> K[End]

    style A fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD
    style B fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00
    style C fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F
    style D fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457
    style E fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593
    style F fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593
    style G fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593

    style I fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style J fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C
    style K fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#4527A0
```

## 4.3 K-Means vs GMM — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KM[KMeans Hard Clustering]
        KM1[Data Points] --> KM2[Compute Euclidean Distance to Each Centroid]
        KM2 --> KM3[Hard Assignment: Pick Nearest Centroid]
        KM3 --> KM4[Recompute Centroid as Mean]
        KM4 --> KM2
    end

    subgraph GMM[GMM Soft Clustering]
        G1[Data Points] --> G2[Compute Gaussian PDF per Component]
        G2 --> G3[Soft Assignment: Responsibility via Bayes Rule]
        G3 --> G4[Update pi, mu, Sigma via Weighted MLE]
        G4 --> G2
    end

    style KM1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style KM2 fill:#BBDEFB
    style KM3 fill:#BBDEFB
    style KM4 fill:#BBDEFB
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style G2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style G3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style G4 fill:#C8E6C9
```

## 4.4 K-Means Convergence Landscape (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: N data points, K initial centroids] --> B[Iteration t Begins]
    B --> C[Assignment Phase: Fix mu, find C_k]
    C --> D[Update Phase: Fix C_k, find new mu]
    D --> E[Compute Objective J at Step t]
    E --> F{J decreased compared to J at Step t minus 1}
    F -->|Yes| G[Accept new mu and C_k, t equals t plus 1]
    F -->|No| H[Stop: Local Minimum Reached]
    G --> I{Maximum Iterations or J Converged}
    I -->|No| B
    I -->|Yes| H
    H --> J[Output: Final Clusters and Centroids]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD
    style B fill:#FFF9C4
    style C fill:#C5E1A5
    style D fill:#FFCCBC
    style E fill:#B39DDB
    style F fill:#F48FB1
    style G fill:#80CBC4
    style H fill:#FFAB91

    style J fill:#A5D6A7
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define unsupervised learning. How does it differ from supervised learning? Give one example application in machine vision.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Unsupervised learning is a machine learning paradigm where the model learns patterns from **unlabeled data** $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}\}$ without any ground-truth target outputs. The objective is to discover intrinsic structure, clusters, or density estimates within the data.

**Difference from Supervised Learning:** In supervised learning, the dataset is $\{ (x^{(n)}, y^{(n)}) \}$ containing both inputs and labels, and the goal is to learn a mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$. In unsupervised learning, only $x^{(n)}$ is available, and the model must infer structure without guidance.

**Machine Vision Application:** Image segmentation using K-Means clustering on pixel colors in RGB space to separate foreground objects from the background.

---

### Question 2: What is a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)? Write its probability density function and explain the role of mixing coefficients.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A GMM is a probabilistic model that represents a dataset as being generated from a weighted combination of $K$ multivariate Gaussian distributions. Its density is:

$$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

**Role of Mixing Coefficients $\pi_k$:** They represent the **prior probability** that a randomly drawn data point belongs to the $k$-th Gaussian component. They satisfy two constraints:
- **Non-negativity:** $\pi_k \geq 0$ for all $k$.
- **Normalization:** $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$.

Thus, $\pi_k$ acts as the relative weight or importance of the $k$-th cluster in the overall data distribution.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): K-Means Algorithm

#### Part (a) [7 marks]: Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm with a clear step-by-step procedure. State its objective function and show that the centroid update rule is the mean of the cluster points.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Choose the number of clusters $K$.
2. Initialize $K$ centroids (e.g., using K-Means++).
3. **Assignment Step:** For each data point $x^{(n)}$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid:
$$c^{(n)} = \arg\min_{k} \Vert x^{(n)} - \mu_k \Vert^2$$
4. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster:
$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x^{(n)} \in C_k} x^{(n)}$$
5. Repeat steps 3–4 until convergence (centroids stabilize or maximum iterations reached).

**Objective Function (WCSS):**

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

**Derivation of Update Rule:** Differentiating $J$ with respect to $\mu_k$ (treating cluster assignments as fixed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} &= \sum_{x \in C_k} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_k} (x - \mu_k)^T(x - \mu_k) \\
&= \sum_{x \in C_k} -2(x - \mu_k) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \mu_k &= \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x \in C_k} x
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the optimal centroid is the **arithmetic mean**.

#### Part (b) [7 marks]: Discuss the limitations of K-Means and explain how the Elbow Method is used to choose K.

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of K-Means:**
1. **Fixed number of clusters:** Requires $K$ to be specified in advance.
2. **Sensitivity to initialization:** Different initializations lead to different local minima.
3. **Assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters:** Uses Euclidean distance, so it struggles with elongated or irregularly shaped clusters.
4. **Sensitive to outliers:** A single outlier can pull a centroid far from the true cluster center.
5. **Hard assignment:** Cannot represent uncertainty — each point belongs to exactly one cluster.
6. **Linear boundary:** Cluster boundaries are always linear (perpendicular bisectors), limiting expressiveness.

**Elbow Method for Choosing $K$:**
1. Run K-Means for a range of $K$ values, e.g., $K = 1, 2, \ldots, 10$.
2. Compute the WCSS $J(K)$ for each $K$.
3. Plot $J(K)$ vs $K$.
4. Identify the **"elbow"** — the point where adding more clusters yields only marginal reduction in $J$. This is the optimal $K$.

The intuition: the elbow marks the point of **diminishing returns**, where additional clusters are not capturing genuinely new structure.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Gaussian Mixture Models

#### Part (a) [7 marks]: Derive the E-step and M-step update equations for the EM algorithm applied to a Gaussian Mixture Model. Clearly state the responsibilities.

**Model Solution:**

**Setting up the EM algorithm for GMM:**

The observed data log-likelihood is:

$$\log p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

This is intractable due to the log-sum. EM introduces latent variables $z^{(n)} \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$.

**E-Step:** Compute the posterior probability (responsibility) that component $k$ generated point $x^{(n)}$ using current parameters $\theta^{\text{old}}$:

$$
\gamma(z_{nk}) = p(z^{(n)} = k \mid x^{(n)}, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \frac{\pi_k^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k^{\text{old}}, \Sigma_k^{\text{old}})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_j^{\text{old}}, \Sigma_j^{\text{old}})}
$$

**M-Step:** Maximize the expected complete-data log-likelihood:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \log \left[ \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x^{(n)} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

The resulting updates are:

$$
\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}, \quad \mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) x^{(n)}, \quad \Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) (x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T
$$

where $N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$ is the effective number of points in component $k$.

#### Part (b) [7 marks]: Compare K-Means and GMM in terms of cluster shape, assignment type, computational cost, and ability to handle overlapping clusters.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | K-Means | Gaussian Mixture Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Shape** | Spherical, equal variance (Voronoi cells) | Ellipsoidal, any shape (full covariance) |
| **Assignment Type** | Hard (each point → one cluster) | Soft (responsibility $\gamma(z_{nk}) \in [0,1]$) |
| **Cluster Size** | Assumes roughly equal sizes | Naturally accommodates different sizes via $\pi_k$ |
| **Overlap Handling** | Poor — assigns boundary points arbitrarily | Excellent — gives probabilistic membership |
| **Objective** | Minimize WCSS $J$ | Maximize log-likelihood $\mathcal{L}$ |
| **Algorithm** | Lloyd's (direct coordinate descent) | EM (iterative, monotonically non-decreasing) |
| **Initialization Sensitivity** | Very high | High, but more forgiving due to soft assignments |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | High (centroid pulled by outliers) | Lower (low responsibility for outliers) |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(NKdT)$ — very fast | $O(NK D^2 T)$ — heavier due to matrix inversions |
| **Output** | Cluster labels | Probabilities, density, parameters |
| **Choosing $K$** | Elbow, Silhouette | BIC, AIC, cross-validated likelihood |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $\sum \pi_k = 1$:** Always state the normalization condition on mixing coefficients. Many students lose 1 mark for omitting this.
> 2. **Confusing hard and soft assignment:** A common error is describing K-Means as probabilistic. Remember: K-Means uses $\arg\min$ (hard), GMM uses responsibilities (soft).
> 3. **Skipping the ELBO justification for EM:** Examiners specifically look for awareness that EM is needed because the log-likelihood contains a log-sum that is intractable. Mentioning "latent variables make direct MLE hard" earns full marks.
> 4. **Not specifying the distance metric:** Always state "squared Euclidean distance" for K-Means and "Mahalanobis distance" for GMM.
> 5. **Computational cost:** Students often write $O(NK)$ without the iteration count $T$. Include the number of iterations explicitly.
> 6. **Convergence:** K-Means converges in finite steps (centroids can only take finitely many values), but to a **local** minimum. State this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Unsupervised Learning** uses only inputs $x$, no labels $y$. Goal = discover structure.
- **Clustering** partitions data into $K$ groups based on similarity.
- **K-Means** is a **hard, centroid-based, iterative** algorithm that minimizes WCSS:
  $$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$$
- **K-Means steps:** (1) Initialize centroids, (2) Assign points to nearest centroid, (3) Update centroids as means, (4) Repeat until convergence.
- **Optimal centroid = mean** of assigned points (proven by setting $\partial J / \partial \mu_k = 0$).
- **K-Means converges to a local minimum**, not global — use K-Means++ initialization.
- **Limitations of K-Means:** Spherical clusters only, sensitive to outliers, hard assignment, needs $K$ upfront.
- **GMM** models data as a **mixture of $K$ Gaussians**:
  $$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$
- **Mixing coefficients** $\pi_k \geq 0$, $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$.
- **GMM uses EM** because latent variables make direct MLE intractable.
- **E-step** computes **responsibilities** (soft assignments) via Bayes' theorem.
- **M-step** updates $\pi_k, \mu_k, \Sigma_k$ as **weighted** MLE estimates using responsibilities.
- **GMM advantages:** Soft assignment, flexible cluster shape (via $\Sigma_k$), handles overlap, provides density.
- **Choosing $K$:** Elbow method (K-Means) or BIC/AIC (GMM).
- **In machine vision:** K-Means = color-based segmentation; GMM = background subtraction, visual word models.
- **Always remember:** K-Means is a special case of GMM when $\Sigma_k = \sigma^2 I$ (spherical, equal variance) and assignments become hard.
- **Key examiners' phrases to use:** "monotonically non-decreasing likelihood," "local optimum," "responsibility-weighted update," "evidence lower bound (ELBO)."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deep Neural Networks (DNN) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** A **Deep Neural Network (DNN)** is a computational model composed of multiple processing layers, each containing interconnected **artificial neurons (perceptron units)**. It learns hierarchical representations of data by adjusting the strength of connections (**weights**) through an optimization process (**backpropagation**) that minimizes a **loss function**.

The term "deep" refers to the presence of **multiple hidden layers** between the input and output layers, enabling the network to model highly non-linear and complex relationships — a foundational requirement for modern **machine vision** tasks such as object detection, image classification, and semantic segmentation.

---

## 1.2 The Perceptron Unit — The Atom of a Neural Network

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Perceptron** is the fundamental computational unit of a neural network. Inspired by the biological neuron, it takes a set of inputs, applies weights and a bias, sums them, and passes the result through a non-linear **activation function** to produce an output.

Mathematically, a single perceptron computes:

$$y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$

where:
- $x_i$ = input features
- $w_i$ = learnable weights
- $b$ = bias term
- $\phi(\cdot)$ = activation function
- $y$ = output of the perceptron

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Voting Committee

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a perceptron as a **judge in a talent show** deciding whether a contestant advances to the next round. Each judge (input) has a personal **opinion strength (weight)** about specific skills (features) — singing, dancing, stage presence. The judge combines all weighted opinions, adds a **personal bias (preference)**, and then applies a **decision rule (activation function)** — "Yes, advance" or "No, eliminate." A deep neural network is like a **panel of many such judges** organized in tiers (layers), where each tier refines the decision before the final verdict.

---

## 1.4 Weights, Biases, and Layers — The Structural Skeleton

| Component | Role in the Network | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Weights ($w$)** | Scale the importance of each input | "How much should I trust this signal?" |
| **Bias ($b$)** | Shifts the activation function left/right | "My default opinion before hearing anyone" |
| **Input Layer** | Receives raw feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ | The "eyes" of the network |
| **Hidden Layers** | Extract intermediate abstract features | The "thinking" stage |
| **Output Layer** | Produces final prediction $\hat{y}$ | The "decision/verdict" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In Machine Vision, the input layer typically receives a **flattened image vector** (e.g., for a $28 \times 28$ grayscale image, $n = 784$ input neurons). Deep networks used for vision often replace fully-connected input layers with **Convolutional Layers** to exploit spatial structure — covered in later modules.

---

## 1.5 Activation Functions — The Non-Linear Heart

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **activation function** introduces non-linearity into the network, enabling it to learn complex decision boundaries. Without non-linearity, stacking multiple layers would collapse mathematically into a single linear transformation.

Common activation functions used in deep learning for vision:

| Function | Formula | Output Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Binary classification output |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers (older networks) |
| **ReLU** | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **Default hidden layer choice** |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z), \; \alpha \approx 0.01$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Fixes "dying ReLU" |
| **Softmax** | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to 1 | Multi-class output |

---

## 1.6 Loss Functions — The Quantified Error

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **loss function** $L(\hat{y}, y)$ measures the discrepancy between the network's prediction $\hat{y}$ and the ground-truth label $y$. The training objective is to **minimize** this quantity.

| Loss Function | Formula | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression |
| **Binary Cross-Entropy** | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Binary classification |
| **Categorical Cross-Entropy** | $L = -\sum_i y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Multi-class classification |
| **Hinge Loss** | $L = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot \hat{y})$ | SVM-style classification |

---

## 1.7 Backpropagation — The Learning Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Backpropagation** (short for *backward propagation of errors*) is the algorithm used to **compute gradients** of the loss function with respect to every weight in the network. It applies the **chain rule of calculus** recursively from the output layer back to the input layer, enabling **gradient descent** to update the weights.

It is the cornerstone of training *any* deep neural network, including CNNs used in machine vision.

---

## 1.8 Why This Topic Matters for Machine Vision

- **Image classification** (e.g., digit recognition, defect detection) requires networks to learn non-linear mappings from pixel space to label space.
- **Feature hierarchies** (edges → textures → parts → objects) emerge naturally from deep stacked layers.
- **Loss functions** drive the network toward predictions that match ground-truth annotations (e.g., bounding boxes, class labels).
- **Backpropagation** is the universal engine that makes learning tractable in networks with millions of parameters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Perceptron — Operational Logic

A perceptron operates in two precise stages:

**Stage 1 — Pre-activation (Linear Combination):**
$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

**Stage 2 — Activation (Non-linear Transformation):**
$$\hat{y} = \phi(z)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this two-stage design?** The linear stage computes a weighted vote. The non-linear stage converts this vote into a bounded, differentiable output that can be interpreted as a probability, a firing rate, or simply a feature signal. This separation is what allows stacking many perceptrons to model arbitrarily complex functions (Universal Approximation Theorem).

---

## 2.2 Network Layers — The Layered Architecture

A standard **Fully-Connected (Dense) Feed-Forward Network** is structured as:

1. **Input Layer** — Dimension equals number of input features. No computation; just data ingestion.
2. **Hidden Layer(s)** — Apply linear transformation + activation. Depth $L$ means $L-1$ hidden layers.
3. **Output Layer** — Produces $\hat{y}$. Activation depends on task (softmax for multi-class, sigmoid for binary, identity for regression).

For a network with $L$ layers, the **forward pass** is:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$$
$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)} \quad \text{for } l = 1, 2, \dots, L$$
$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \phi^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Vectorized Notation:** $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$ is the weight matrix of layer $l$, and $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ is the bias vector. This vectorized form is what makes GPU-based training feasible.

---

## 2.3 Activation Functions — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Sigmoid

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$$

- **Range:** $(0, 1)$
- **Drawback:** Suffers from the **vanishing gradient problem** when $z$ is very large or very small — gradients approach 0, halting learning.

### 2.3.2 Hyperbolic Tangent (Tanh)

$$\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$$

$$\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$$

- **Range:** $(-1, 1)$ — zero-centered, which helps convergence.
- Still suffers from vanishing gradients for extreme inputs.

### 2.3.3 ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)

$$\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$$

$$\text{ReLU}'(z) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } z < 0 \\ 1 & \text{if } z > 0 \end{cases}$$

- **Advantages:** Computationally cheap, mitigates vanishing gradient for positive inputs, induces **sparsity**.
- **Disadvantage:** "**Dying ReLU**" — neurons can get stuck outputting 0 if they fall into the negative regime.

### 2.3.4 Softmax

$$\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

- **Range:** Each output in $(0, 1)$, sum to 1 — interpretable as a probability distribution over $K$ classes.
- **Used in:** Multi-class classification (e.g., ImageNet with $K = 1000$).

---

## 2.4 Loss Functions — Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 Mean Squared Error (MSE)

$$L_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

- **Gradient:** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = -\frac{2}{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$
- **Best for:** Regression (e.g., predicting house prices, bounding-box coordinates).

### 2.4.2 Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)

$$L_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

- **Best for:** Binary classification (e.g., cat vs. dog, defect vs. non-defect).

### 2.4.3 Categorical Cross-Entropy

$$L_{\text{CCE}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{K} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$

- **Best for:** Multi-class classification with one-hot encoded labels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When using **softmax + cross-entropy**, the combined gradient simplifies beautifully to $\hat{y} - y$, making the backpropagation derivation cleaner. This is why they are the canonical pair in deep learning.

---

## 2.5 Backpropagation — The Gradient Computation Engine

### 2.5.1 The Chain Rule Foundation

For a composition of functions $f(g(h(x)))$:

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{df}{dg} \cdot \frac{dg}{dh} \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}$$

A neural network is precisely such a composition, so the chain rule lets us compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$ for every layer.

### 2.5.2 The Four Backpropagation Equations (Vectorized)

Let $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$ denote the error signal at layer $l$.

**Output layer error (assuming softmax + cross-entropy):**
$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$$

**Hidden layer error (recursion):**
$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \left(\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)\top} \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}\right) \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

**Gradient w.r.t. weights:**
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)\top}$$

**Gradient w.r.t. biases:**
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$$

where $\odot$ is the element-wise (Hadamard) product.

### 2.5.3 Weight Update via Gradient Descent

$$\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$$

$$\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** — a critical hyperparameter.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers 80% of the numerical questions on this topic.**

| Concept | Formula | Key Symbol | Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perceptron Pre-activation | $z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ | $z$ | Scalar or vector |
| Perceptron Output | $\hat{y} = \phi(z)$ | $\hat{y}$ | Task-dependent |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma$ | $(0,1)$ |
| Tanh | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $\tanh$ | $(-1,1)$ |
| ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | $\text{ReLU}$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Softmax | $\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $K$ classes | Sums to 1 |
| Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | $\sigma'$ | Max = 0.25 at $z=0$ |
| Tanh Derivative | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | $\tanh'$ | Max = 1 at $z=0$ |
| ReLU Derivative | $0$ if $z<0$, else $1$ | $\text{ReLU}'$ | Piecewise |
| MSE Loss | $\frac{1}{N}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $L$ | Regression |
| Cross-Entropy | $-\sum y \log(\hat{y})$ | $L$ | Classification |
| Weight Update | $w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$ | $\eta$ | $\eta \in (10^{-4}, 10^{-1})$ |
| Output Error (Softmax+CE) | $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ | $\delta$ | Simplified form |
| Backprop Recursion | $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)\top} \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}) \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | $\delta$ | Chain rule |
| Weight Gradient | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)\top}$ | — | Matrix form |
| Forward Pass (General) | $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \phi(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)})$ | $\mathbf{a}$ | Layer-wise |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Vision:** Backpropagation-trained networks power **autonomous vehicle perception** (Tesla, Waymo), **medical imaging diagnostics** (tumor detection in MRI/CT), and **industrial quality control** (surface defect detection on manufacturing lines).
- **General AI:** Every modern LLM (GPT, BERT), image generator (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E), and speech system (Whisper) is trained using variants of the backpropagation algorithm described here.
- **Optimization Research:** Variants such as **Adam, RMSProp, SGD with momentum** all build directly on the gradient computation framework introduced in this module.
