topics.title: Convolutional Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Convolutional Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a specialized class of deep feed-forward neural networks that employs a mathematical operation called **convolution** (specifically, cross-correlation in practice) in place of general matrix multiplication in at least one of its layers. CNNs are engineered to process data that has a known, grid-like topology — most notably **2D image pixels** and **1D time-series signals**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A CNN is a hierarchical deep-learning architecture that automatically and adaptively learns **spatial feature hierarchies** by stacking alternating **convolutional**, **activation**, and **pooling** layers, terminating in **fully connected (dense)** layers for task-specific prediction (classification, detection, or regression).

The defining architectural constraints of a CNN, in contrast to a vanilla Artificial Neural Network (ANN), are:
- **Local Receptive Fields** (each neuron connects only to a small region of the previous layer)
- **Parameter Sharing** (the same filter weights are slid across the entire input)
- **Spatial Sub-sampling / Pooling** (dimension reduction to enforce translation invariance)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Magnifying Glass on a Photographic Film"

Imagine a detective investigating a large photograph. Instead of staring at the entire photo at once, he slides a small **magnifying glass** (the *filter / kernel*) across the image. At every position, the glass "responds" — it lights up when it spots a useful pattern (an edge, a corner, a texture). He notes down the strength of response at each location in a new, smaller map (the *feature map*). He then repeats this with multiple magnifying glasses, each looking for a different pattern.

The deeper the layers go, the more abstract the patterns become: the first layer detects **edges and color blobs**, middle layers detect **eyes, wheels, windows**, and the final layers detect the **whole face or object**. The "translation invariance" property means — a wheel is a wheel whether it appears on the left or right of the image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Inspiration:** The local-receptive-field concept in CNNs is directly inspired by the organization of the **primary visual cortex (V1)** in the human brain, where individual neurons respond to stimuli only in restricted regions of the visual field, as established by **Hubel and Wiesel (1959, 1962)**.

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks (Block-Level View)

A typical CNN is a sequential pipeline of these functional blocks:

1. **Input Layer** — Holds the raw pixel matrix of the image. For RGB, this is a tensor of shape $\left(H, W, 3\right)$.
2. **Convolutional Layer (CONV)** — Performs the local dot-product between filters and input patches; produces feature maps.
3. **Activation Layer (ReLU)** — Introduces non-linearity via $f(x) = \max(0, x)$.
4. **Pooling Layer (POOL)** — Down-samples feature maps to enforce spatial invariance and reduce computation.
5. **Fully Connected Layer (FC)** — High-level reasoning; maps the learned features to the final output classes.
6. **Output Layer (Softmax / Sigmoid)** — Produces class probabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** The number of **trainable parameters** in a CNN must always be calculated. The CONV layers share parameters (low count), while FC layers do not (high count). This is why CNNs are far more parameter-efficient than fully connected ANNs for image data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Working

### Step 1 — The Convolution Operation (Mathematical Foundation)
A convolution layer applies a learnable **kernel** (filter) $K$ of size $F \times F \times D$ (where $D$ = depth of input) that slides over the input volume $I$ of size $H \times W \times D$. At each spatial location $(i, j)$, the network computes the **dot product** between the kernel and the local input patch.

> **Why convolution?** Because the same kernel is reused across all spatial locations, the network is forced to learn **position-independent feature detectors** (e.g., an edge-detector works regardless of where the edge appears in the image).

### Step 2 — Stride and Padding
- **Stride ($S$):** The number of pixels the filter shifts at each step. Default is $S = 1$.
- **Padding ($P$):** Zero-valued pixels added around the input border to control output size. Most common is $P = (F-1)/2$ ("Same Padding"), which preserves spatial dimensions.

### Step 3 — Non-Linear Activation (ReLU)
After the linear convolution output, the **Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU)** is applied element-wise:

$$
f(x) = \max(0, x)
$$

It is preferred over sigmoid/tanh in deep CNNs because it mitigates the **vanishing gradient problem** and induces **sparse activations** (a form of implicit regularization).

### Step 4 — Pooling (Sub-Sampling)
A pooling operation summarizes a local neighborhood into a single value. Two dominant types:

- **Max Pooling:** Takes the maximum value in the $F \times F$ window. **Most commonly used.**
- **Average Pooling:** Takes the average of the window. Used in the final layer sometimes (Global Average Pooling).

> **Why pooling?** To progressively reduce the spatial size of the feature maps, decreasing computational cost and enforcing a small degree of **translation invariance**.

### Step 5 — Fully Connected & Softmax
After several CONV → ReLU → POOL blocks, the 3D feature volume is **flattened** into a 1D vector and fed into a standard MLP. The final layer uses a **Softmax** activation for multi-class classification:

$$
P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}
$$

where $K$ is the number of output classes and $z_k$ is the logit of class $k$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Conv Output Dimension | $H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $H$: height (px), $F$: filter (px), $P$: pad (px), $S$: stride (px) | Architectural dimensioning |
| 2 | Number of Parameters in a CONV Layer | $N_p = (F \times F \times D_{in} + 1) \times K$ | $K$: number of filters, $+1$ for bias per filter | Memory / FLOPs estimation |
| 3 | Number of Parameters in an FC Layer | $N_p = (N_{in} + 1) \times N_{out}$ | $N_{in}$: input neurons, $N_{out}$: output neurons | Memory budgeting |
| 4 | ReLU Activation | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Unitless | Non-linearity injection |
| 5 | Sigmoid Activation | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | Unitless | Binary classification output |
| 6 | Softmax (multi-class) | $P_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | $z_k$: logit, $P_k \in [0,1]$ | Probability vector output |
| 7 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$ | Unitless | Training objective |
| 8 | Receptive Field Growth (Stacking CONVs) | $RF_{n} = RF_{n-1} + (F_n - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} S_i$ | $F_n$: filter size, $S_i$: stride of layer $i$ | Contextual field of view |
| 9 | Total Receptive Field (with Pooling) | $RF_{total} = 1 + \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} (F_\ell - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{\ell-1} S_i \cdot P_i$ | $P_i$: pooling stride of layer $i$ | Determines context size |
| 10 | Number of MACs per CONV layer | $MACs = H_{out} \times W_{out} \times F \times F \times D_{in} \times K$ | FLOPs $\approx 2 \times MACs$ | Hardware profiling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** In KTU board papers, do **not** confuse **convolution** (which mathematically flips the kernel) with **cross-correlation** (which does not). All standard deep-learning frameworks — TensorFlow, PyTorch — implement **cross-correlation** and call it convolution. For exam purposes, state explicitly: *"Deep-learning libraries use cross-correlation, not strict convolution."*

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Tumor detection in MRI / CT scans (U-Net architecture).
- **Autonomous Driving:** Real-time road-sign and pedestrian detection.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Defect classification on PCB / metal surfaces.
- **Agriculture:** Disease detection in leaf images.
- **Satellite & Remote Sensing:** Land-use classification, disaster assessment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Dimension of a Convolutional Layer

Let the input image have spatial dimensions $H_{in} \times W_{in}$. We slide a filter of size $F \times F$ with stride $S$ and padding $P$ (on each side).

**Total span traversed by the filter (vertical) = $H_{in} + 2P - F + 1$.**
The number of distinct filter positions equals the integer part of this span divided by the stride:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{out} &= \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2P - F}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
        &= \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The "$-F$" accounts for the filter size occupying $F$ pixels, "$+2P$" accounts for padding on both top and bottom, and the floor function handles non-integer division. The same formula applies to $W_{out}$ by symmetry.

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Trainable Parameters in a Conv Layer

Each filter has spatial extent $F \times F$ and must span the **entire depth** $D_{in}$ of the input volume (so it sees all color channels simultaneously). Therefore, the number of weights in one filter is:

$$
W_{filter} = F \times F \times D_{in}
$$

Adding one bias term per filter: $W_{filter} + 1 = F \times F \times D_{in} + 1$.
Multiplying by the total number of filters $K$ (which determines the depth of the output volume):

$$
N_p = (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot K
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** An RGB image of size $32 \times 32 \times 3$ is fed to a CONV layer with 10 filters of size $5 \times 5$, stride $S=1$, and padding $P=2$ ("Same Padding"). Compute: (a) Output volume shape, (b) Total parameters, (c) Total MACs.

### Part (a) — Output Volume Shape

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{out} &= \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
        &= \left\lfloor \frac{27 + 4}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
        &= 31 + 1 = 32 \\
W_{out} &= 32 \text{ (by symmetry)} \\
D_{out} &= K = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Output volume $\to 32 \times 32 \times 10$.

### Part (b) — Total Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_p &= (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot K \\
    &= (5 \times 5 \times 3 + 1) \cdot 10 \\
    &= (75 + 1) \cdot 10 \\
    &= 76 \times 10 = 760 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Only **760 parameters**! Compare this to a fully connected layer mapping $32 \times 32 \times 3 = 3072$ inputs to 10 outputs: $(3072 + 1) \times 10 = 30{,}730$ parameters — a **40× reduction**.

### Part (c) — Total Multiply-Accumulate Operations (MACs)

$$
\begin{aligned}
MACs &= H_{out} \times W_{out} \times F \times F \times D_{in} \times K \\
     &= 32 \times 32 \times 5 \times 5 \times 3 \times 10 \\
     &= 1024 \times 25 \times 3 \times 10 \\
     &= 768{,}000 \text{ MACs}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total FLOPs $\approx 2 \times MACs = 1{,}536{,}000$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Forward Pass of a Single Convolution (Cross-Correlation)

Consider an input patch $\mathbf{X}$ of size $F \times F$ and a filter $\mathbf{K}$ of the same size. The **cross-correlation** output at one spatial location is:

$$
Z = \sum_{i=1}^{F} \sum_{j=1}^{F} X_{ij} \cdot K_{ij} + b
$$

where $b$ is the scalar bias. Stacking this for all $H_{out} \times W_{out}$ positions yields a 2D **feature map** $Z_{ij}$. With $K$ filters, the full layer output is a 3D tensor of size $H_{out} \times W_{out} \times K$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of CNN vs. Vanilla ANN for Image Data

| Property | Vanilla ANN (MLP) | CNN |
|----------|------------------|-----|
| Input representation | Flattened 1D vector | 3D tensor $(H, W, D)$ |
| Parameter count | Very high (e.g., $\sim$10M for $224 \times 224$) | Much lower due to weight sharing |
| Spatial locality | Ignored (treats far pixels identically) | Exploited (local receptive fields) |
| Translation invariance | Not enforced | Strong (via pooling + weight sharing) |
| Training data required | Very large | Comparatively smaller |
| Feature engineering | Manual (SIFT, HOG, etc.) | Automatic hierarchical learning |
| Compute (FLOPs) for $224 \times 224$ | $\mathcal{O}(H^2 W^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(H \cdot W \cdot F^2 \cdot D \cdot K)$ |
| Overfitting tendency | High (too many parameters) | Moderate (built-in regularization) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 High-Level CNN Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1["INPUT IMAGE  HxWx3"]
    conv1["CONV LAYER  F=5 S=1 P=2  K=10  ReLU"]
    pool1["MAX POOL  F=2 S=2"]
    conv2["CONV LAYER  F=5 S=1 P=2  K=20  ReLU"]
    pool2["MAX POOL  F=2 S=2"]
    flat["FLATTEN  VECTOR"]
    fc1["FC LAYER  128 NEURONS  ReLU"]
    drop["DROPOUT  p=0.5"]
    fc2["FC OUTPUT  K CLASSES  SOFTMAX"]

    input1 --> conv1 --> pool1 --> conv2 --> pool2 --> flat --> fc1 --> drop --> fc2

    classDef conv fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pool fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef fc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class input1,fc2 io
    class conv1,conv2 conv
    class pool1,pool2 pool
    class fc1,flat,drop fc
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Forward Pass Through One CONV Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT TENSOR  HxWxD_in"]
    B["EXTRACT PATCH  FxFxD_in"]
    C["MULTIPLY ELEMENT WISE WITH FILTER  FxFxD_in"]
    D["SUM ALL VALUES  PLUS BIAS b"]
    E["PRODUCE SINGLE SCALAR z_ij"]
    F["SLIDE FILTER  STRIDE S  ACROSS ALL POSITIONS"]
    G["COLLECT ALL z_ij  INTO FEATURE MAP  H_out x W_out"]
    H["REPEAT FOR EACH OF K FILTERS"]
    I["OUTPUT TENSOR  H_out x W_out x K"]
    J["APPLY ReLU  ELEMENT WISE"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J

    classDef step fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#fff0f0,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    class A,J io
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H step
```

## 4.3 Spatial Dimension Evolution Through the Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0["32x32x3  INPUT"]
    s1["32x32x10  AFTER CONV1  K=10 P=2"]
    s2["16x16x10  AFTER POOL1  S=2"]
    s3["16x16x20  AFTER CONV2  K=20 P=2"]
    s4["8x8x20  AFTER POOL2  S=2"]
    s5["1x1x128  AFTER FC1  DENSE LAYER"]
    s6["1x1xK  SOFTMAX  CLASS PROBABILITIES"]

    s0 --> s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6

    classDef shrink fill:#ffe7ba,stroke:#874d00,color:#000
    classDef dense fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#135200,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#ffd6e7,stroke:#780650,color:#000
    class s0,s6 io
    class s1,s2,s3,s4 shrink
    class s5 dense
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Feature Learning (Visual Cortex Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["LAYER 0  INPUT PIXELS"]
    L1["LAYER 1  EDGES AND COLOR GRADIENTS"]
    L2["LAYER 2  TEXTURES AND PATTERN MOTIFS"]
    L3["LAYER 3  OBJECT PARTS  WHEELS EYES WINDOWS"]
    L4["LAYER 4  FULL OBJECTS  CAR FACE DOG"]
    L5["LAYER 5  SCENE SEMANTICS  STREET KITCHEN"]

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    classDef lvl fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#1d39c4,color:#000
    class L0,L1,L2,L3,L4,L5 lvl
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Convolutional Neural Network. List any two advantages of CNNs over traditional fully connected neural networks for image classification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a class of deep neural networks specifically designed to process data with grid-like topology, such as images, by employing convolution (cross-correlation) operations in place of general matrix multiplication. It learns hierarchical spatial features through alternating convolutional, activation, and pooling layers.

**Two advantages over fully connected ANNs:**
1. **Parameter Sharing:** The same filter weights are used across all spatial locations, drastically reducing the total number of trainable parameters.
2. **Local Receptive Fields & Translation Invariance:** CNNs exploit spatial locality of pixels and are robust to the position of features in the input image.
3. *(Any other valid point: automatic feature extraction, hierarchical learning, robustness to small shifts/distortions — 1 mark for any one valid advantage.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the pooling layer in a CNN? Differentiate between max pooling and average pooling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Role of Pooling Layer (1.5 marks):**
The pooling layer performs **spatial down-sampling** of feature maps. Its main roles are:
- Reducing the spatial dimensions (height and width) of feature maps, thereby lowering computational cost and memory footprint.
- Introducing a small degree of **translation invariance** — small shifts in the input do not change the pooled output.
- Acting as a form of implicit regularization, helping reduce overfitting.

**Max Pooling vs. Average Pooling (1.5 marks):**

| Property | Max Pooling | Average Pooling |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Operation | Takes the **maximum** value in the window | Takes the **average** of all values in the window |
| Use Case | Most common in intermediate layers; preserves strong activations | Often used in final layers (Global Average Pooling) |
| Sensitivity | Captures the most prominent feature | Smooths the feature map; may dilute strong features |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the bias term** in parameter-count questions (always $+1$ per filter/neuron).
> - **Confusing cross-correlation with strict convolution** — KTU expects you to state that DL libraries use cross-correlation.
> - **Ignoring padding on both sides** — total padding is $2P$, not $P$.
> - **Not flattening the tensor** before the FC layer in architectural diagrams.
> - Using the word "convolution" without qualifying that in deep learning it is technically **cross-correlation**.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical Convolutional Neural Network used for image classification. Describe the function of each major layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An input grayscale image of size $28 \times 28 \times 1$ is passed through a CONV layer with 8 filters of size $3 \times 3$, stride $S = 1$, and padding $P = 1$. Calculate: (i) the spatial dimensions of the output feature map, (ii) the number of trainable parameters in this layer, and (iii) the total number of multiply-accumulate operations (MACs). **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A:**

**Part (a) — CNN Architecture (7 marks):**

A typical CNN for image classification consists of the following sequential layers (refer to the block diagram in SECTION 4.1):

1. **Input Layer (0.5 mark):** Holds the raw pixel matrix. For grayscale: $(H, W, 1)$; for RGB: $(H, W, 3)$.
2. **Convolutional Layer (CONV) (1.5 marks):** Applies a set of learnable filters (kernels) that slide across the input. Each filter detects a specific local pattern (edge, texture, etc.). The output is a stack of 2D **feature maps**, one per filter. Mathematically, each output pixel $Z_{ij}$ is the dot product of the filter weights $\mathbf{K}$ and the local input patch $\mathbf{X}$:

$$
Z_{ij} = \sum_{u=1}^{F} \sum_{v=1}^{F} X_{i+u, j+v} \cdot K_{u,v} + b
$$

3. **Activation — ReLU (1 mark):** Applied element-wise after every CONV to introduce non-linearity: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$.
4. **Pooling Layer (1 mark):** Down-samples each feature map (commonly max pooling with $2 \times 2$ window and stride 2), halving the spatial dimensions and enforcing local translation invariance.
5. **Fully Connected (Dense) Layer (1 mark):** The 3D feature volume is flattened and fed into one or more FC layers that perform high-level reasoning.
6. **Output Layer — Softmax (1 mark):** Produces a probability distribution over the $K$ classes using:

$$
P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}
$$

The class with the highest probability is the final predicted label.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $H_{in} = W_{in} = 28$, $D_{in} = 1$, $F = 3$, $S = 1$, $P = 1$, $K = 8$.

**(i) Output Spatial Dimensions (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{out} &= \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
        &= \left\lfloor \frac{28 - 3 + 2(1)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
        &= \lfloor 25 + 2 \rfloor + 1 \\
        &= 27 + 1 = 28
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry, $W_{out} = 28$. Output volume $\to 28 \times 28 \times 8$.

**(ii) Trainable Parameters (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_p &= (F \times F \times D_{in} + 1) \times K \\
    &= (3 \times 3 \times 1 + 1) \times 8 \\
    &= (9 + 1) \times 8 \\
    &= 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total MACs (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MACs &= H_{out} \times W_{out} \times F \times F \times D_{in} \times K \\
     &= 28 \times 28 \times 3 \times 3 \times 1 \times 8 \\
     &= 784 \times 9 \times 8 \\
     &= 56{,}448 \text{ MACs}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total FLOPs $\approx 2 \times 56{,}448 = 112{,}896$ FLOPs.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the following operations in a CNN with neat illustrations: (i) Convolution with stride and padding, (ii) Max pooling with a $2 \times 2$ window and stride 2. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast a standard Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and a CNN for the task of image classification. Use a quantitative example: for a $64 \times 64$ RGB image fed to a network with a single hidden layer of 100 neurons, compute the parameters in (i) a fully connected ANN and (ii) a CNN front-end with 16 filters of size $5 \times 5$, stride 1, padding 2, followed by the same hidden layer. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B:**

**Part (a) — Convolution and Pooling Operations (7 marks):**

**(i) Convolution with Stride and Padding (3.5 marks):**

The convolution operation slides a small filter $K$ of size $F \times F$ across the input image. At each spatial position, the element-wise product of the filter and the overlapping input patch is summed, plus a bias. The filter then shifts by $S$ pixels (the **stride**). To control the output spatial size and preserve border information, **zero-padding** of $P$ pixels is applied around the input border.

- **Stride $S = 1$:** Filter moves 1 pixel at a time — produces a large output.
- **Stride $S = 2$:** Filter moves 2 pixels — produces a smaller, down-sampled output.
- **Padding $P$:** Adds $P$ rows/columns of zeros around the input.

The output size is governed by:

$$
H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Worked mini-example:** Input $5 \times 5$, Filter $3 \times 3$, $S=1$, $P=0$:

$$
H_{out} = \frac{5 - 3 + 0}{1} + 1 = 3 \Rightarrow \text{output } 3 \times 3
$$

If we set $P=1$ (one zero-pixel border), input becomes $7 \times 7$:

$$
H_{out} = \frac{7 - 3 + 0}{1} + 1 = 5 \Rightarrow \text{output } 5 \times 5 \text{ (same as input)}
$$

This is the famous **"Same Padding"** trick.

**(ii) Max Pooling (3.5 marks):**

Max pooling divides each feature map into non-overlapping (or partially overlapping) $2 \times 2$ windows and outputs the **maximum value** from each window. With stride $S = 2$, the spatial dimensions are **halved**.

**Worked example on a $4 \times 4$ feature map:**

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 & 1 & 2 \\ 7 & 8 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 & 5 & 6 \end{pmatrix}
\;\xrightarrow{\;\text{MaxPool } 2\times2,\;S=2\;}\;
\begin{pmatrix} 6 & 4 \\ 8 & 6 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The maximum of $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 5 & 6 \end{vmatrix}$ is 6, and so on. The result is a $2 \times 2$ map, reducing computation by $4 \times$ in this layer.

---

**Part (b) — Quantitative Comparison (7 marks):**

Given: RGB image $64 \times 64 \times 3$, hidden layer of 100 neurons.

**(i) Parameters in a Fully Connected ANN (3.5 marks):**

In a vanilla ANN, the input is **flattened** to a vector of length $64 \times 64 \times 3 = 12{,}288$. Each of the 100 hidden neurons connects to all 12,288 input values, plus one bias each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{p,\text{ANN}} &= (N_{in} + 1) \times N_{hidden} \\
                 &= (12{,}288 + 1) \times 100 \\
                 &= 12{,}289 \times 100 \\
                 &= 1{,}228{,}900 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Parameters in the CNN Front-End (3.5 marks):**

CONV layer: $F = 5$, $D_{in} = 3$, $K = 16$, $P = 2$, $S = 1$.

Output dimensions (with same padding): $64 \times 64 \times 16$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{p,\text{CONV}} &= (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot K \\
                  &= (5 \times 5 \times 3 + 1) \times 16 \\
                  &= 76 \times 16 = 1{,}216 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

Flatten: $64 \times 64 \times 16 = 65{,}536$ input neurons to hidden layer.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{p,\text{FC}} &= (65{,}536 + 1) \times 100 = 6{,}553{,}700 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total CNN parameters:

$$
N_{p,\text{CNN}} = 1{,}216 + 6{,}553{,}700 = 6{,}554{,}916 \text{ parameters}
$$

**Comparison (extra credit insight):**
- The CONV layer itself is shockingly **parameter-efficient** — only 1,216 weights to learn the spatial features, regardless of input size.
- Most CNN parameters still come from the **FC tail**, which is why modern architectures (ResNet, MobileNet) prefer **Global Average Pooling** to eliminate the FC tail.
- The CNN's CONV layer has the **same 1,216 parameters** even if the image were $1024 \times 1024$ — this is the power of **weight sharing**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- **CNN Definition:** Deep network using convolution (cross-correlation) on grid-structured data, learning hierarchical features automatically.
- **Three Pillars of CNN:** Local Receptive Fields + Weight Sharing + Pooling.
- **Core Layers in Order:** INPUT $\to$ [CONV $\to$ ReLU $\to$ POOL] $\times N$ $\to$ FLATTEN $\to$ FC $\to$ SOFTMAX.
- **Output Size Formula:** $H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$.
- **Same Padding Trick:** $P = (F - 1) / 2$ when $S = 1$ preserves spatial dimensions.
- **Parameter Formula (CONV):** $N_p = (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot K$ — **always add the +1 bias**.
- **Parameter Formula (FC):** $N_p = (N_{in} + 1) \cdot N_{out}$.
- **ReLU:** $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ — preferred activation; mitigates vanishing gradients.
- **Softmax:** $P_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ — produces class probabilities summing to 1.
- **Max Pooling:** Takes maximum of a window; preserves the strongest activation.
- **Average Pooling:** Takes mean; smooths the feature map.
- **Cross-Correlation vs Convolution:** Deep learning libraries use cross-correlation (no kernel flip).
- **Stride $S$:** Controls down-sampling — larger stride = smaller output.
- **Padding $P$:** Zero border to preserve spatial size or improve edge detection.
- **Receptive Field:** Grows with depth; the final layer "sees" a large context of the original image.
- **Translation Invariance:** Achieved via weight sharing + pooling.
- **Biological Inspiration:** Hubel & Wiesel's V1 visual cortex experiments.
- **Hierarchical Features:** Edges $\to$ Textures $\to$ Parts $\to$ Objects $\to$ Scenes.
- **MACs vs FLOPs:** FLOPs $\approx 2 \times$ MACs in a CONV layer.
- **Advantages of CNN over ANN:** Fewer parameters, exploits spatial locality, automatic feature engineering, translation invariance, robust to small distortions.
- **Disadvantages of CNN:** Requires GPU for training, opaque/interpretability issues, large labelled datasets needed.
- **Loss Function:** Cross-entropy $L = -\sum_k y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$.
- **Backprop Concept:** Gradients flow backward through pooling (upsample), ReLU (gradient mask), and CONV (transposed convolution).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instance Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instance Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Instance Recognition** is a fundamental computer vision task that involves identifying, localizing, and verifying the presence of a *specific known object instance* (a particular physical exemplar) within an image or a sequence of images, distinguishing it from other instances of the same category.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> Instance recognition is the process of matching a query image (or region) against a database of *previously seen, specific object exemplars* and assigning an identity label along with a geometric pose (position, scale, orientation). It is a *closed-world problem* — the system knows exactly which instances it is looking for.

Unlike **object categorization** (which asks "Is this a *car*?"), instance recognition asks *"Is this **my** car — the red Swift Dzire KL-01-AB-1234?"* This subtle distinction is a frequent KTU exam question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-categories of Recognition in Machine Vision (KTU Module 4):**
> 1. **Instance Recognition** — Find a specific known object.
> 2. **Category Recognition (Classification)** — Find any object of a class.
> 3. **Detection** — Localize instances with bounding boxes.
> 4. **Semantic Segmentation** — Pixel-level class labeling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you walk into a huge library containing 10 million books.

- **Object Categorization** is like asking the librarian: *"Do you have any mystery novels?"* — They just point to the *Mystery* section.
- **Instance Recognition** is like asking: *"Do you have the **first edition, 1954 print** of Tolkien's *The Lord of the Rings* with the broken spine and the coffee stain on page 47?"* — They must pull out *that exact book*, verify it, and tell you its shelf coordinates.

In machine vision, the "specific book" is replaced by a *keypoint descriptor signature*, and the "shelf coordinates" become the **6-DoF pose** (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world deployment examples of instance recognition:**
> - Industrial part picking (bin-picking robots).
> - AR/VR: Recognizing a specific landmark via phone camera.
> - Retail: Identifying counterfeit products.
> - Medical imaging: Matching a tumor slice against a patient's prior scan.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The most important numerical metric in instance recognition is the **IoU (Intersection over Union) threshold**, which is conventionally fixed at **0.5** for the PASCAL VOC standard. Modern standards (MS-COCO) use a *mean* over thresholds 0.5 to 0.95 in steps of 0.05.

Other standard metrics that must be memorized:
- **Precision (P)** and **Recall (R)**
- **mAP (mean Average Precision)**
- **F1-Score**
- **Inlier ratio** for geometric verification (typically a *minimum of 3* inlier correspondences is required for a valid homography).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (The "Why" and "How")

Instance recognition systems are almost universally built on a **detect-then-describe-then-match** pipeline. The "why" behind each step is critical for KTU theory questions.

**Step 1 — Interest Point Detection (Why?):**
Pixels are redundant. We need *salient*, *repeatable* locations (corners, blobs) that survive changes in scale, rotation, and illumination. The "how" is to apply a detector like **Harris**, **SIFT**, or **ORB** to find these keypoints.

**Step 2 — Local Feature Description (Why?):**
A keypoint is just a coordinate. We need a *vector* (descriptor) that summarizes the local image patch in a way that is *invariant* to the nuisance transformations. The "how" is to compute a histogram of oriented gradients (SIFT), binary tests (ORB), or a learned CNN embedding.

**Step 3 — Matching (Why?):**
Descriptors from the query image must be compared against descriptors from every model image in the database. The "how" is to use **Nearest Neighbor Search** (e.g., k-d tree, FLANN, or approximate methods) with a distance metric such as **Euclidean (L2)** for SIFT or **Hamming** for ORB.

**Step 4 — Geometric Verification (Why?):**
Pure descriptor matching is *approximate* and produces many false positives. We need to confirm that the matched keypoints are *spatially consistent*. The "how" is to estimate a geometric transformation (typically a **Homography H** for planar objects) using **RANSAC** and reject outliers.

**Step 5 — Hypothesis Scoring & Decision (Why?):**
Among multiple competing hypotheses (since a query can match several database models), we need to rank them. The "how" is to count **inliers** (the number of matches consistent with the estimated transformation). The model with the most inliers wins.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Euclidean Distance (L2)** between SIFT descriptors $d_A, d_B \in \mathbb{R}^{128}$ | $\text{dist}(A,B) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{128}(d_{A,i} - d_{B,i})^2}$ | Pixel-intensity units (normalized). Lower is better. |
| 2 | **Hamming Distance** for ORB / binary descriptors | $d_H(b_1, b_2) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_{1,i} \oplus b_{2,i}$ | Bits (0 to $n$, where $n=256$ for ORB). Lower is better. |
| 3 | **Lowe's Ratio Test** (rejects ambiguous matches) | $\text{ratio} = \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} < \tau$ | Threshold $\tau \approx 0.7$ to $0.8$ (empirical). |
| 4 | **Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index)** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}$ | Range $[0, 1]$. Standard threshold = **0.5** for PASCAL VOC. |
| 5 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Range $[0, 1]$. |
| 6 | **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Range $[0, 1]$. |
| 7 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Range $[0, 1]$. Harmonic mean of P and R. |
| 8 | **Average Precision (AP)** | $AP = \int_0^1 p(r) \, dr$ (area under P-R curve) | Range $[0, 1]$. |
| 9 | **mAP** (mean over $C$ classes) | $mAP = \dfrac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$ | Range $[0, 1]$. |
| 10 | **Homography Matrix** (planar object, 3×3) | $H = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 8 degrees of freedom → needs ≥ 4 point correspondences. |
| 11 | **RANSAC Iterations** (probability $p$ of success, inlier ratio $\epsilon$) | $N = \dfrac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-\epsilon^s)}$ | $s$ = samples per iteration (4 for homography). |
| 12 | **SIFT Scale** | $\sigma \leftarrow k \cdot \sigma$, typically $k = 2^{1/3}$ | Octaves double $\sigma$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> The IoU formula is the cornerstone of *every modern detector* (YOLO, Faster R-CNN, Mask R-CNN). The Lowe ratio test is the single most important trick taught by David Lowe in his landmark 2004 SIFT paper. RANSAC iteration count directly controls the **trade-off between speed and robustness** in real-time systems (e.g., autonomous vehicles).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Homography Transformation (Planar Instance)

When a planar object is viewed from two different camera angles, the mapping from the model plane to the image plane is a **homography**. Deriving the mathematics is a common KTU 14-mark question.

**Setup:** Let $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, 1)^T$ be a point in the model image, and $\mathbf{x}' = (x', y', 1)^T$ be the corresponding point in the query image. The relationship is a projective transformation.

$$
\mathbf{x}' \sim H \mathbf{x}
$$

In expanded form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= \dfrac{h_{11} x + h_{12} y + h_{13}}{h_{31} x + h_{32} y + h_{33}} \\
y' &= \dfrac{h_{21} x + h_{22} y + h_{23}}{h_{31} x + h_{32} y + h_{33}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Count the unknowns.**
The matrix $H$ has 9 entries, but projective scaling removes 1 degree of freedom (multiplying all of $H$ by a non-zero constant yields the same transformation). Therefore, $H$ has **8 degrees of freedom**.

**Step 2 — Each correspondence provides 2 equations.**
A single point pair $(x, y) \leftrightarrow (x', y')$ gives the two equations above (numerator of $x'$ and $y'$). Cross-multiplying to clear the denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x'(h_{31} x + h_{32} y + h_{33}) &= h_{11} x + h_{12} y + h_{13} \\
y'(h_{31} x + h_{32} y + h_{33}) &= h_{21} x + h_{22} y + h_{23}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stack the equations.**
Rearranging into the standard form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$, where $\mathbf{h}$ is the 9-vector of entries of $H$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
x & y & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -x'x & -x'y & -x' \\
0 & 0 & 0 & x & y & 1 & -y'x & -y'y & -y'
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} h_{11} \\ h_{12} \\ h_{13} \\ h_{21} \\ h_{22} \\ h_{23} \\ h_{31} \\ h_{32} \\ h_{33} \end{bmatrix}
= \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum number of correspondences.**
Since each point gives 2 equations and we have 8 unknowns, we need at least $\lceil 8/2 \rceil = 4$ point pairs. With exactly 4 pairs, the system is *square* (8×8) and can be solved directly. With more than 4, it becomes over-determined and is solved via **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** by minimizing the algebraic error $\Vert A\mathbf{h} \Vert_2$ subject to $\Vert \mathbf{h} \Vert_2 = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ≥ 4 points?**
> KTU students frequently answer "3 points" — this is *wrong*. Three points can determine an affine transformation (6 DOF), but a homography has **8 DOF** and requires **4 point correspondences**. The 4th point disambiguates the projective component (vanishing-line mapping).

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: Lowe's Ratio Test

A common KTU problem: "Given two SIFT matches with distances to the 1st and 2nd nearest neighbors, determine if the match is accepted."

**Given:**
- Match to Database Image A: nearest distance $d_1 = 0.12$, second-nearest $d_2 = 0.41$
- Match to Database Image B: nearest distance $d_1' = 0.18$, second-nearest $d_2' = 0.21$

**Threshold:** $\tau = 0.75$

**Step 1 — Compute ratio for Image A.**

$$
\text{ratio}_A = \dfrac{0.12}{0.41} \approx 0.293
$$

Since $0.293 < 0.75$, the match to Image A is **ACCEPTED** (the descriptor is *unambiguous*).

**Step 2 — Compute ratio for Image B.**

$$
\text{ratio}_B = \dfrac{0.18}{0.21} \approx 0.857
$$

Since $0.857 \geq 0.75$, the match to Image B is **REJECTED** (the descriptor is *ambiguous* — almost equally close to two different features, likely a repetitive texture like windows on a building).

> [!TIP]
> **The Logic:** Lowe showed that a *true* match has a nearest neighbor *significantly* closer than the second-nearest. Ratio ≈ 1 means the feature is in a "crowded" part of descriptor space (e.g., grass, foliage, repetitive patterns) and cannot be reliably matched.

## 3.3 Worked Example: IoU Calculation

**Given:** Ground-truth bounding box $B_{gt} = (0, 0, 10, 10)$ (top-left and bottom-right), and predicted box $B_p = (2, 2, 11, 11)$.

**Step 1 — Area of ground truth.**

$$
A_{gt} = (10 - 0) \times (10 - 0) = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Area of predicted.**

$$
A_p = (11 - 2) \times (11 - 2) = 81
$$

**Step 3 — Intersection coordinates.**
Top-left = $\max(0, 2) = 2$, $\max(0, 2) = 2$.
Bottom-right = $\min(10, 11) = 10$, $\min(10, 11) = 10$.

$$
A_{int} = (10 - 2) \times (10 - 2) = 64
$$

**Step 4 — Union.**

$$
A_{union} = A_{gt} + A_p - A_{int} = 100 + 81 - 64 = 117
$$

**Step 5 — IoU.**

$$
\text{IoU} = \dfrac{64}{117} \approx 0.547
$$

Since $0.547 > 0.5$, the prediction is a **TRUE POSITIVE** under the PASCAL VOC standard.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Template Matching | Feature-based (SIFT/ORB) | Deep Learning (CNN) |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| **Invariance to Scale** | ✗ (Needs pyramid) | ✓ (Built-in via scale-space) | ✓ (Learned) |
| **Invariance to Rotation** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Occlusion Robustness** | ✗ | ✓ (Local features survive) | Partial |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Training Required** | None | None | Yes (large dataset) |
| **KTU 2024 Status** | Foundational | **Core** | Supplementary |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical Instance Recognition Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query Image] --> B[Interest Point Detector]
    A1[Model Image Database] --> B1[Interest Point Detector]
    B --> C[Keypoint Set Query]
    B1 --> C1[Keypoint Set Database]
    C --> D[Local Descriptor Extractor]
    C1 --> D1[Local Descriptor Extractor]
    D --> E[Descriptor Vectors Query]
    D1 --> E1[Descriptor Vectors Database]
    E --> F[Nearest Neighbor Matcher]
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[Lowe Ratio Test Filter]
    G --> H[RANSAC Geometric Verification]
    H --> I[Homography H Estimation]
    I --> J[Inlier Count Computation]
    J --> K[Best Model Hypothesis]
    K --> L[Instance Identity + Pose Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (left-to-right, top-to-bottom):** Two parallel branches (Query and Database) compute keypoints and descriptors independently. They converge at the *matcher*, then a sequence of filters (ratio test, RANSAC) progressively removes outliers. The final block outputs both the **identity** and the **geometric pose**.

## 4.2 Geometric Verification via RANSAC (Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: N putative matches] --> P1[Set inlier count = 0, best H = none]
    P1 --> P2[Randomly sample 4 point pairs]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Homography H from 4 pairs]
    P3 --> P4[Apply H to all model points]
    P4 --> P5[Compute symmetric transfer error e for each]
    P5 --> P6{e less than threshold t?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Count as inlier]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Reject as outlier]
    P7 --> P9[Total inliers in this iteration]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10{More inliers than best so far?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Update best H and best count]
    P10 -- No --> P12[Discard H]
    P11 --> P13{Max iterations N reached?}
    P12 --> P13
    P13 -- No --> P2
    P13 -- Yes --> P14[Return best H and inlier set]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "4 point pairs"?** This directly ties back to Section 3.1 — a homography has 8 DOF, and each correspondence provides 2 equations, so the *minimum sample size* is 4.

## 4.3 Recognition Hierarchy (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Visual Recognition Tasks] --> R1[Instance Recognition]
    R0 --> R2[Category Recognition]
    R0 --> R3[Detection and Localization]
    R0 --> R4[Semantic Segmentation]
    R1 --> R1a[Template Matching]
    R1 --> R1b[Feature Based Matching]
    R1 --> R1c[Deep Embedding Matching]
    R1b --> R1b1[SIFT and SURF]
    R1b --> R1b2[ORB and BRISK]
    R1b --> R1b3[Bag of Visual Words]
    R1c --> R1c1[Siamese Networks]
    R1c --> R1c2[Metric Learning]
    R3 --> R3a[Sliding Window Classifiers]
    R3 --> R3b[Region Proposal CNN]
    R3 --> R3c[Single Shot YOLO Family]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between Object Categorization and Instance Recognition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Object categorization is a coarse task that assigns a *class label* to an image (e.g., "this is a *car*"). Instance recognition is a fine-grained task that identifies a *specific physical object* (e.g., "this is the *blue Toyota Etios KL-32-AC-7766*"). Categorization is a *closed-set* problem with thousands of classes, while instance recognition is typically a *closed-world* problem where the system only needs to recognize a small set of pre-enrolled objects. Categorization uses global features (CNNs, HOG), while instance recognition uses local invariant features (SIFT, ORB) combined with geometric verification.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the significance of Lowe's ratio test in feature matching. What happens if the threshold is set too high or too low?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lowe's ratio test compares the distance to the nearest neighbor with the distance to the second-nearest neighbor; a match is accepted only if the ratio is below a threshold $\tau$ (typically 0.7-0.8). It eliminates *ambiguous* matches in repetitive regions (e.g., windows, foliage) where many features look similar. If $\tau$ is **too low** (e.g., 0.3), too many correct matches are rejected, reducing recall. If $\tau$ is **too high** (e.g., 0.99), too many false positives are accepted, reducing precision. The choice of $\tau$ directly controls the **precision-recall trade-off**.

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the SIFT algorithm with its four major stages. Explain why SIFT is considered *invariant* to scale, rotation, and partially to illumination.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the following putative matches between a model image and a query image (SIFT descriptor distances, kd-tree search):

| Match | 1st NN Distance | 2nd NN Distance |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| M1    | 0.15            | 0.55            |
| M2    | 0.22            | 0.27            |
| M3    | 0.18            | 0.62            |
| M4    | 0.30            | 0.48            |
| M5    | 0.10            | 0.40            |

Apply Lowe's ratio test with $\tau = 0.75$. List the accepted matches and justify the rejections.

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — SIFT Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Scale-Space Extrema Detection:** Construct a Gaussian scale-space by convolving the image with Gaussian kernels of increasing $\sigma$:

$$
L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)
$$

The Difference of Gaussians (DoG) is computed between consecutive octaves:

$$
D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)
$$

Candidate keypoints are local extrema of $D(x, y, \sigma)$ across scale and space. This gives **scale invariance** because features are detected at their characteristic scale.

**Stage 2 — Keypoint Localization:** Low-contrast candidates and edge responses are rejected using a Taylor expansion of $D$ and a Hessian-matrix check for principal curvature ratio. This improves robustness.

**Stage 3 — Orientation Assignment:** A histogram of gradient orientations is computed in a neighborhood of each keypoint. The dominant orientation becomes the *canonical* orientation, and all subsequent descriptors are computed *relative* to it. This gives **rotation invariance**.

**Stage 4 — Descriptor Computation:** A 16×16 neighborhood around the keypoint is divided into 4×4 sub-blocks. In each sub-block, an 8-bin orientation histogram is computed. This yields a 4×4×8 = **128-dimensional descriptor**. Gradients are weighted by a Gaussian and clipped to reduce the effect of large illumination changes, providing **partial illumination invariance**.

> **Distribution of marks (a):** Stages (4×1.25 = 5) + Invariance discussion (2).

**Part (b) — Ratio Test Computation (7 marks):**

Compute ratio for each match:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ratio}(M_1) &= \frac{0.15}{0.55} \approx 0.273 \\
\text{ratio}(M_2) &= \frac{0.22}{0.27} \approx 0.815 \\
\text{ratio}(M_3) &= \frac{0.18}{0.62} \approx 0.290 \\
\text{ratio}(M_4) &= \frac{0.30}{0.48} = 0.625 \\
\text{ratio}(M_5) &= \frac{0.10}{0.40} = 0.250
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply $\tau = 0.75$:

| Match | Ratio | Decision | Reason |
|-------|-------|----------|--------|
| M1    | 0.273 | **Accepted** | Well below $\tau$ |
| M2    | 0.815 | **Rejected** | Above $\tau$ — ambiguous (2nd NN is too close) |
| M3    | 0.290 | **Accepted** | Well below $\tau$ |
| M4    | 0.625 | **Accepted** | Below $\tau$ |
| M5    | 0.250 | **Accepted** | Well below $\tau$ |

**Final accepted set:** {M1, M3, M4, M5} → 4 matches kept, 1 rejected.

> **Distribution of marks (b):** Computation (5×1 = 5) + Tabulated decision with justification (2).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):**
> 1. In part (a), students often *omit Stage 2* (keypoint localization) and directly jump to descriptors — **lose 1.5 marks**.
> 2. In part (b), forgetting to *state the rule* before applying it is a common 1-mark deduction. Always write: *"Lowe's ratio test accepts matches where $d_1/d_2 < 0.75$."*
> 3. Rounding to 2-3 decimal places is expected; excessive precision wastes time.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of an instance recognition system. Identify the role of each block in handling nuisance variables (illumination, viewpoint, occlusion, scale).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two bounding boxes are given: $B_1 = (10, 20, 110, 120)$ and $B_2 = (30, 40, 130, 140)$. Compute the IoU and classify the prediction as True Positive or False Positive at a threshold of 0.5. If a detector returns 80 True Positives, 30 False Positives, and 20 False Negatives, compute the Precision, Recall, and F1-score.

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Instance Recognition Pipeline (7 marks):**

The pipeline consists of the following sequential blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):
1. **Detector Block:** Extracts repeatable keypoints (handles *scale* via multi-scale detection).
2. **Descriptor Block:** Encodes local appearance into an invariant vector (handles *illumination* via gradient normalization, handles *rotation* via canonical orientation).
3. **Matching Block:** Finds candidate correspondences via nearest-neighbor search in descriptor space.
4. **Filtering Block (Lowe's Ratio):** Removes ambiguous matches (handles *repetitive texture*).
5. **Geometric Verification (RANSAC):** Estimates a homography; outliers (e.g., from *occlusion* or *background clutter*) are rejected because they are not spatially consistent.
6. **Decision Block:** The model with the most inliers is the recognized instance; the homography provides *viewpoint* (pose).

> **Distribution of marks (a):** Diagram (3) + Explanation with nuisance variables (4).

**Part (b) — IoU and Metrics (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — IoU calculation.**

Coordinates: $B_1$ top-left $(10, 20)$, bottom-right $(110, 120)$. $B_2$ top-left $(30, 40)$, bottom-right $(130, 140)$.

Width and height of $B_1$: $100 \times 100$. Area: $A_1 = 10{,}000$.

Width and height of $B_2$: $100 \times 100$. Area: $A_2 = 10{,}000$.

Intersection top-left: $(\max(10, 30), \max(20, 40)) = (30, 40)$.

Intersection bottom-right: $(\min(110, 130), \min(120, 140)) = (110, 120)$.

Intersection width: $110 - 30 = 80$. Intersection height: $120 - 40 = 80$. $A_{int} = 6400$.

Union: $A_1 + A_2 - A_{int} = 10000 + 10000 - 6400 = 13600$.

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{6400}{13600} \approx 0.471
$$

Since $0.471 < 0.5$, the prediction is a **FALSE POSITIVE** at the standard threshold.

**Step 2 — Precision, Recall, F1.**

Given: $TP = 80$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 20$.

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{80}{80+30} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.727
$$

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{80}{80+20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.800
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.727 \cdot 0.800}{0.727 + 0.800} = \frac{1.163}{1.527} \approx 0.762
$$

> **Distribution of marks (b):** IoU computation (4) + P/R/F1 (3).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. **Bounding box convention:** KTU follows the $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$ format (top-left, bottom-right). Mixing it with $(x, y, w, h)$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Union formula:** Students often write $A_1 + A_2$ instead of $A_1 + A_2 - A_{int}$, causing the union to be overcounted. Memorize: $\text{Union} = \text{Sum} - \text{Intersection}$.
> 3. **F1 formula:** Do not compute it as $(P+R)/2$ — that is the *arithmetic mean*, not the F1-score. The harmonic mean punishes imbalance between P and R.
> 4. **IoU < 0.5 = FP:** This is a hard rule for PASCAL VOC; some textbooks incorrectly call it a "borderline match".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Instance recognition** is a *closed-world* problem: identify a specific known exemplar among enrolled models.
- It differs from **categorization** (class-level) and **detection** (localize + class).
- The canonical pipeline is: **Detect → Describe → Match → Filter (Lowe's ratio) → Verify (RANSAC + Homography) → Decide (inlier count).**
- **SIFT** is the gold-standard local feature: 128-D, scale + rotation invariant, partially illumination invariant.
- **ORB** is a faster binary alternative (256 bits), uses Hamming distance.
- **Lowe's ratio test** threshold: $\tau \approx 0.7$ to $0.8$; formula: $d_1/d_2 < \tau$.
- **Homography** has **8 DOF**, needs **≥ 4 point correspondences**, solved by SVD for over-determined systems.
- **RANSAC iteration count** formula: $N = \log(1-p) / \log(1-\epsilon^s)$.
- **IoU threshold** for PASCAL VOC: **0.5**; MS-COCO uses mean over 0.5–0.95.
- **Precision** = TP/(TP+FP); **Recall** = TP/(TP+FN); **F1** = harmonic mean.
- **mAP** is the primary metric for ranking detectors; AP is the area under the Precision-Recall curve.
- **Minimum 3 inlier correspondences** are required for a *statistically valid* geometric verification (degenerate case check).
- **Bag of Visual Words (BoVW)** is the classical method to convert local features into a global image representation for instance retrieval.
- **Template matching** fails under scale/rotation changes unless a multi-scale pyramid and rotational search are explicitly added.
- **Deep learning approaches** (Siamese networks, metric learning) currently dominate benchmark performance but require large training datasets.
- **Real-time constraints** (e.g., 30 FPS for AR) usually force a switch from SIFT to ORB or learned keypoint detectors (SuperPoint, R2D2).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Classification - Feature-Based Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Classification - Feature-Based Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Classification** is the task of assigning a semantic label (from a predefined set of classes) to a digital image as a whole, based on its visual content. In the **Feature-Based paradigm**, classification is performed in two decoupled stages:

1. **Feature Extraction Stage** — A numerical *feature vector* $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is computed from the raw pixel array, representing discriminative properties of the image (edges, corners, textures, blobs, regions).
2. **Decision Stage** — A learned classifier $f: \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ maps the feature vector to a discrete class label $y \in \mathcal{Y} = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_K\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Board Examiner expects you to clearly distinguish between *feature detection* (locating interest points) and *feature description* (encoding the local neighbourhood into a descriptor vector). These are *not* the same process.

Formally, the classification pipeline can be written as the composite function:

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; f\!\left(\, \Phi(\mathbf{I}) \,\right)
$$

where $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ is the input image, $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the feature-extraction operator, and $f(\cdot)$ is the trained decision function.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a fruit market blindfolded, and a friend hands you a basket of fruits. You are asked: *"Is this an apple, an orange, or a mango?"* How do you decide?

You do **not** look at every pixel (the fruit's surface detail). Instead, you instinctively measure a few *signature traits*:

- **Color** (red-orange-yellow)
- **Size** (small-medium-large)
- **Skin texture** (smooth, waxy, dimpled)
- **Shape roundness** (round, oval, oblong)

You feed these few numbers to your brain's "internal classifier", which has been trained by past experience. **Feature-based image classification does exactly this**:

| Human Senses | Machine Vision Equivalent | Mathematical Object |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes see pixel detail | Raw image $\mathbf{I}$ | $H \times W \times C$ tensor |
| Brain extracts traits | Feature extractor $\Phi$ | Operator on image |
| Few signature numbers | Feature vector | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| Past experience | Trained classifier | $f: \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ |
| "It is a mango" | Output label | $\hat{y} \in \mathcal{Y}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why features at all?** The *curse of dimensionality* makes it infeasible to classify directly on raw pixels. A $256 \times 256$ grayscale image lives in $\mathbb{R}^{65{,}536}$. A good feature vector reduces this to $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d$ is typically $64$, $128$, or $1024$, while preserving class-discriminative information.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the **canonical performance metrics** the KTU Examiner expects you to know by heart:

- **Euclidean norm:** $\| \mathbf{x} \|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i^{2}}$
- **Pixel intensity range:** $\mathbf{I}(u,v) \in [0, 255]$ for 8-bit images
- **Class-label cardinality:** $K = \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert$ (number of classes)
- **Standard benchmark descriptors:** SIFT = 128-D, SURF = 64-D, HOG = variable (typically 3,780-D for 64×128 window), LBP = configurable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the Examiner asks "What is the dimensionality of a SIFT descriptor?", the correct answer is **128**. Memorize this; it is a frequently-repeated KTU Part-A question.

---

### 1.4 The KTU Classification Pipeline (High-Level)

The Feature-Based Classification pipeline has **four canonical blocks**:

1. **Image Acquisition** $\rightarrow$ digitised matrix $\mathbf{I}$
2. **Pre-processing** $\rightarrow$ noise removal, normalisation, resizing
3. **Feature Extraction + Description** $\rightarrow$ vector $\mathbf{x}$
4. **Classification** $\rightarrow$ predicted label $\hat{y}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus mapping (KTU 2024 OERUT834 — Module 4):** This topic covers feature detectors (Harris, SIFT, SURF), descriptors (SIFT, HOG, LBP, Gabor), the Bag-of-Visual-Words (BoVW) model, and classical classifiers (Nearest Neighbour, Bayes, SVM).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Features

Features used for classification are organised along two orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — Spatial Scope:**

- **Global Features** describe the *entire image* with one vector (e.g., colour histogram, GIST). They are compact but brittle to clutter, occlusion, and viewpoint change.
- **Local Features** describe *small neighbourhoods* around detected keypoints (e.g., SIFT, SURF). They are robust to occlusion and clutter but require aggregation.

**Axis 2 — Invariance Properties (the "Why" of Feature Design):**

- **Rotation Invariance:** Descriptor value is unchanged when image is rotated.
- **Scale Invariance:** Descriptor value is unchanged when image is rescaled.
- **Illumination Invariance:** Descriptor value is unchanged under brightness/contrast shifts.
- **Affine Invariance:** Invariant to skew and aspect-ratio changes.
- **Translation Invariance:** Invariant to shifts in image position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these matter in the KTU paper:** Questions on "compare SIFT and Harris corner detector" or "justify why SIFT is preferred over raw pixel matching" are *invariance-comparison* questions. Knowing what each detector is invariant to is half the battle.

---

### 2.2 Feature Detectors (Locating "Where")

A **detector** answers: *"At which pixel coordinates $(u,v)$ does something interesting happen?"*

#### 2.2.1 Harris Corner Detector
The Harris response at a point is based on the second-moment (structure) tensor:

$$
\mathbf{M} \;=\; \sum_{(x,y) \in W} \nabla \mathbf{I}(x,y)\, \nabla \mathbf{I}(x,y)^{\!\top}
$$

where $\nabla \mathbf{I} = \left[\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{I}}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{I}}{\partial y}\right]^{\!\top}$ is the image gradient and $W$ is a local window.

The **Harris corner response** is:

$$
R \;=\; \det(\mathbf{M}) \;-\; k \cdot \bigl(\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{M})\bigr)^{2} \;=\; \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \;-\; k\,(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^{2}
$$

where $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are eigenvalues of $\mathbf{M}$ and $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ is an empirical constant.

- $R > 0$ and large $\Rightarrow$ **Corner**
- $R < 0$ and large magnitude $\Rightarrow$ **Edge**
- $\vert R \vert$ small $\Rightarrow$ **Flat region**

**Why this works:** At a corner, gradients in *multiple directions* exist, so both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are large, making $\det(\mathbf{M})$ large and $R$ large. Harris is **rotation invariant but NOT scale invariant**.

#### 2.2.2 Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) Keypoint Detection
SIFT finds keypoints that are repeatable across scales by searching the **Difference-of-Gaussian (DoG)** scale space:

$$
D(u, v, \sigma) \;=\; L(u, v, k\sigma) \;-\; L(u, v, \sigma)
$$

where $L(u,v,\sigma) = G(u,v,\sigma) \ast \mathbf{I}$ is the scale-space representation built by convolving with a Gaussian kernel.

Keypoint localisation finds extrema of $D(u,v,\sigma)$ in 3D space (2D location + 1D scale). SIFT descriptors are then computed as a **128-D histogram of oriented gradients** in a $16 \times 16$ neighbourhood, divided into $4 \times 4$ cells of $4 \times 4$ pixels, each cell contributing an 8-bin orientation histogram.

#### 2.2.3 SURF (Speeded-Up Robust Features)
SURF uses **box filters** to approximate the Hessian determinant at multiple scales, achieving faster computation than SIFT while retaining comparable robustness. The descriptor is a **64-D vector** of Haar-wavelet responses.

#### 2.2.4 FAST and ORB
- **FAST (Features from Accelerated Segment Test):** Compares a circle of 16 pixels around a candidate to a threshold. Extremely fast, suitable for real-time.
- **ORB (Oriented FAST + Rotated BRIEF):** Adds orientation to FAST and uses a rotation-aware version of the BRIEF binary descriptor.

---

### 2.3 Feature Descriptors (Encoding "What")

A **descriptor** answers: *"What does the local neighbourhood around $(u,v)$ look like, as a vector?"*

| Descriptor | Dim | Invariance | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw pixel patch | $n^{2}$ | None | Baseline |
| SIFT | **128** | Rotation, Scale, Illumination | Object recognition |
| SURF | **64** | Rotation, Scale, Illumination | Real-time matching |
| HOG | Variable | Illumination, small rotation | Pedestrian detection |
| LBP | $2^{P}$ where $P$ = neighbours | Monotonic gray-scale | Texture classification |
| Gabor filter bank | Variable | Limited | Texture / fingerprint |
| BRIEF / ORB | 128–512 bits | Rotation (ORB) | Real-time SLAM |

#### 2.3.1 Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)
Steps:
1. Compute gradients $G_x, G_y$ via $[-1, 0, +1]$ kernels.
2. Compute magnitude $m(u,v) = \sqrt{G_x^{2} + G_y^{2}}$ and orientation $\theta(u,v) = \arctan\!\bigl(\tfrac{G_y}{G_x}\bigr)$.
3. Divide image into cells (e.g., $8 \times 8$ pixels).
4. Build a 9-bin orientation histogram per cell, weighted by gradient magnitude.
5. Group cells into blocks (e.g., $2 \times 2$ cells) and $L_2$-normalise the concatenated histogram.
6. Concatenate all block vectors into the final HOG descriptor.

#### 2.3.2 Local Binary Pattern (LBP)
For each pixel, compare it with $P$ neighbours on a circle of radius $R$:

$$
\mathrm{LBP}_{P,R} \;=\; \sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s\!\bigl(i_p - i_c\bigr) \cdot 2^{p}, \qquad s(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z \ge 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}
$$

The histogram of LBP codes over a region forms the texture descriptor.

---

### 2.4 Bag of Visual Words (BoVW)

The BoVW model adapts the text-domain "Bag of Words" idea to images. It is the **standard pipeline** the KTU syllabus expects you to draw and explain.

**Steps:**
1. Extract local feature descriptors (e.g., SIFT) from a training set of images — you get $N$ descriptor vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$.
2. **Cluster** these $N$ vectors into $K$ clusters using **k-Means** — the $K$ cluster centroids are the **visual words** (codebook).
3. For any new image:
   - Extract its local descriptors.
   - For each descriptor, find the **nearest** codebook word (via Euclidean distance).
   - Build a **histogram of $K$ bins**, where bin $k$ counts how many descriptors of this image are closest to word $k$.
   - $L_1$ or $L_2$-normalise the histogram.
4. Feed the histogram vector into a classifier (SVM is the most common choice in KTU answers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The histogram is built over the *whole image* and is therefore a *global* feature. BoVW is *not* rotation- or scale-invariant per se, but the underlying SIFT features are.

---

### 2.5 Classical Classifiers (The Decision Stage)

#### 2.5.1 k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN)
The class of a query feature vector $\mathbf{x}_q$ is the **majority vote** among its $k$ nearest training samples in feature space.

Distance metrics:
- **Euclidean:** $d_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i - y_i)^{2}}$
- **Manhattan / City-block:** $d_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$
- **Mahalanobis:** $d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})^{\!\top} \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})}$ where $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is the class covariance matrix.
- **Cosine:** $d_C(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 1 - \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\| \mathbf{x} \|_2 \,\| \mathbf{y} \|_2}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mahalanobis?** It accounts for correlated features and scale differences, unlike Euclidean. This is a frequent 7-mark "explain with diagram" KTU question.

#### 2.5.2 Bayes (Minimum-Risk) Classifier
For class $c_j$, the decision rule that minimises probability of error is:

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; \underset{c_j \in \mathcal{Y}}{\arg\max}\; P(c_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \;=\; \underset{c_j \in \mathcal{Y}}{\arg\max}\; P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j)\, P(c_j)
$$

where $P(c_j)$ is the class prior and $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j)$ is the class-conditional density. With Gaussian class-conditional:

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j) \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_j \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^{\!\top} \mathbf{\Sigma}_j^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) \right)
$$

#### 2.5.3 Support Vector Machine (SVM)
For a binary linearly-separable problem, SVM finds the hyperplane that **maximises the margin** between two classes. Primal form:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b}\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \| \mathbf{w} \|_2^{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1,\; \forall i
$$

Soft-margin (non-separable) form with slack variables $\xi_i \ge 0$ and penalty $C$:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}}\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \| \mathbf{w} \|_2^{2} \;+\; C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i
$$

The **kernel trick** maps data into a higher-dimensional space: $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\!\top} \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$. Common kernels:
- Linear: $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_j$
- Polynomial: $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma\, \mathbf{x}_i^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_j + r)^{d}$
- RBF / Gaussian: $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \| \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \|_2^{2})$

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Default Value / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Harris response | $R = \lambda_1\lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)^{2}$ | $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ | Corner if $R > R_{\text{th}}$ |
| SIFT scale space | $D(u,v,\sigma) = L(u,v,k\sigma) - L(u,v,\sigma)$ | $k = 2^{1/s}$ per octave | $s$ = levels per octave |
| SIFT descriptor | 4×4 cells × 8 orientations | **128-D** | Gradient histogram |
| SURF descriptor | 4×4 cells × 4 sums | **64-D** | Haar-wavelet response |
| HOG cell | 8×8 pixels, 9 orientation bins | 9-D per cell | $L_2$-norm over block |
| LBP$_{P,R}$ | $\sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(i_p - i_c) \cdot 2^{p}$ | $P=8, R=1$ typical | Texture |
| k-NN decision | $\arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}[y_i = c]$ | $k$ odd, $k>1$ | Majority vote |
| Mahalanobis | $\sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^{\!\top} \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}$ | $\mathbf{\Sigma} \succ 0$ | Scale-aware |
| Bayes decision | $\arg\max_{c_j} P(\mathbf{x}\vert c_j) P(c_j)$ | Sum of priors $=1$ | Min-error rule |
| SVM margin | $\frac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2}$ | Maximise | Dual: $\alpha_i > 0$ = support vectors |
| BoVW histogram | $h_k = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[z_j = v_k]$ | $K$ bins | $L_1$ or $L_2$ normalised |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** In the SVM margin formula, students write $\tfrac{1}{\| \mathbf{w} \|}$ or forget the factor of 2. The canonical form is $\tfrac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2}$ for the *full* margin between the two parallel canonical hyperplanes.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Feature-Based Classifier Used | Industrial Product |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | HOG + SVM | Pedestrian detection (Daimler, INRIA datasets) |
| Medical Imaging | LBP + SVM | Tumour classification on mammograms |
| Quality Control | SIFT + k-NN | PCB defect detection |
| Biometrics | Gabor + cosine distance | Fingerprint / Iris recognition |
| Remote Sensing | BoVW + SVM | Land-cover classification from satellite |
| Document Analysis | HOG + MLP | Handwritten character recognition (MNIST) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Harris Detector Intuition to Response Function

**Why:** We want a single scalar $R(u,v)$ that is *large at corners* and *small in flat regions / along edges*, using only image gradients.

**Step 1 — Local image patches shift by $[u,v]$ produce an intensity change:**

$$
E(u,v) \;=\; \sum_{(x,y) \in W} \bigl[\, \mathbf{I}(x+u,\, y+v) \;-\; \mathbf{I}(x,y) \,\bigr]^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — First-order Taylor expansion** of $\mathbf{I}(x+u, y+v)$ around $(x,y)$:

$$
\mathbf{I}(x+u, y+v) \;\approx\; \mathbf{I}(x,y) \;+\; u\, \mathbf{I}_x(x,y) \;+\; v\, \mathbf{I}_y(x,y)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $E(u,v)$:**

$$
E(u,v) \;\approx\; \sum_{(x,y) \in W} \bigl[\, u\, \mathbf{I}_x \;+\; v\, \mathbf{I}_y \,\bigr]^{2} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{M} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the structure tensor $\mathbf{M}$:**

$$
\mathbf{M} \;=\; \sum_{(x,y) \in W} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}_x^{2} & \mathbf{I}_x \mathbf{I}_y \\ \mathbf{I}_x \mathbf{I}_y & \mathbf{I}_y^{2} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \langle \mathbf{I}_x^{2} \rangle & \langle \mathbf{I}_x \mathbf{I}_y \rangle \\ \langle \mathbf{I}_x \mathbf{I}_y \rangle & \langle \mathbf{I}_y^{2} \rangle \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denotes windowed summation.

**Step 5 — Diagonalise $\mathbf{M}$** to find principal curvatures: $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{R}^{-\!\top}\, \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)\, \mathbf{R}^{-1}$.

**Step 6 — Avoid explicit eigen-decomposition** (expensive) by using the *trace* and *determinant* invariants:

$$
\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{M}) \;=\; \lambda_1 + \lambda_2, \qquad \det(\mathbf{M}) \;=\; \lambda_1 \lambda_2
$$

**Step 7 — Form the response** so that:
- Corner $\Rightarrow$ both $\lambda$ large $\Rightarrow$ $\det$ large, $\mathrm{tr}^{2}$ moderate
- Edge $\Rightarrow$ one $\lambda$ large, one small $\Rightarrow$ $\det$ small, $\mathrm{tr}^{2}$ large
- Flat $\Rightarrow$ both $\lambda$ small $\Rightarrow$ $\det$ small, $\mathrm{tr}^{2}$ small

The canonical **Harris** choice is:

$$
R \;=\; \det(\mathbf{M}) \;-\; k\,\bigl(\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{M})\bigr)^{2}
$$

A common alternative is the **Shi-Tomasi (Good Features to Track)** response:

$$
R_{\text{ST}} \;=\; \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)
$$

which directly uses the smaller eigenvalue.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bayes Decision Rule from Probability of Error

**Why:** We want a decision rule that is *optimal* under a 0-1 loss (i.e., minimise the probability of misclassification).

**Step 1 — Probability of error for a decision rule $d(\mathbf{x})$:**

$$
P(\text{error}) \;=\; \int P\bigl(d(\mathbf{x}) \ne y \mid \mathbf{x}\bigr)\, P(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} \;=\; \int \bigl[\,1 - P\bigl(d(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x}\bigr)\,\bigr]\, P(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 2 — Since $P(\mathbf{x}) \ge 0$ and $\int P(\mathbf{x})d\mathbf{x} = 1$,** minimising the integral reduces to **pointwise minimisation** of $1 - P(d(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x})$, equivalently **maximising** $P(d(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x})$.

**Step 3 — Expand via total probability:**

$$
P(c_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \;=\; \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j)\, P(c_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is the same for all $j$, the maximiser depends only on the numerator:

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; \underset{c_j}{\arg\max}\; P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j)\, P(c_j)
$$

**Step 4 — For multivariate Gaussian class-conditional:**

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j) \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_j \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^{\!\top} \mathbf{\Sigma}_j^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Take logarithm** (monotonic, simplifies product to sum) and drop constants independent of $j$:

$$
g_j(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2} \ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_j \vert \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^{\!\top} \mathbf{\Sigma}_j^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) \;+\; \ln P(c_j)
$$

The Bayes decision is:

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; \underset{j}{\arg\max}\; g_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When the covariances are equal** ($\mathbf{\Sigma}_j = \mathbf{\Sigma}$ for all $j$), the Bayes classifier becomes **linear** in $\mathbf{x}$ — the decision boundaries are hyperplanes. This is the well-known *Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)* case.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: 2-Class Bayes with 1-D Gaussian

**Given:**
- Class 1: $\mu_1 = 2$, $\sigma_1^{2} = 1$, $P(c_1) = 0.6$
- Class 2: $\mu_2 = 4$, $\sigma_2^{2} = 2$, $P(c_2) = 0.4$

**Question:** Classify $\mathbf{x} = 3.0$ using Bayes minimum-error rule.

**Step 1 — Compute $P(\mathbf{x}=3 \mid c_1)$:**

$$
P(3 \mid c_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 1}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(3-2)^{2}}{2 \cdot 1} \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-0.5} \approx 0.2420
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(\mathbf{x}=3 \mid c_2)$:**

$$
P(3 \mid c_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(3-4)^{2}}{2 \cdot 2} \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi}}\, e^{-0.25} \approx 0.1210
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by priors (posteriors up to a constant):**

$$
P(c_1)\, P(3 \mid c_1) \;=\; 0.6 \times 0.2420 \;=\; 0.1452
$$

$$
P(c_2)\, P(3 \mid c_2) \;=\; 0.4 \times 0.1210 \;=\; 0.0484
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
0.1452 \;>\; 0.0484 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{y} = c_1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU answer-format tip:** Always show *all four* steps in this order: Gaussian likelihood → prior multiplication → comparison → decision. Examiners award partial marks for the correct setup even if arithmetic slips.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: k-NN Decision

**Given:** 5 training samples in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$:

| Sample | $\mathbf{x}$ | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $(1, 1)$ | A |
| $s_2$ | $(2, 2)$ | A |
| $s_3$ | $(3, 1)$ | B |
| $s_4$ | $(4, 3)$ | B |
| $s_5$ | $(5, 4)$ | A |

**Query point:** $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 3)$. Classify using $k = 3$ and Euclidean distance.

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distances:**

$$
d(s_1, q) = \sqrt{(1-2)^{2} + (1-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236
$$

$$
d(s_2, q) = \sqrt{(2-2)^{2} + (2-3)^{2}} = 1
$$

$$
d(s_3, q) = \sqrt{(3-2)^{2} + (1-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236
$$

$$
d(s_4, q) = \sqrt{(4-2)^{2} + (3-3)^{2}} = 2
$$

$$
d(s_5, q) = \sqrt{(5-2)^{2} + (4-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162
$$

**Step 2 — Sort and pick 3 nearest:** $s_2$ (A, 1.0), $s_4$ (B, 2.0), $s_1$ (A, 2.236) / $s_3$ (B, 2.236) — tie. Break tie deterministically: pick $s_1$.

**Step 3 — Majority vote:** A : B = 2 : 1 $\Rightarrow$ $\hat{y} = A$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: SVM Margin Calculation

**Given:** 2-D feature vectors with two support vectors $\mathbf{x}_+ = (1, 1)$ from class $+1$ and $\mathbf{x}_- = (-1, -1)$ from class $-1$.

**Step 1 — Hyperplane normal direction** (perpendicular to the line connecting support vectors, pointing from $-$ to $+$):

$$
\mathbf{w} \;\propto\; \mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_- \;=\; (2, 2) \;\propto\; (1, 1)
$$

**Step 2 — Set $\mathbf{w} = (1, 1)$** and find $b$ such that both support vectors lie exactly on the margin:

$$
y_i (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \;=\; 1
$$

For $\mathbf{x}_+ = (1,1)$ with $y_+ = +1$:

$$
(1)(1 + 1 + b) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad b = -1
$$

For $\mathbf{x}_- = (-1,-1)$ with $y_- = -1$:

$$
(-1)(-1 - 1 - 1) = (-1)(-3) = 3 \;\ne\; 1 \quad \text{(inconsistent)}
$$

**Step 3 — Re-scale $\mathbf{w}$.** Let $\mathbf{w} = \alpha(1,1)$. Margin constraints:

$$
\alpha(1+1) + b = 1, \quad -\alpha(-1-1) + b = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 2\alpha + b = 1, \quad 2\alpha - b = 1
$$

Adding: $4\alpha = 2 \Rightarrow \alpha = 0.5$. Subtracting: $2b = 0 \Rightarrow b = 0$.

**Step 4 — Final hyperplane and margin:**

$$
\mathbf{w} = (0.5, 0.5), \quad b = 0, \quad \text{margin} = \frac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{0.5}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
$$

**Step 5 — Decision function:**

$$
\hat{y} = \mathrm{sign}\bigl(\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x} + b\bigr) = \mathrm{sign}(0.5\, x_1 + 0.5\, x_2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this fits:** A complete KTU answer to a "design a classifier" question should also include *evaluation*. The diagram above shows how predicted vs true labels flow into the standard $K \times K$ confusion matrix from which **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy** are computed.

For a binary problem ($K = 2$), the four cells are:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive ($TP$) | False Negative ($FN$) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive ($FP$) | True Negative ($TN$) |

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}, \quad \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$
