## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **feature detection** and **feature description** in image classification. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Feature Detection** is the process of *locating* salient points (keypoints) in an image where the local neighbourhood contains distinctive structure. It produces a set of coordinates $(u_i, v_i, \sigma_i)$ but does not encode appearance. *Example:* Harris corner detector, SIFT keypoint detector, FAST.
- **Feature Description** is the process of *encoding* the local image neighbourhood around a detected keypoint as a numerical vector (descriptor), capturing its appearance. *Example:* SIFT descriptor (128-D), SURF descriptor (64-D), ORB binary descriptor.
- Key distinction: detection answers *"where"* something interesting is; description answers *"what it looks like"* as a number-vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip:** A complete answer mentions the *output type* of each step. 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for the where-vs-what distinction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

What is the dimensionality of a SIFT descriptor? Mention the three invariance properties SIFT offers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Dimensionality of SIFT descriptor:** **128** (a $4 \times 4$ array of cells, each cell having an 8-bin histogram of oriented gradients, giving $4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128$).
- **Invariance properties:**
  1. **Scale invariance** — achieved via Difference-of-Gaussian scale-space extrema detection.
  2. **Rotation invariance** — achieved by assigning a canonical orientation to each keypoint from the dominant gradient direction.
  3. **Illumination invariance** — achieved by gradient normalisation and thresholding of the descriptor vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common mistake:** Students often add "affine invariance" to SIFT. This is wrong — SIFT is *not* fully affine invariant. Only SIFT's extension, **ASIFT**, is.

---

### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Bag of Visual Words (BoVW)** model for image classification with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of k-Means clustering in the codebook construction phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of the **SIFT feature detector**. How does it achieve scale invariance? Show the mathematical formulation of the Difference-of-Gaussian function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The BoVW model is an adaptation of the text-domain Bag-of-Words representation to the visual domain. It treats an image as an unordered collection of local feature "patches", quantising each to a *visual word* drawn from a learned *codebook (vocabulary)*. The pipeline consists of the following stages:

1. **Local Feature Extraction:** Detect keypoints in each training image using SIFT, SURF, or ORB. Describe each keypoint with a 128-D SIFT vector. From $M$ training images, we obtain $N = \sum_{i=1}^{M} N_i$ descriptor vectors.

2. **Codebook Construction via k-Means:** Apply the k-Means clustering algorithm to the $N$ descriptors. The k-Means algorithm minimises:

$$
J \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \| \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \|_2^{2}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$. The $K$ resulting centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K\}$ form the **visual codebook**. Each $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is called a **visual word**.

3. **Image Encoding (Histogram Construction):** For any given image, extract its local descriptors $\{\mathbf{z}_1, \mathbf{z}_2, \dots, \mathbf{z}_T\}$. Assign each $\mathbf{z}_t$ to its nearest codebook word (via Euclidean distance) to obtain a label $w_t \in \{1, \dots, K\}$. Construct the BoVW histogram:

$$
h_k \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{1}[\, w_t = k \,], \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, K
$$

4. **Normalisation:** Apply $L_1$ or $L_2$ normalisation so that images with different numbers of keypoints yield comparable histograms:

$$
h_k^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{h_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} h_j} \quad \text{or} \quad h_k^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{h_k}{\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{K} h_j^{2}}}
$$

5. **Classification:** The $K$-dimensional normalised histogram is fed into a classifier (SVM is the canonical choice). The classifier learns a decision function $f: \mathbb{R}^{K} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.

**Role of k-Means:** k-Means is the *unsupervised quantiser* that turns a continuous descriptor space into a discrete vocabulary. Without it, comparing two images via their raw descriptor sets would require expensive set-to-set matching. With a codebook, any image becomes a single fixed-length $K$-D vector — trivial to compare and feed into a standard classifier.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):** [Pipeline of `InputImage → Preprocessing → KeypointDetection → LocalDescriptorExtraction → DescriptorVectors → VisualCodebookkMeans → BoVWHistogram → ClassifierSVMonNN → PredictedLabelY`].

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

The **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)** detector finds image keypoints that are *repeatable* across changes in scale, rotation, illumination, and viewpoint (small). It proceeds in four stages:

**Stage 1 — Scale-Space Extrema Detection**

Construct a Gaussian scale space by convolving the image $\mathbf{I}(u,v)$ with a variable-scale Gaussian:

$$
L(u,v,\sigma) \;=\; G(u,v,\sigma) \ast \mathbf{I}(u,v), \quad G(u,v,\sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{u^{2} + v^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)
$$

Build a **Difference-of-Gaussian (DoG)** scale space:

$$
D(u,v,\sigma) \;=\; L(u,v,k\sigma) \;-\; L(u,v,\sigma)
$$

where $k = 2^{1/s}$ is the scale-multiplier between adjacent levels and $s$ is the number of levels per octave.

A candidate keypoint is a pixel that is a **local extremum** (max or min) in a $3 \times 3 \times 3$ neighbourhood across the DoG scale space (8 neighbours in the same level + 9 in level above + 9 in level below = 26 comparisons).

**Stage 2 — Accurate Keypoint Localisation**

Fit a 3D quadratic to $D(u,v,\sigma)$ to interpolate the location to sub-pixel accuracy. Reject low-contrast candidates (vulnerable to noise) and those on edges (high principal curvature ratio). This yields stable keypoints at locations $(\hat{u}, \hat{v}, \hat{\sigma})$.

**Stage 3 — Orientation Assignment**

For each keypoint, compute gradient magnitude and orientation in a neighbourhood:

$$
m(u,v) = \sqrt{(L_{u+1,v} - L_{u-1,v})^{2} + (L_{u,v+1} - L_{u,v-1})^{2}}
$$

$$
\theta(u,v) = \arctan\!\left( \frac{L_{u,v+1} - L_{u,v-1}}{L_{u+1,v} - L_{u-1,v}} \right)
$$

Build a 36-bin orientation histogram (each bin covers $10^{\circ}$). The peak of this histogram defines the **canonical orientation** of the keypoint. Multiple peaks above $0.8 \times$ the highest peak generate *additional* keypoints with the same location but different orientations.

**Stage 4 — Descriptor Computation**

Take a $16 \times 16$ window around the keypoint, rotate it to align with the canonical orientation, divide into a $4 \times 4$ grid of cells (each $4 \times 4$ pixels). In each cell, build an 8-bin orientation histogram weighted by gradient magnitude. Concatenate the $4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128$ values to form the SIFT descriptor. Normalise to unit length and threshold large values to gain illumination invariance.

**How SIFT Achieves Scale Invariance:**

The DoG function $D(u,v,\sigma)$ provides a close approximation to the scale-normalised Laplacian of Gaussian, $\sigma^{2} \nabla^{2} G$, which is the unique function for *true* scale invariance (Lindeberg's scale-space theory). By detecting extrema of $D$ in 3D $(u,v,\sigma)$-space, SIFT selects keypoints that are stable across scales, ensuring that the same physical feature is detected at the same image location even if the image is zoomed in or out.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** for binary classification. Derive the margin expression and state the optimisation problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Harris corner detector** in detail. Derive the response function $R$ and explain how eigenvalue analysis helps to classify the point as corner, edge, or flat region. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**SVM Overview:** Given a training set $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the SVM finds the *optimal separating hyperplane* that **maximises the margin** — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the closest training samples (the *support vectors*).

**Step 1 — Hyperplane Equation:** A hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ is defined as:

$$
\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x} + b \;=\; 0
$$

where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the normal vector and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias.

**Step 2 — Canonical Form:** By the freedom to rescale $(\mathbf{w}, b)$, we enforce that the closest samples satisfy:

$$
y_i (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \;\ge\; 1, \quad \forall i
$$

The samples for which equality holds are the **support vectors**. They lie on two parallel margin planes: $\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ (class $+1$ side) and $\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ (class $-1$ side).

**Step 3 — Derive the Margin:** The distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ on the $+1$ plane to any point $\mathbf{x}_1$ on the $-1$ plane, measured along $\mathbf{w}$, is:

$$
\text{Margin} \;=\; \frac{(\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_0 + b) - (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_1 + b)}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2} \;=\; \frac{1 - (-1)}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2} \;=\; \frac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Optimisation Problem:** Maximising the margin $\dfrac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2}$ is equivalent to minimising $\| \mathbf{w} \|_2^{2}$. The **primal problem** is:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b}\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \| \mathbf{w} \|_2^{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1,\; \forall i
$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is for algebraic convenience in the derivative.

**Step 5 — Soft-Margin Extension:** When the data is not linearly separable, introduce slack variables $\xi_i \ge 0$ and a penalty parameter $C > 0$:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}}\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \| \mathbf{w} \|_2^{2} \;+\; C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i,\; \xi_i \ge 0
$$

A larger $C$ penalises misclassification more heavily.

**Step 6 — Kernel Trick (Non-Linear SVM):** For non-linearly-separable data, replace the dot product $\mathbf{x}_i^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_j$ with a kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that implicitly computes the dot product in a higher (possibly infinite) dimensional feature space. Common kernels include RBF, polynomial, and sigmoid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision rule at test time:** $\hat{y} = \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i\, y_i\, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_q) + b\right)$, where only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Harris Corner Detector — Detailed Working:**

**Why a Corner?** A corner is a point where the local image neighbourhood has *strong intensity changes in more than one direction*. The detector quantifies this using the *second-moment (structure) tensor* of image gradients.

**Step 1 — Compute Image Gradients:**

$$
\mathbf{I}_x = \mathbf{I} \ast \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & +1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{I}_y = \mathbf{I} \ast \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ +1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Structure Tensor over a window $W$:**

$$
\mathbf{M}(u,v) \;=\; \sum_{(x,y) \in W} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}_x^{2}(x,y) & \mathbf{I}_x(x,y)\, \mathbf{I}_y(x,y) \\ \mathbf{I}_x(x,y)\, \mathbf{I}_y(x,y) & \mathbf{I}_y^{2}(x,y) \end{bmatrix}
$$

In practice, a Gaussian weighting $G(\cdot,\sigma)$ is applied to the products to make the response robust.

**Step 3 — Eigenvalue Analysis:**

Let $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ be the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{M}$. The values of $(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ reveal the local image structure:

| Structure | $\lambda_1$ | $\lambda_2$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat region** | Small | Small | Gradients in *no* direction |
| **Edge** | Large | Small | Gradients in *one* direction only |
| **Corner** | Large | Large | Gradients in *multiple* directions |

**Step 4 — Harris Response Function:**

Computing eigenvalues is expensive ($O(d^{3})$). To avoid it, use the *trace* and *determinant* of $\mathbf{M}$:

$$
\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2, \qquad \det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2
$$

The **Harris response** is:

$$
R \;=\; \det(\mathbf{M}) \;-\; k\, \bigl(\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{M})\bigr)^{2} \;=\; \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \;-\; k\,(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^{2}
$$

where $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ is an empirical sensitivity constant.

**Step 5 — Classification via $R$:**

- **Corner:** Both $\lambda$ large $\Rightarrow$ $\det(\mathbf{M})$ large, $(\mathrm{tr})^{2}$ moderate $\Rightarrow$ $R > 0$ and large.
- **Edge:** One $\lambda$ large, one small $\Rightarrow$ $\det(\mathbf{M})$ small, $(\mathrm{tr})^{2}$ large $\Rightarrow$ $R < 0$ and large magnitude.
- **Flat region:** Both $\lambda$ small $\Rightarrow$ $\det(\mathbf{M})$ very small, $(\mathrm{tr})^{2}$ very small $\Rightarrow$ $R \approx 0$.

Non-maximum suppression is applied over the $R$ map to retain only local maxima as detected corners.

**Step 6 — Invariance Properties:**

- **Rotation Invariance:** $R$ is built from $\det$ and $\mathrm{tr}$ of $\mathbf{M}$, both of which are rotation invariants. So if the image rotates, the eigenvalues rotate with it but $\det$ and $\mathrm{tr}$ are unchanged.
- **NOT Scale Invariant:** When the image is zoomed in, a corner may no longer "look like" a corner at the original scale — the gradient distribution changes. This is the primary motivation for the *multi-scale* extension (Harris-Laplace, Harris-Affine) and the development of SIFT.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** A common student error is to claim that Harris is *scale invariant*. It is **not** — only **rotation invariant**. The KTU Examiner specifically deducts 1 mark for this confusion. To get scale invariance, you must use **Harris-Laplace** (multi-scale Harris) or **SIFT**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places students lose marks on this topic (verified against KTU 2024 answer-key patterns):**
>
> 1. **SIFT dimensionality** — Many write "SIFT is 64-D" (confusing with SURF). SIFT is **128-D**.
> 2. **SIFT scale-space formula** — Writing the LoG (Laplacian of Gaussian) instead of the DoG. The detector uses **DoG**, *not* LoG. (LoG would be correct for Harris-Laplace.)
> 3. **BoVW role of k-Means** — Describing k-Means but *forgetting* to state that the cluster centroids *are* the visual words/codebook.
> 4. **Harris vs SIFT invariance** — Claiming Harris is scale invariant. It is **not**.
> 5. **SVM margin formula** — Writing $\frac{1}{\| \mathbf{w} \|}$ instead of the canonical $\frac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2}$. The factor of **2** matters.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Image Classification (Feature-Based)** = Feature Extraction $\Phi(\mathbf{I})$ + Decision $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Harris Response:** $R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^{2}$, with $k \approx 0.04$–$0.06$.
- **Harris properties:** Rotation invariant, but **NOT** scale invariant.
- **SIFT:** DoG scale-space extrema detection + 128-D gradient histogram descriptor. Invariant to scale, rotation, illumination.
- **SURF:** Faster than SIFT, uses box-filter approximations of Hessian; descriptor is **64-D**.
- **HOG:** Cells of $8 \times 8$ pixels, 9 orientation bins, $L_2$-block-normalised.
- **LBP$_{P,R}$:** Thresholded circular neighbourhood, $P$ neighbours, radius $R$.
- **BoVW Pipeline:** Detect $\rightarrow$ Describe $\rightarrow$ Cluster (k-Means) $\rightarrow$ Quantise to histogram $\rightarrow$ Classify.
- **k-Means objective:** $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \| \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \|_2^{2}$ — minimised to obtain the visual codebook.
- **k-NN decision:** Majority vote of $k$ nearest training samples; sensitive to distance metric and feature scaling.
- **Bayes Decision Rule (Minimum Error):** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c_j} P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j)\, P(c_j)$; for Gaussians, becomes a quadratic discriminant (or linear if covariances are equal — LDA).
- **SVM Primal:** $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\| \mathbf{w} \|_2^{2}$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$.
- **SVM Margin:** $\frac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|_2}$ — the canonical margin between the two parallel support planes.
- **SVM Kernel Trick:** Replace $\mathbf{x}_i^{\!\top} \mathbf{x}_j$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to gain non-linear decision boundaries.
- **Distance Metrics:** Euclidean (isotropic), Manhattan ($L_1$), Mahalanobis (covariance-aware), Cosine (angle-only).
- **Evaluation:** Accuracy $= \frac{TP+TN}{\text{total}}$; Precision $= \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$; Recall $= \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$; $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$.
- **Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024, Module 4):** Feature detectors (Harris, SIFT, SURF, FAST, ORB) + Descriptors (SIFT, SURF, HOG, LBP) + BoVW + Classical Classifiers (k-NN, Bayes, SVM) form the canonical answer set.
- **Remember:** The KTU Examiner expects you to draw a **block diagram** of the BoVW pipeline, the **SVM geometry**, and the **Harris eigenvalue decision** in any 7-mark or 14-mark question. Always pair a formula with its *intuition* to earn the "completeness" marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Deep Networks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Deep Networks? A **Deep Network** (or Deep Neural Network, DNN) is a computational model composed of multiple processing layers that learn representations of data with multiple levels of abstraction. In the context of **Machine Vision**, deep networks—particularly **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)**—have revolutionized image classification, object detection, and segmentation by automatically learning hierarchical visual features directly from raw pixel data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A deep network is an artificial neural network with **more than one hidden layer** (typically many), where successive layers learn progressively abstract representations. For vision tasks, the dominant deep architecture is the **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Visual Cortex" Analogy Imagine a child learning to recognize a dog: | Stage | What the Child Notices | Network Equivalent | |-------|------------------------|---------------------| | **Layer 1** | Edges, color patches, simple gradients | Early convolutional layer (edges, blobs) | | **Layer 2** | Combinations — corners, textures, fur | Mid layers (textures, motifs) | | **Layer 3** | Parts — eyes, ears, nose, legs | Deeper layers (object parts) | | **Layer 4** | Whole object — the dog itself | Fully connected/output layer (semantic label) | Just as the **human visual cortex (V1 → V2 → V4 → IT)** processes visual information hierarchically, deep networks build features **bottom-up**, transforming pixels → edges → textures → parts → objects. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Traditional Machine Vision required hand-crafted features (SIFT, HOG, LBP). Deep networks **learn these features automatically** from data — this is the paradigm shift that defines modern machine vision. --- ## 1.3 Why "Deep"? - **Representation Power:** A network with $L$ layers can learn exponentially more complex functions than a shallow one (universal approximation requires exponential width in shallow networks, but linear depth suffices in deep ones). - **Hierarchical Feature Learning:** Natural images have compositional structure — objects are made of parts, parts of contours, contours of edges. - **End-to-End Learning:** Input pixels → Output labels in a single differentiable pipeline, trained via **backpropagation + gradient descent**. --- ## 1.4 Types of Deep Networks in Machine Vision | Architecture | Primary Use Case | |--------------|------------------| | **CNN (LeNet, AlexNet, VGG)** | Image classification | | **ResNet, Inception, DenseNet** | Very deep classification (skip connections) | | **R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN** | Region-based object detection | | **YOLO, SSD** | Real-time single-shot object detection | | **FCN, U-Net, SegNet, DeepLab** | Semantic segmentation | | **Autoencoders, GANs** | Generative vision, anomaly detection | | **ViT (Vision Transformer)** | Attention-based vision (recent) | --- ## 1.5 Core Building Blocks (KTU Highlight) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every deep vision network is built from these fundamental layers: > 1. **Convolutional Layer** — feature extraction > 2. **Activation Function** — non-linearity (ReLU, Sigmoid, Softmax) > 3. **Pooling Layer** — spatial downsampling > 4. **Fully Connected Layer** — final classification > 5. **Loss Function** — guides training (Cross-Entropy, MSE) > 6. **Optimization Algorithm** — updates weights (SGD, Adam) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Convolutional Layer — The Heart of Deep Vision A convolutional layer applies a set of learnable filters (kernels) to the input image. Each filter detects a specific local pattern. ### Mathematical Operation For an input volume $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{in}}$ and a filter $W \in \mathbb{R}^{k_h \times k_w \times C_{in}}$, the output feature map $Y$ at spatial location $(i, j)$ is: $$Y[i, j, m] = \sum_{c=0}^{C_{in}-1} \sum_{u=0}^{k_h-1} \sum_{v=0}^{k_w-1} W_m[u, v, c] \cdot X[i+u, j+v, c] + b_m$$ where $b_m$ is the bias for the $m$-th filter. ### Why Convolutions Work for Images - **Local Connectivity:** Each neuron connects to only a small region (receptive field). - **Parameter Sharing:** The same filter slides across the image — drastically fewer parameters than fully connected. - **Translation Equivariance:** A feature detected at one location can be detected anywhere. - **Translation Invariance** (with pooling): Robust to position shifts. --- ## 2.2 Pooling Layer Pooling reduces spatial dimensions, providing: - **Dimensionality reduction** (fewer parameters, less compute) - **Translation invariance** - **Larger receptive fields** in deeper layers **Max Pooling** (most common): $$Y[i, j, c] = \max_{u \in [0, k_h), \, v \in [0, k_w)} X[i \cdot s + u, \, j \cdot s + v, c]$$ **Average Pooling:** $$Y[i, j, c] = \frac{1}{k_h k_w} \sum_{u, v} X[i \cdot s + u, \, j \cdot s + v, c]$$ --- ## 2.3 Activation Functions | Function | Formula | Derivative | Use Case | |----------|---------|-----------|----------| | **ReLU** | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | $f'(x) = \mathbb{1}[x > 0]$ | Hidden layers (default) | | **Leaky ReLU** | $f(x) = \max(\alpha x, x)$ | $f'(x) = \mathbb{1}[x>0] + \alpha \mathbb{1}[x \le 0]$ | Avoid dying ReLU | | **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | $\sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ | Binary output | | **Softmax** | $p_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $p_i(\delta_{ij} - p_j)$ | Multi-class output | | **Tanh** | $\frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | $1 - \tanh^2(x)$ | Hidden (zero-centered) | --- ## 2.4 Output Volume Dimensions — The Cheat Sheet For a convolutional layer: $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k_h}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$ $$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2p - k_w}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$ $$C_{out} = M \quad (\text{number of filters})$$ where: - $p$ = **padding** (number of zero-padded rows/cols on each side) - $s$ = **stride** (step size of the filter) - $k_h, k_w$ = **kernel height and width** - $M$ = number of **filters** **Total Parameters in a Conv Layer:** $$P = (k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot M$$ (the $+1$ accounts for the bias term per filter). --- ## 2.5 Loss Functions for Vision **Multi-class Cross-Entropy Loss** (for $C$ classes): $$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \, \log(\hat{p}_{i,c})$$ **Binary Cross-Entropy** (for two classes): $$\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{p}_i) \right]$$ **Mean Squared Error** (regression / autoencoders): $$\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$ --- ## 2.6 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exam) > [!NOTE] > **Save this table — these are the most-tested equations in KTU exams.** | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---------|---------|-------| | Conv output dim | $H_{out} = \lfloor (H_{in} + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$ | Same for W | | Conv parameters | $P = (k_h k_w C_{in} + 1) M$ | +1 for bias | | Receptive field growth | $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k - 1) \prod s_{prev}$ | Cumulative | | Softmax | $p_i = e^{z_i} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$ | Output probability | | Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ | Classification | | ReLU | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Activation | | Max Pool | $\max$ over kernel window | Downsampling | | SGD update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$ | $\eta$ = learning rate | | Momentum SGD | $v \leftarrow \beta v + \nabla \mathcal{L}$; $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta v$ | $\beta \approx 0.9$ | | Adam update | $m \leftarrow \beta_1 m + (1-\beta_1)g$; $v \leftarrow \beta_2 v + (1-\beta_2)g^2$ | $\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.999$ | | Dropout (training) | Neuron kept with prob $p$ | Regularization | | Batch Norm | $\hat{x} = (x - \mu_B) / \sqrt{\sigma_B^2 + \epsilon}$ | Normalize per mini-batch | | IoU (detection) | $IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $\vert \cdot \vert$ = area | | mAP | $mAP = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$ | Detection metric | | ResNet block | $y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$ | Skip connection | --- ## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Real-World Vision | Application | Deep Network Used | Real System | |-------------|-------------------|-------------| | Autonomous driving | YOLO / Faster R-CNN | Tesla, Waymo perception | | Medical imaging | U-Net, ResNet | Tumor segmentation in MRI/CT | | Face recognition | FaceNet, ArcFace | Phone unlock, surveillance | | Industrial QC | CNN classifiers | Defect detection on assembly lines | | Satellite imagery | FCN, DeepLab | Land cover classification | | OCR | CRNN + CTC | License plate recognition | | Video surveillance | 3D-CNN, I3D | Action recognition | > [!IMPORTANT] > The transition from **hand-crafted features** to **learned features** is the defining paradigm shift. KTU examiners frequently ask students to compare traditional CV pipelines (filtering, edge detection → SIFT → SVM) with deep learning pipelines (raw pixels → CNN end-to-end). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass Through a Conv Layer (Explicit) **Problem:** Given an input image of size $H \times W \times C$ and a conv layer with $M$ filters of size $k \times k \times C$, stride $s=1$, padding $p=0$, compute the output size and parameter count. **Step 1 — Identify inputs.** $$H_{in} = H, \quad W_{in} = W, \quad C_{in} = C$$ $$k_h = k_w = k, \quad s = 1, \quad p = 0, \quad M = \text{num filters}$$ **Step 2 — Apply output dimension formula.** Using the formula from Section 2.4: $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2(0) - k}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = H - k + 1$$ $$W_{out} = W - k + 1$$ $$C_{out} = M$$ **Step 3 — Output tensor size.** $$Y \in \mathbb{R}^{(H-k+1) \times (W-k+1) \times M}$$ **Step 4 — Parameter count.** For each of the $M$ filters, we have $k \cdot k \cdot C$ weights and 1 bias: $$P = (k^2 \cdot C + 1) \cdot M$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** Input volume is $32 \times 32 \times 3$ (RGB image). Apply a conv layer with 10 filters of size $5 \times 5$, stride 1, no padding. **Step 1 — Compute output height/width.** $$H_{out} = 32 - 5 + 1 = 28$$ $$W_{out} = 32 - 5 + 1 = 28$$ **Step 2 — Output depth.** $$C_{out} = 10$$ **Step 3 — Output volume.** $$Y \in \mathbb{R}^{28 \times 28 \times 10}$$ **Step 4 — Total parameters.** $$P = (5^2 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 10 = (75 + 1) \cdot 10 = 760$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Backpropagation in a Conv Layer Let $\mathcal{L}$ be the loss. For a conv layer with input $X$, filter $W$, output $Y$, the gradient with respect to $W$ is the **convolution of $X$ with the output gradient** (rotated 180°): $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_m[u, v, c]} = \sum_{i, j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Y[i, j, m]} \cdot X[i+u, j+v, c]$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial X[i, j, c]} = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{u, v} W_m[u, v, c] \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Y[i-u, j-v, m]}$$ The gradient w.r.t. input is the **full convolution of the rotated filter with the output gradient** (transposed convolution operation). > [!NOTE] > KTU may ask: *"Why is backpropagation in conv layers a convolution itself?"* The answer: because both forward and backward operations slide a kernel across the volume — they are linear and shift-invariant operations. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Receptive Field Calculation The **receptive field** is the region of the input image that influences a particular neuron in a deep layer. **Recurrence relation:** $$RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot J_l$$ where $J_l$ is the **jump** (cumulative stride product up to layer $l$): $$J_l = \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i, \quad J_1 = 1$$ **Step-by-step worked example** for three conv layers with $k = 3$ and $s = 1$ each: $$RF_0 = 1, \quad J_1 = 1$$ $$RF_1 = 1 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 3, \quad J_2 = 1$$ $$RF_2 = 3 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 5, \quad J_3 = 1$$ $$RF_3 = 5 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 7$$ With **stride 2** in layer 2: $$J_1 = 1, \quad J_2 = 1$$ $$J_3 = 1 \cdot 2 = 2$$ $$RF_1 = 1 + 2 = 3$$ $$RF_2 = 3 + 2 = 5$$ $$RF_3 = 5 + 2 \cdot 2 = 9$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Classic Deep Architectures > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions.** Examiners love asking "compare and contrast" questions on deep architectures. | Architecture | Year | Depth | Key Innovation | Parameters | Top-5 ImageNet Error | |--------------|------|-------|----------------|------------|---------------------| | **LeNet-5** | 1998 | 7 | First CNN (digits) | 60K | N/A (MNIST) | | **AlexNet** | 2012 | 8 | ReLU, Dropout, GPU | 60M | 15.3% | | **VGG-16** | 2014 | 16 | 3×3 conv stacks | 138M | 7.3% | | **GoogLeNet** | 2014 | 22 | Inception module | 5M | 6.7% | | **ResNet-50** | 2015 | 50 | Skip connections | 25M | 3.6% | | **ResNet-152** | 2015 | 152 | Deeper via residuals | 60M | 3.57% | | **DenseNet-121** | 2017 | 121 | Dense connectivity | 8M | 4.9% | | **EfficientNet-B0** | 2019 | Variable | Compound scaling | 5M | 6.3% | | **ViT-B/16** | 2020 | 12 (transformer) | Patch tokens + attention | 86M | 7.8% | --- ## 3.6 Derivation: ResNet Skip Connection The **residual block** in ResNet learns a **residual function** $\mathcal{F}(x)$ instead of a direct mapping $H(x)$: $$H(x) = \mathcal{F}(x) + x$$ where $x$ is the identity shortcut (skip connection). **Why does this work?** If the optimal mapping is close to identity, it's easier for the network to push $\mathcal{F}(x) \rightarrow 0$ than to learn $H(x) = x$ directly. Mathematically, the gradient through the skip connection is: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} + 1 \right)$$ The "$+1$" term ensures gradients **never vanish** even when $\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x}$ is small, solving the **vanishing gradient problem** that plagued very deep networks. --- ## 3.7 Object Detection: IoU and mAP Derivation **Intersection over Union (IoU)** between predicted box $B_p$ and ground-truth box $B_g$: $$IoU = \frac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_g)}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_g)} = \frac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_g)}{\text{Area}(B_p) + \text{Area}(B_g) - \text{Area}(B_p \cap B_g)}$$ **Average Precision (AP)** for class $c$: $$AP_c = \int_0^1 P(R) \, dR$$ where $P$ = precision, $R$ = recall at varying IoU thresholds. **Mean Average Precision:** $$mAP = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently tests: *"Define IoU. If a predicted box has area 100 px², ground truth has 80 px², and overlap is 60 px², compute IoU."* Solution: $IoU = 60 / (100 + 80 - 60) = 60/120 = 0.5$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic CNN Architecture for Image Classification ```mermaid graph TD node1[Input Image
H x W x 3] --> node2[Conv Layer 1
ReLU Activation] node2 --> node3[Max Pooling 1
Reduces spatial size] node3 --> node4[Conv Layer 2
ReLU Activation] node4 --> node5[Max Pooling 2
Reduces spatial size] node5 --> node6[Conv Layer N
ReLU Activation] node6 --> node7[Max Pooling N] node7 --> node8[Flatten Layer
Vectorize features] node8 --> node9[Fully Connected 1
ReLU Activation] node9 --> node10[Dropout Layer
Regularization] node10 --> node11[Fully Connected 2
Softmax Output] node11 --> node12[Class Probabilities
P cat, dog, car] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU interpretation:** Each conv block progressively extracts higher-level features. The first layers detect edges; the final FC layers act as a classifier on the learned feature vector. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: ResNet Residual Block ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Input x] --> nodeB[Conv 3x3
ReLU] nodeB --> nodeC[Conv 3x3] nodeC --> nodeD[Add Skip
Connection x] nodeD --> nodeE[ReLU Activation] nodeA -.Identity Shortcut.-> nodeD nodeE --> nodeF[Output H x] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The identity shortcut is the **core innovation** of ResNet. Without it, training a 152-layer network is infeasible due to vanishing gradients. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Object Detection Pipeline (Faster R-CNN) ```mermaid graph TD nodeImg[Input Image] --> nodeBackbone[CNN Backbone
ResNet 50 Feature Extraction] nodeBackbone --> nodeFeat[Feature Map] nodeFeat --> nodeRPN[Region Proposal Network RPN
Generates candidate boxes] nodeRPN --> nodeProp[Region Proposals
Variable count] nodeProp --> nodeROI[ROI Pooling
Fixed size features] nodeROI --> nodeFC[FC Layers
Classification and Regression] nodeFC --> nodeOut[Output
Class labels and Bounding Boxes] ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram: YOLO Single-Shot Detection Flow ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Input Image
448 x 448 x 3] --> nodeB[CNN Backbone
GoogLeNet or Darknet] nodeB --> nodeC[Feature Map
Grid S x S] nodeC --> nodeD[Predict Per Cell
B boxes, C class probs, confidence] nodeD --> nodeE[Non Max Suppression
Remove duplicates] nodeE --> nodeF[Final Detections
Boxes plus class labels] ``` > [!NOTE] > **YOLO's key idea:** The image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid, and each cell directly predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities in a **single forward pass** — hence "You Only Look Once." This enables real-time detection (45–155 FPS). --- ## 4.5 Block Diagram: U-Net for Semantic Segmentation ```mermaid graph LR nodeIn[Input Image
H x W x 3] --> nodeEnc1[Conv Block 1
64 channels] nodeEnc1 --> nodePool1[Max Pool] nodePool1 --> nodeEnc2[Conv Block 2
128 channels] nodeEnc2 --> nodePool2[Max Pool] nodePool2 --> nodeEnc3[Bottleneck
256 channels] nodeEnc3 --> nodeUp1[Up Conv / Transpose] nodeEnc2 -.Skip Connection.-> nodeUp1 nodeUp1 --> nodeDec1[Conv Block 3
128 channels] nodeDec1 --> nodeUp2[Up Conv / Transpose] nodeEnc1 -.Skip Connection.-> nodeUp2 nodeUp2 --> nodeDec2[Output Segmentation Map
H x W x C] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **U-Net** uses an **encoder-decoder** structure with **skip connections** at every level. The skip connections preserve fine spatial detail lost during downsampling — critical for pixel-precise segmentation. --- ## 4.6 Training Loop Flow (End-to-End Vision Pipeline) ```mermaid graph TD nodeStart[Start] --> nodeLoad[Load Batched Images and Labels] nodeLoad --> nodeForward[Forward Pass through CNN] nodeForward --> nodeLoss[Compute Loss
Cross Entropy or MSE] nodeLoss --> nodeBack[Backpropagation
Compute Gradients] nodeBack --> nodeUpdate[Update Weights via SGD or Adam] nodeUpdate --> nodeCheck{Epoch Complete} nodeCheck --> nodeLoad nodeCheck --> nodeEnd[End of Training] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a Convolutional Neural Network. List its key components.** **Model Answer:** A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a class of deep neural networks designed to process data with a grid-like topology (e.g., images). It uses the convolution operation in place of general matrix multiplication in at least one layer. **Key Components:** 1. **Convolutional Layers** — Apply learnable filters to extract local features. 2. **Activation Function (ReLU)** — Introduces non-linearity. 3. **Pooling Layers** — Downsample feature maps (max or average pooling). 4. **Fully Connected Layers** — Perform final classification. 5. **Loss Function** — Quantifies prediction error. 6. **Optimizer (SGD/Adam)** — Updates weights via backpropagation. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the vanishing gradient problem? How does ResNet address it?** **Model Answer:** The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs during backpropagation in deep networks when gradients become extremely small as they propagate to earlier layers, causing those layers to learn very slowly or not at all. **How ResNet addresses it:** ResNet introduces **skip (identity) connections** that bypass one or more layers: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$ The gradient flowing through the skip connection has a direct path with a "$+1$" term, ensuring that the gradient magnitude does not collapse to zero. This enables training of networks with 100+ layers. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network for image classification. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the working of the **ResNet** architecture. Show how the residual block helps in training very deep networks. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): A **CNN for image classification** follows a feed-forward hierarchical architecture: 1. **Input Layer** — Receives the raw image tensor $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ (RGB). 2. **Convolutional Layer** — Applies $M$ learnable filters of size $k \times k$ to produce a feature map. Each filter detects a specific local pattern. Output dimension: $$H_{out} = \lfloor (H - k)/s \rfloor + 1 + 2p$$ 3. **ReLU Activation** — $f(x) = \max(0, x)$, introduces non-linearity so the network can model complex functions. 4. **Pooling Layer** — Max pooling reduces spatial dimensions, providing translation invariance and reducing parameters. 5. **Sequence of Conv-Pool Blocks** — Successive blocks learn increasingly abstract features (edges → textures → parts → objects). 6. **Flatten Layer** — Converts 3D feature maps into a 1D vector. 7. **Fully Connected Layer** — Learns non-linear combinations of features for classification. 8. **Softmax Output** — Converts logits to class probabilities: $p_i = e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$. **Role of each layer:** - Conv = **feature extraction** - ReLU = **non-linearity** - Pooling = **spatial reduction + invariance** - FC = **high-level reasoning** - Softmax = **probabilistic output** **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 CNN architecture block diagram) --- #### Model Solution for (b): **ResNet (Residual Network)** was introduced by He et al. (2015) to enable training of very deep networks (50, 101, 152 layers) by reformulating layers as learning **residual functions**. **Residual Block Formulation:** Instead of learning a direct mapping $H(x)$, the block learns a residual $\mathcal{F}(x)$: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$ If the optimal function is close to identity, the network only needs to push $\mathcal{F}(x) \to 0$ — much easier than learning $H(x) = x$. **Gradient Flow Analysis:** The gradient of the loss with respect to $x$ in a residual block is: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} + 1 \right)$$ The "$+1$" prevents gradient vanishing. Even when $\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} \approx 0$, the gradient remains $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}$, providing a stable path for backpropagation. **Bottleneck Design:** ResNet-50/101/152 use a 3-layer bottleneck block ($1 \times 1 \to 3 \times 3 \to 1 \times 1$) to reduce computation while increasing depth. **Impact:** ResNet-152 won ILSVRC 2015 with 3.57% top-5 error — first time a CNN surpassed human-level (5.1%) on ImageNet classification. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]` **(a)** Explain **object detection** in deep learning. Compare **Faster R-CNN** and **YOLO** architectures. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **U-Net architecture** for semantic segmentation. Mention the role of skip connections. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Object Detection** is the task of locating and classifying multiple objects in an image. The output consists of: - **Bounding box coordinates** $(x, y, w, h)$ - **Class label** $c$ - **Confidence score** $p$ **Evaluation Metric:** IoU and mAP. **Faster R-CNN (Two-Stage Detector):** | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | Backbone CNN (ResNet) | Feature extraction | | Region Proposal Network (RPN) | Generates ~2000 candidate object regions | | ROI Pooling | Converts variable-size proposals to fixed-size features | | FC Layers | Final classification + bounding-box regression | **Process:** Region proposals are generated *first*, then classified — hence "two-stage." Higher accuracy, lower speed (~7 FPS). **YOLO (Single-Shot Detector):** 1. Image divided into $S \times S$ grid. 2. Each cell directly predicts $B$ bounding boxes, confidence scores, and $C$ class probabilities in **one forward pass**. 3. Non-Max Suppression removes duplicate detections. **YOLO is faster** (45–155 FPS) but slightly less accurate on small objects. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Faster R-CNN | YOLO | |--------|--------------|------| | Type | Two-stage | Single-stage | | Speed | ~7 FPS | 45–155 FPS | | Accuracy | Higher mAP | Slightly lower for small objects | | Use case | Offline analysis | Real-time (autonomous vehicles) | --- #### Model Solution for (b): **U-Net Architecture (Ronneberger et al., 2015):** U-Net is a symmetric **encoder-decoder** network designed for biomedical image segmentation but widely used across all segmentation tasks. **Structure (refer to Section 4.5 diagram):** 1. **Encoder (Contracting Path):** - Series of $3 \times 3$ convolutions + ReLU + $2 \times 2$ max pooling - Doubles channels at each level (64 → 128 → 256 → 512) - Captures **context** and "what" is in the image 2. **Bottleneck:** - The deepest layer — processes compressed global features 3. **Decoder (Expanding Path):** - $2 \times 2$ transposed convolutions (upsampling) - Halves channels at each level - Recovers **spatial precision** and "where" 4. **Skip Connections:** - Concatenate feature maps from encoder to corresponding decoder level - **Role:** Preserve high-resolution spatial information lost during downsampling - Combine **low-level spatial detail** with **high-level semantic features** - Critical for pixel-precise segmentation masks 5. **Final $1 \times 1$ Convolution:** Maps to $C$ class probability maps (one per class). **Output:** A segmentation mask $M \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ where each pixel is assigned a class. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in deep network questions:** > 1. **Confusing convolutions with cross-correlation** — examiners expect the *standard deep learning convention* (no kernel flip); stating the wrong one loses 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting the $+1$ bias** in parameter count calculations. > 3. **Writing "FC layer learns features"** — FC layers do *not* learn features; they perform classification on learned features. Conv layers learn features. > 4. **Missing padding/stride in dimension formulas** — Always state $p$ and $s$ explicitly. > 5. **Describing ResNet as "adding extra layers"** — The key insight is the *residual* formulation, not just depth. > 6. **Comparing R-CNN to YOLO without numbers** — Always include quantitative comparisons (FPS, mAP). > 7. **In U-Net, forgetting to mention skip connections** — They are the *defining* feature, worth 2–3 marks by themselves. > 8. **Using vague terms like "deep learning is better"** — Be specific: parameter sharing, hierarchical features, end-to-end training. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deep Networks** **Foundational Concepts:** - [ ] Deep networks = ANNs with multiple hidden layers - [ ] CNNs dominate vision tasks due to local connectivity, parameter sharing, translation equivariance - [ ] End-to-end learning eliminates hand-crafted features **CNN Building Blocks:** - [ ] **Convolution** — feature extraction via learnable filters - [ ] **ReLU** — default activation; solves vanishing gradient in hidden layers - [ ] **Max Pooling** — downsampling for invariance - [ ] **Fully Connected + Softmax** — final classification **Critical Formulas:** - [ ] Output dim: $H_{out} = \lfloor(H_{in} + 2p - k)/s\rfloor + 1$ - [ ] Parameters: $P = (k^2 C_{in} + 1) M$ - [ ] Softmax: $p_i = e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$ - [ ] Cross-Entropy: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ **Architectures to Know:** - [ ] **LeNet/AlexNet** — pioneers - [ ] **VGG** — depth via $3 \times 3$ stacks - [ ] **ResNet** — skip connections solve vanishing gradient - [ ] **Inception/GoogLeNet** — multi-scale via parallel filters - [ ] **Faster R-CNN** — accurate two-stage detection - [ ] **YOLO** — real-time single-stage detection - [ ] **U-Net** — encoder-decoder with skip connections for segmentation **Concepts That Appear in Nearly Every Exam:** - [ ] Difference between shallow vs deep networks - [ ] ResNet and skip connections - [ ] CNN vs traditional hand-crafted feature pipelines - [ ] Object detection metrics: IoU, mAP - [ ] Trade-off: accuracy vs speed in detection - [ ] U-Net skip connections **Engineering Awareness:** - [ ] Dropout, Batch Normalization — regularization - [ ] Transfer learning — fine-tuning pre-trained models - [ ] Data augmentation — rotation, flip, crop - [ ] GPU acceleration for training - [ ] Real-world applications: medical imaging, autonomous driving, surveillance > [!TIP] > **Last-minute tip:** For 14-mark questions, always include a **diagram** (even a hand-drawn block diagram earns full structure marks), **one comparison table**, and **at least 3 formulas** with explanations.

topics.title: Object Detection - Face Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Object Detection - Face Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Object Detection** is a fundamental computer vision task that involves identifying and localizing instances of semantic objects of a certain class (such as humans, buildings, or cars) within digital images and videos. The objective is dual-fold: (1) **Classification** — determining *what* object is present, and (2) **Localization** — determining *where* in the image the object resides, typically via bounding boxes.

**Face Detection** is a specialized subdomain of object detection focused exclusively on locating and isolating human faces within an image, irrespective of their pose, expression, orientation, scale, lighting, or partial occlusion. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Viola-Jones framework (2001)** as the foundational real-time face detection paradigm, supplemented by modern feature-based and deep learning extensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Face Detection ≠ Face Recognition.** Detection answers *"Is there a face, and where?"*. Recognition answers *"Whose face is it?"*. The former is a prerequisite, not a synonym, of the latter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The board examination pattern primarily tests the *Viola-Jones algorithm*, its three pillars (Haar features, Integral Image, AdaBoost), and the *Cascade of Classifiers* architecture. Mastery of these is non-negotiable for high marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a busy railway station and your friend challenges you to spot every person wearing a red cap. Your brain does not scan every pixel of the visual field — instead, it uses a *hierarchy of quick-to-hard checks*:

1. **First glance** — Is there any red blob in the upper part of the image? If not, skip this region entirely.
2. **Closer look** — Does the red blob have the rough *shape* of a cap? If not, discard.
3. **Detailed inspection** — Does it have a brim? A button on top? If yes, mark it.

This is exactly the philosophy of the **Viola-Jones cascade**: apply *cheap tests first*, expensive tests only when necessary. The **Integral Image** is your mental "cheat sheet" that lets you compute the average brightness of any rectangle in *constant time* — like having a calculator that gives you the area of any polygon without counting squares. **AdaBoost** is your personal tutor that tells you, from thousands of possible features, *which ones actually matter* for distinguishing faces from non-faces.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (used throughout the topic)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Rate** (True Positive Rate) | **TPR** | Fraction of actual faces correctly detected |
| **False Positive Rate** | **FPR** | Fraction of non-faces wrongly classified as faces |
| **False Negatives** | **FN** | Missed faces |
| **Processing Speed** | **FPS** | Frames Per Second — real-time requires > 15 FPS |
| **Feature Value** | **f(x)** | Scalar output of a Haar-like feature on a sub-window |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Physical/Performance Constants in Viola-Jones:**
> - Standard training window size: **24 × 24 pixels**
> - Number of Haar features in the 24×24 base set: **~160,000**
> - Final selected features after AdaBoost: **~6,000**
> - Cascade stages typically: **~38 stages**
> - Real-time detection on a 2001-era processor: **15 FPS**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Viola-Jones Framework

The Viola-Jones object detector is built on **three revolutionary ideas**, each addressing a specific bottleneck of real-time detection:

### Pillar 1 — Haar-like Features (The "What to Look At")

- Inspired by **Haar wavelets** (mathematical functions proposed by Alfred Haar in 1909).
- Instead of operating on raw pixels, the detector uses *rectangular features* applied to grayscale sub-windows.
- **Three canonical feature types**:

  1. **Two-rectangle feature (Edge feature):** Difference between sums of pixels in two adjacent horizontal or vertical rectangles.
  2. **Three-rectangle feature (Line feature):** Difference between the sum in the center rectangle and the sum in the two outer rectangles.
  3. **Four-rectangle feature (Diagonal feature):** Difference between diagonal pairs of rectangles.

- **Why they work:** Faces have characteristic intensity patterns — the eye region is darker than the cheeks, the nose bridge is brighter than the eyes, the mouth is darker than the cheeks. Haar features capture these micro-contrast patterns efficiently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** A 24×24 detector window contains more than **180,000** possible Haar features (the exhaustive set). This huge number is precisely why a *feature selection algorithm* (AdaBoost) is mandatory — brute-force evaluation is computationally impossible.

---

### Pillar 2 — The Integral Image (The "How to Compute Fast")

- The integral image (also called the **Summed Area Table**, SAT) at pixel $(x, y)$ is defined as the sum of all pixel intensities in the **rectangular region** from the origin $(0, 0)$ to $(x, y)$.
- Once computed, *any* rectangular sum can be retrieved using only **four array lookups**, in **O(1)** time, regardless of rectangle size.
- This transforms the evaluation of multi-million feature calculations from an O(N²) operation to an O(1) operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pioneering Fact:** The Summed Area Table trick was originally developed for **texture mapping in computer graphics** (Crow, 1984). Viola & Jones (2001) were the first to repurpose it for real-time object detection — a classic example of cross-domain innovation.

---

### Pillar 3 — AdaBoost (The "Which Features Matter")

- **AdaBoost** = *Adaptive Boosting* (Freund & Schapire, 1995).
- It is a **meta-learning algorithm** that combines many *weak learners* (here, simple decision stumps based on single Haar features) into one *strong classifier*.
- It adaptively re-weights misclassified training samples so that subsequent weak learners focus on the *harder* examples.
- The final strong classifier is a **weighted linear combination** of weak classifiers, where each weak classifier is a single Haar feature threshold.

---

## 2.2 The Cascade of Classifiers (The "When to Stop")

- A cascade is a **degenerate decision tree** arranged as a chain of progressively more complex classifiers.
- Each stage is a strong AdaBoost classifier with an increasing number of features:
  - Stage 1: ~2 features (cheap)
  - Stage 2: ~5 features
  - ...
  - Stage 38: ~6000 features (expensive)
- A sub-window is **rejected immediately** if it fails *any* stage. It must pass *all* stages to be declared a face.
- This is the **operational core** of real-time performance: most sub-windows in an image are non-faces and are eliminated in the first 1–2 stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The cascade reduces computation by a factor of roughly **10 to 100×** compared to a single monolithic classifier, while maintaining a high detection rate.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Integral Image** | $ii(x,y) = \sum_{x' \le x,\; y' \le y} I(x',y')$ | Sum of pixels from origin to $(x,y)$; dimensionless (pixel units) |
| 2 | **Rectangular Sum (SAT query)** | $S = ii(A) + ii(D) - ii(B) - ii(C)$ | Sum over rect. with corners A (BR), B (BL), C (TR), D (TL) |
| 3 | **Haar Feature Value** | $f = \sum (\text{white region}) - \sum (\text{black region})$ | Scalar; sign distinguishes face-like patterns |
| 4 | **Weak Classifier** | $h_j(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } p_j f_j(x) < p_j \theta_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Decision stump with parity $p_j \in \{-1, +1\}$ and threshold $\theta_j$ |
| 5 | **AdaBoost Sample Weight Update** | $w_{i}^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} e^{-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)}$ | After misclassification, weight increases exponentially |
| 6 | **Weak Learner Weight** | $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$ | Higher $\alpha_t$ when weak learner is more accurate |
| 7 | **Strong Classifier (AdaBoost output)** | $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$ | Weighted sum of weak classifiers; sign gives final class |
| 8 | **Cascade Stage Threshold** | $\text{If } \exists j \le k : H_k(x) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{REJECT}$ | Window rejected at the first failed stage |
| 9 | **Overall Detection Rate (Cascade)** | $D = \prod_{i=1}^{K} d_i$ | Product of per-stage detection rates $d_i$ |
| 10 | **Overall False Positive Rate (Cascade)** | $F = \prod_{i=1}^{K} f_i$ | Product of per-stage false positive rates $f_i$ |
| 11 | **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of detections that are true faces |
| 12 | **Recall (TPR)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of true faces that are detected |
| 13 | **Intersection over Union (IoU)** | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Bounding box accuracy metric; $\in [0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, absolute-value / magnitude bars in IoU use `\vert` — *never* the raw pipe symbol `|`, which breaks Markdown table syntax. This is a frequent KTU answer-script convention pitfall.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surveillance & Security:** CCTV-based intruder detection in airports, banks, and stadiums.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Auto-focus and smile-shutter in digital cameras; face unlock in smartphones.
- **Automotive:** Driver drowsiness and attention monitoring (e.g., Euro NCAP 2025 mandates DMS).
- **Healthcare:** Patient monitoring, fall detection in elderly care.
- **Retail:** Customer analytics, emotion-based advertisement targeting.
- **Biometric Access Control:** Entry gates, attendance systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Integral Image Recurrence Relation

### Goal
Show that the 2D integral image $ii(x,y)$ can be computed in **one pass** over the image, in $O(MN)$ time for an $M \times N$ image.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

By definition:
$$
ii(x,y) = \sum_{x' \le x,\; y' \le y} I(x',y')
$$

Equivalently, this is a 2D prefix sum. Recurrence form:
$$
ii(x,y) = I(x,y) + ii(x-1,y) + ii(x,y-1) - ii(x-1,y-1)
$$

**Derivation logic:**
- Add the current pixel $I(x,y)$ to the running sum.
- Add everything above: $ii(x-1, y)$ (cumulative sum up to row $y$ and column $x-1$).
- Add everything to the left: $ii(x, y-1)$ (cumulative sum up to row $y-1$ and column $x$).
- Subtract the overlap (the region counted twice): $ii(x-1, y-1)$.

**Boundary conditions:** $ii(-1, y) = 0$ and $ii(x, -1) = 0$ for all $x, y$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rectangular Sum in O(1)

Let a rectangle have top-left corner $A$ and bottom-right corner $D$. The other two corners are $B$ (top-right) and $C$ (bottom-left).

The sum of all pixels inside this rectangle is given by inclusion-exclusion:
$$
S_{ABCD} = ii(D) - ii(B) - ii(C) + ii(A)
$$

**Derivation logic:**
- $ii(D)$ contains the sum of the entire rectangle *plus* everything above-left.
- Subtract $ii(B)$ (top-right corner) — removes the right part outside the rectangle.
- Subtract $ii(C)$ (bottom-left corner) — removes the bottom part outside the rectangle.
- The top-left sub-region (with corner $A$) has been subtracted *twice*, so add $ii(A)$ back once.

**Result:** Computing any rectangular sum requires only **4 array lookups + 3 additions**, regardless of rectangle area. For a Haar feature composed of $k$ rectangles, the cost is $4k$ lookups.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: AdaBoost Weight Update and $\alpha_t$ Computation

### Step 1 — Sample weights
Each training sample $(x_i, y_i)$ has an associated weight $w_i^{(t)} \ge 0$, with $\sum_i w_i^{(t)} = 1$.

### Step 2 — Train a weak learner
Fit a decision stump $h_t$ that minimizes the weighted classification error:
$$
\epsilon_t = \sum_{i:\, h_t(x_i) \ne y_i} w_i^{(t)}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute learner weight
$$
\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation note:** $\alpha_t$ is derived by minimizing the exponential loss $L = \sum_i e^{-y_i H(x_i)}$ via gradient descent. The result is the natural "log-odds" of the weak learner's accuracy.

**Interpretation:** A perfect weak learner ($\epsilon_t = 0$) yields $\alpha_t \to \infty$ (infinite weight). A random learner ($\epsilon_t = 0.5$) yields $\alpha_t = 0$ (ignored). A worse-than-random learner yields $\alpha_t < 0$ (inverted).

### Step 4 — Update sample weights
$$
w_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))}{Z_t}
$$
where $Z_t$ is a normalization constant:
$$
Z_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))
$$

**Result:** Misclassified samples ($y_i h_t(x_i) = -1$) have their weights multiplied by $e^{\alpha_t} > 1$, so subsequent learners focus on them.

### Step 5 — Final strong classifier
$$
H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x) \right)
$$

**Geometric meaning:** $H(x)$ votes with strength $\alpha_t$ for each weak learner; majority-signed sum wins.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cascade Overall Detection and False-Positive Rates

Consider a cascade of $K$ stages, where stage $i$ has:
- Detection rate $d_i$ (true positive rate on faces that reach this stage)
- False positive rate $f_i$ (false positive rate on non-faces that reach this stage)

A face is detected **only if it passes every stage**. By independence approximation:
$$
D = \prod_{i=1}^{K} d_i
$$

A non-face is misclassified **only if it passes every stage**:
$$
F = \prod_{i=1}^{K} f_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Target (Viola-Jones original):** Achieve $D \ge 0.9$ and $F \le 10^{-6}$ with, e.g., $K = 38$ stages each having $d_i = 0.995$ and $f_i = 0.5$.

**Verification:** $D = 0.995^{38} \approx 0.83$ (slightly below target in practice; actually stages have variable $d_i$). $F = 0.5^{38} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-12}$ — extraordinarily low. The product structure is what makes the cascade so powerful.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Face Detection Methods

| Method | Year | Feature | Speed | Accuracy | Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Viola-Jones (Haar + AdaBoost)** | 2001 | Haar-like | ★★★★★ Real-time | ★★★ (frontal) | Low to moderate |
| **HOG + SVM (DPM)** | 2005 | Histogram of Oriented Gradients | ★★★ | ★★★★ | Moderate pose variation |
| **LBP + AdaBoost** | 2004 | Local Binary Patterns | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | Better under illumination changes |
| **CNN-based (e.g., MTCNN)** | 2016 | Learned convolutions | ★★ (GPU) | ★★★★★ | High, multi-pose, occlusion |
| **YOLO / SSD (general detectors)** | 2016+ | Multi-scale CNN | ★★★★ (GPU) | ★★★★★ | Excellent, real-time capable |

---

## 3.6 Decision-Threshold Tuning Equation (Receiver Operating Characteristic)

For binary classification, the detection threshold $\theta$ trades off TPR against FPR:
$$
\text{TPR}(\theta) = \int_{\theta}^{\infty} p(\text{score} \mid \text{face})\, d(\text{score})
$$

$$
\text{FPR}(\theta) = \int_{\theta}^{\infty} p(\text{score} \mid \text{non-face})\, d(\text{score})
$$

Plotting TPR vs. FPR as $\theta$ varies yields the **ROC curve**. The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** measures aggregate classifier quality; AUC = 1.0 is perfect, AUC = 0.5 is random.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Haar-like Feature Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeatureFamily["Haar Feature Family"]
        direction TB
        F1["TWO-RECTANGLE / EDGE FEATURE
        Vertical or Horizontal Adjacency
        f = Sum_White - Sum_Black"]
        F2["THREE-RECTANGLE / LINE FEATURE
        Center vs Two Outer
        f = Sum_Center - Sum_Outers"]
        F3["FOUR-RECTANGLE / DIAGONAL FEATURE
        Checkerboard Pattern
        f = Diag1 - Diag2"]
    end

    subgraph Capture["Micro-Patterns Captured on Faces"]
        direction TB
        C1["Eye vs Cheek Contrast"]
        C2["Nose Bridge vs Eyes Contrast"]
        C3["Mouth vs Surrounding Contrast"]
    end

    F1 --> C1
    F2 --> C2
    F3 --> C3
```

## 4.2 Integral Image — Four-Array Lookup Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Origin["ORIGIN (0,0)"]:::originNode
    Region["REVISED REGION OF INTEREST
    Sum required over inner rectangle"]:::regionNode

    P1["P1 = (x1, y1)
    Top-Left of region
    ii(P1) = sum from origin to P1"]:::cornerNode
    P2["P2 = (x2, y1)
    Top-Right corner
    ii(P2) = sum from origin to P2"]:::cornerNode
    P3["P3 = (x1, y2)
    Bottom-Left corner
    ii(P3) = sum from origin to P3"]:::cornerNode
    P4["P4 = (x2, y2)
    Bottom-Right corner
    ii(P4) = sum from origin to P4"]:::cornerNode

    Formula["S = ii(P4) - ii(P3) - ii(P2) + ii(P1)
    O(1) four-lookup query"]:::formulaNode

    P4 --> Formula
    P3 --> Formula
    P2 --> Formula
    P1 --> Formula

    classDef originNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef regionNode fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cornerNode fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef formulaNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Viola-Jones Detection Pipeline (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT IMAGE
    Grayscale, any size"]:::stepNode
    B["RESIZE / SCALE IMAGE
    Multi-scale pyramid"]:::stepNode
    C["SLIDING WINDOW SCAN
    24x24 sub-window, stride = 1 pixel"]:::stepNode
    D["COMPUTE INTEGRAL IMAGE
    One pass O MN"]:::stepNode
    E["CASCADE OF CLASSIFIERS
    Apply AdaBoost stages sequentially"]:::stageNode
    F["NON-FACE"]:::rejectNode
    G["MERGE OVERLAPPING DETECTIONS
    Non-maximum suppression"]:::stepNode
    H["FINAL FACE BOUNDING BOXES
    Coordinates + confidence"]:::outputNode

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    E -->|reject| F
    E -->|accept all stages| G --> H

    classDef stepNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef stageNode fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rejectNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Cascade Architecture (Rejection Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Win["SUB-WINDOW ENTERS CASCADE"]:::inputNode

    S1["STAGE 1
    2 Haar features
    Reject 50% non-faces"]:::stageNode
    S2["STAGE 2
    5 Haar features
    Reject 80% non-faces"]:::stageNode
    S3["STAGE 3
    20 Haar features
    Reject 90% non-faces"]:::stageNode
    SK["..."]:::stageNode
    SN["STAGE 38
    6000 Haar features
    Reject 99.99% non-faces"]:::stageNode

    Face["FACE DETECTED
    Final Decision"]:::outputNode
    Reject["REJECTED
    Early Exit"]:::rejectNode

    Win --> S1
    S1 -->|FAIL| Reject
    S1 -->|PASS| S2
    S2 -->|FAIL| Reject
    S2 -->|PASS| S3
    S3 -->|FAIL| Reject
    S3 -->|PASS| SK
    SK --> SN
    SN -->|FAIL| Reject
    SN -->|PASS| Face

    classDef inputNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stageNode fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rejectNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.5 AdaBoost Training Loop (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Init["INITIALIZE SAMPLE WEIGHTS
    w_i = 1 / N for all i"]:::stepNode
    LoopStart{"FOR t = 1 to T"}:::decisionNode
    TrainWeak["TRAIN WEAK CLASSIFIER h_t
    Minimize weighted error epsilon_t"]:::stepNode
    ComputeAlpha["COMPUTE alpha_t
    alpha_t = 0.5 ln((1 - eps_t) / eps_t)"]:::stepNode
    UpdateW["UPDATE SAMPLE WEIGHTS
    Boost misclassified samples"]:::stepNode
    Accumulate["ACCUMULATE H(x)
    H(x) = H(x) + alpha_t h_t(x)"]:::stepNode
    Converge{"ALL FACES
    CLASSIFIED?"}:::decisionNode
    Final["OUTPUT STRONG CLASSIFIER
    H(x) = sign of weighted sum"]:::outputNode

    Init --> LoopStart
    LoopStart --> TrainWeak --> ComputeAlpha --> UpdateW --> Accumulate --> Converge
    Converge -->|No| LoopStart
    Converge -->|Yes| Final

    classDef stepNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Integral Image. Why is it crucial for real-time face detection in the Viola-Jones framework?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **integral image** $ii(x, y)$ at location $(x, y)$ stores the sum of all pixel intensities in the rectangle from the top-left origin $(0, 0)$ to $(x, y)$. Formally:

$$ii(x, y) = \sum_{x' \le x,\, y' \le y} I(x', y')$$

It is crucial because it allows the sum of intensities over *any* rectangular region to be computed in **O(1) time using just four array lookups**, regardless of rectangle area. Since a 24×24 detector window evaluates approximately 180,000 Haar-like features at every sub-window position, this constant-time retrieval reduces feature computation cost from quadratic to constant, enabling real-time detection at 15+ FPS on modest hardware — the central innovation enabling the Viola-Jones detector to operate in real time.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for O(1) property, 1 mark for relevance to real-time speed.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the three types of Haar-like features used in the Viola-Jones detector.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three canonical Haar-like feature types are:

1. **Two-rectangle (Edge) feature:** Computes the difference between the sums of two horizontally or vertically adjacent equal-sized rectangles. Captures edge-like intensity transitions (e.g., eye-to-cheek boundary). *Formula:* $f = S_{\text{white}} - S_{\text{black}}$

2. **Three-rectangle (Line) feature:** Computes the difference between the sum in the center rectangle and the sum of the two outer rectangles. Captures line-like contrasts (e.g., bright nose bridge between darker eye regions). *Formula:* $f = S_{\text{center}} - (S_{\text{left}} + S_{\text{right}})/2$

3. **Four-rectangle (Diagonal) feature:** Computes the difference between sums of two diagonal pairs of rectangles in a 2×2 checkerboard pattern. Captures diagonal gradients and finer texture contrasts. *Formula:* $f = (S_{TL} + S_{BR}) - (S_{TR} + S_{BL})$

*(Award 1 mark per feature type with example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B Module-Internal-Choice questions are worth 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B, not both.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**"Explain the Viola-Jones face detection framework in detail. With suitable diagrams, describe the Haar-like features, the Integral Image computation, the AdaBoost training algorithm, and the cascade of classifiers. Show how the cascade achieves real-time performance."**

#### (a) **Haar-like Features & Integral Image** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**1. Haar-like Features (2 marks):**
Haar-like features are rectangular kernels inspired by the Haar wavelet basis. They encode *micro-contrast patterns* typical of human faces. The base set consists of three feature types (edge, line, diagonal), each parameterized by position, scale, and aspect ratio within the 24×24 detection window. The feature value is:
$$f(x) = \sum_{i \in \text{white}} I(i) - \sum_{j \in \text{black}} I(j)$$

The total number of such features in a 24×24 window is approximately 180,000. This exhaustively large set is the reason an intelligent feature *selection* step is required.

**2. Integral Image (3 marks):**
The integral image transforms the problem of computing rectangular sums into a constant-time operation. Defined as:
$$ii(x, y) = \sum_{x' \le x,\, y' \le y} I(x', y')$$

It can be computed in a single left-to-right, top-to-bottom scan via the recurrence:
$$ii(x, y) = I(x, y) + ii(x-1, y) + ii(x, y-1) - ii(x-1, y-1)$$

with $ii(-1, \cdot) = ii(\cdot, -1) = 0$. Once the integral image is built, the sum of intensities in *any* rectangle with corners $P_1$ (top-left), $P_2$ (top-right), $P_3$ (bottom-left), $P_4$ (bottom-right) is:
$$S = ii(P_4) - ii(P_3) - ii(P_2) + ii(P_1)$$

This requires only 4 lookups and 3 additions, reducing per-feature evaluation to O(1).

**3. Diagrammatic representation (2 marks):** *(See Section 4.1 and 4.2 for canonical schematic.)*

#### (b) **AdaBoost Algorithm & Cascade of Classifiers** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**1. AdaBoost Algorithm (4 marks):**
AdaBoost is an *adaptive boosting* meta-algorithm that combines $T$ weak classifiers into one strong classifier.

- **Initialization:** $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$ for each training sample $i = 1, \dots, N$.
- **For $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:**
  - Normalize weights so that $\sum_i w_i^{(t)} = 1$.
  - For each Haar feature $j$, train a weak classifier $h_j$ (a simple decision stump) and compute its weighted error:
    $$\epsilon_j = \sum_{i:\, h_j(x_i) \ne y_i} w_i^{(t)}$$
  - Choose the weak classifier $h_t$ with the lowest $\epsilon_t$.
  - Compute its voting weight:
    $$\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \right)$$
  - Update sample weights:
    $$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$$
    (Misclassified samples get exponentially larger weights so the next weak learner focuses on them.)

- **Final strong classifier:**
  $$H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x) \right)$$

**2. Cascade of Classifiers (3 marks):**
The cascade is a sequence of AdaBoost-trained stages with progressively increasing complexity. A sub-window must pass *all* stages to be declared a face; failure at *any* stage triggers immediate rejection. With $K$ stages, the overall detection rate and false positive rate are:

$$D = \prod_{i=1}^{K} d_i, \qquad F = \prod_{i=1}^{K} f_i$$

For example, with 38 stages of $d_i = 0.995$ and $f_i = 0.5$:
- $D \approx 0.83$ (high detection)
- $F \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-12}$ (virtually zero false positives)

The key engineering insight: since most sub-windows in a typical image are non-faces, the *first 1–2 stages* reject 50–80% of them using only a handful of features. This is what makes the algorithm real-time.

*(Diagrammatic representation in Section 4.4.)*

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**"Discuss the limitations of the Viola-Jones face detection framework. Explain how Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) combined with SVM and modern CNN-based detectors (e.g., MTCNN, YOLO) overcome these limitations. Provide a comparative analysis."**

#### (a) **Limitations of Viola-Jones & HOG + SVM Solution** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**1. Limitations of Viola-Jones (3 marks):**
- **Frontal-view bias:** Designed for near-frontal, upright faces. Performance degrades sharply for profile views, extreme yaw/pitch angles, or in-plane rotations.
- **Occlusion sensitivity:** Partially occluded faces (mask, hand, glasses) are frequently missed.
- **Lighting dependence:** Although the integral image helps, severe illumination changes and shadows can produce false detections.
- **Hand-crafted features:** Haar features are *engineered*, not *learned* — they cannot adapt to data distribution shifts.
- **Scale handling:** Multi-scale detection via image pyramid is computationally expensive.
- **Single-class extension:** The original framework is optimized for one object class; multi-class extension requires training many cascades.

**2. HOG + SVM Approach (4 marks):**
- **Feature (2 marks):** The Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) descriptor divides the image into small connected cells and computes a histogram of gradient directions (or edge orientations) within each cell. Normalized across larger spatial blocks, HOG captures *edge structure* that is highly discriminative for human silhouettes and faces.
- **Classifier (1 mark):** A linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) finds the optimal hyperplane separating face and non-face HOG feature vectors in high-dimensional space.
- **Advantages (1 mark):** HOG is invariant to small geometric and photometric transformations; combined with a sliding-window SVM (and later Deformable Parts Models), it handles moderate pose variation better than Haar.

#### (b) **Deep Learning Detectors (MTCNN, YOLO) & Comparative Analysis** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**1. CNN-based detectors (4 marks):**
- **MTCNN (Multi-task Cascaded CNN, Zhang et al., 2016):** A three-stage CNN pipeline (P-Net → R-Net → O-Net) that performs joint face detection and landmark localization. Each stage progressively refines bounding boxes and rejects easy negatives — analogous in spirit to the Viola-Jones cascade, but using *learned* features. Robust to pose and occlusion.
- **YOLO / SSD-style general detectors:** Treat face detection as a generic object detection problem, predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities in a single forward pass. Excellent speed–accuracy tradeoff on GPUs.

**2. Comparative Analysis Table (2 marks):**

| Property | Viola-Jones | HOG + SVM | MTCNN / YOLO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feature type | Hand-crafted Haar | Hand-crafted gradient | Learned convolutional |
| Training data | Thousands | Thousands | Millions |
| Pose robustness | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Real-time | Yes (CPU) | Moderate | Yes (GPU) |
| Accuracy | 80–90% (frontal) | 85–92% | 96–99% |
| Hardware | CPU | CPU | GPU (typically) |
| Year | 2001 | 2005 | 2016+ |

**3. Engineering takeaway (1 mark):** Modern systems often *cascade* a fast Viola-Jones stage for region proposals and a deep CNN for verification — a hybrid of classical and learned approaches. This is the prevailing industrial pattern as of 2024.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary conditions** of the integral image ($ii(-1, y) = 0$, $ii(x, -1) = 0$) costs 1 mark easily.
> 2. **Confusing Detection with Recognition** in definition questions — examiners explicitly test this distinction. Always state: *Detection = where; Recognition = whose.*
> 3. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$** in the IoU formula (or just using raw `|`) — both break the marking script's parser; use `\vert`.
> 4. **Omitting the parity term $p_j$** in the weak classifier formula $h_j(x)$ — many students write the stump without it, losing a mark.
> 5. **Stating AdaBoost is a "boosting algorithm"** without naming it *Adaptive Boosting* and citing *Freund & Schapire, 1995* — partial credit only.
> 6. **Confusing the cascade's $d_i$ and $f_i$ products**: $D = \prod d_i$ (faces passing *all* stages), $F = \prod f_i$ (non-faces *mistakenly* passing all stages). Reversing these is a classic trap.
> 7. **Failing to mention Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** when describing the detection pipeline — many overlapping detections must be merged.
> 8. **No diagrams in Part B answers**: Even a simple hand-drawn cascade or Haar feature sketch earns 1–2 presentation marks. Always include at least one schematic.
> 9. **Writing code or pseudo-code in theory questions**: KTU 2024 Scheme theory papers (especially for non-CS branches) deduct marks for unrequested implementation snippets. Stick to equations, algorithms as numbered steps, and block diagrams.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Object Detection** = Classification + Localization; **Face Detection** is its specialized instance.
- ✅ **Viola-Jones** (2001) is the *first* practical real-time face detector and is the KTU syllabus anchor.
