- ✅ The framework rests on **three pillars**: (1) **Haar-like features**, (2) **Integral Image**, (3) **AdaBoost**, deployed within a **(4) Cascade of Classifiers**.
- ✅ The 24×24 base window contains **~180,000 Haar features**; only **~6,000** are selected by AdaBoost.
- ✅ The **Integral Image** is computed once per image (cost $O(MN)$); every subsequent rectangular sum is **O(1)** using 4 lookups.
- ✅ **AdaBoost** is *Adaptive Boosting* (Freund & Schapire, 1995); each weak learner is a single-feature decision stump with parity $p_j \in \{-1, +1\}$.
- ✅ The strong classifier is the sign of a **weighted sum of weak classifiers**; weight is $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln[(1-\epsilon_t)/\epsilon_t]$.
- ✅ The **Cascade** rejects non-faces early; overall $D = \prod d_i$, $F = \prod f_i$.
- ✅ Limitations of Viola-Jones: frontal-view bias, occlusion sensitivity, hand-crafted features, single-class optimization.
- ✅ Modern extensions: **LBP features** (illumination-robust), **HOG + SVM** (moderate pose), **CNN-based detectors** like **MTCNN** and **YOLO** (state-of-the-art).
- ✅ Key performance metrics: **Precision, Recall, FPR, F1-score, IoU, FPS**.
- ✅ Always remember: **Face Detection ≠ Face Recognition** — and examiners test this.
- ✅ Standard formula to memorize: $S = ii(D) - ii(B) - ii(C) + ii(A)$ — for any rectangle.
- ✅ Always draw at least one **schematic** (Haar feature, integral-image lookup, or cascade) in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Use `\vert` (not `|`) in markdown tables for absolute values to avoid table-formatting failures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Object Detection - Precision vs. Recall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** General Object Detection - Precision vs. Recall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **General Object Detection** under the KTU 2024 Machine Vision syllabus, **Precision vs. Recall** refers to a pair of fundamental **Information Retrieval and Statistical Classification metrics** used to quantitatively evaluate the performance of an object detector. These metrics are derived from the **Confusion Matrix** (also called the Error Matrix) and measure the trade-off between the *correctness* and *completeness* of detected object instances (bounding boxes + class labels) in an image.

The terminology aligns with **Pascal VOC** (Visual Object Classes) and **COCO** (Common Objects in Context) benchmark standards adopted universally in machine vision research.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Object Detection evaluation does NOT rely on a single scalar accuracy. It is a *multi-metric regime* requiring simultaneous analysis of Precision, Recall, IoU, and mean Average Precision (mAP). Students must understand the *behavioral trade-off*, not just memorize formulas.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **fishing inspector at a seafood market**, and your job is to classify fish on a conveyor belt as either *Salmon* (the target object) or *Not Salmon*.

- **Precision** answers the question: *"Of all the fish I flagged as Salmon, how many were actually Salmon?"*
  - If you flag **10 fish** as Salmon, but only **7** are truly Salmon, your Precision is **7/10 = 70%**. You were "trusted but noisy" — high precision means **few false alarms**.
- **Recall** answers the question: *"Of all the Salmon on the conveyor belt, how many did I actually catch?"*
  - If there were **20 Salmon** passing by, but you only flagged **7** correctly, your Recall is **7/20 = 35%**. You were "thorough but missing" — high recall means **few misses**.

**The Catch (Pun Intended):** You cannot maximize both simultaneously. To catch every Salmon (↑ Recall), you must lower your detection threshold and risk flagging Mackerel (↑ False Positives → ↓ Precision). Conversely, to be 100% sure each flag is correct (↑ Precision), you will miss many Salmon (↓ Recall). This inherent **tension** is the heart of Precision vs. Recall.

## 1.3 The Four Cardinal Outcomes (Standard Metrics in **Bold**)

In any binary detection problem, every prediction falls into one of four cells of the **Confusion Matrix**:

| Predicted → / Actual ↓ | Predicted **Positive** (Object) | Predicted **Negative** (Background) |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** (Object Exists) | **True Positive (TP)** | **False Negative (FN)** — Missed |
| **Actual Negative** (Background) | **False Positive (FP)** — False Alarm | **True Negative (TN)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **True Negative (TN)** is intentionally ignored in object detection evaluation. Detecting "background as background" across millions of pixels is trivial and uninformative. Hence, Precision and Recall in detection are computed **using only TP, FP, and FN**.

## 1.4 Formal Definitions of Precision and Recall

Let **TP** = True Positives, **FP** = False Positives, **FN** = False Negatives.

**Precision (P)** — also called *Positive Predictive Value*:
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall (R)** — also called *Sensitivity* or *True Positive Rate (TPR)*:
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A bounding box is considered a **TP** only if its **Intersection over Union (IoU)** with the ground-truth box exceeds a threshold (commonly **0.5** for PASCAL VOC, and averaged over **0.5 to 0.95** in steps of 0.05 for MS COCO).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Detector Produces TP/FP/FN

The decision pipeline of a generic object detector (e.g., Faster R-CNN, YOLO, SSD) for evaluation purposes works as follows:

1. **Forward Pass:** The detector processes the input image and outputs a set of candidate detections. Each detection is a tuple: *(bounding_box, class_label, confidence_score)*.

2. **Confidence Thresholding (τ):** All detections with `confidence_score ≥ τ` are retained. Those below are discarded (treated as background → TN).

3. **Greedy Matching (Hungarian Algorithm):** Each retained prediction is matched to a ground-truth box using **IoU**. If `IoU ≥ threshold (e.g., 0.5)`, it is a candidate TP.

4. **Class Label Check:** If the predicted class matches the ground-truth class, the match is confirmed as a **TP**. Otherwise, it is an **FP** (wrong class on a correct box).

5. **Unmatched Ground Truths:** Any ground-truth object not matched to any detection is counted as an **FN**.

6. **Aggregation:** Precision and Recall are computed over the **entire dataset**, aggregated by class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters:** Understanding this pipeline reveals *where* the trade-off knob lives. By varying **τ** (confidence threshold), the designer explicitly trades Precision for Recall, and vice versa.

## 2.2 The Precision–Recall Trade-Off Explained

- **Lowering τ** (e.g., from 0.9 → 0.3): More detections survive → more TP captured (↑ Recall) but more spurious detections slip in (↑ FP → ↓ Precision).
- **Raising τ** (e.g., from 0.3 → 0.9): Only the most confident detections remain → fewer FP (↑ Precision) but real objects get filtered out (↑ FN → ↓ Recall).

This monotonic relationship is the foundation of the **Precision–Recall (PR) Curve**.

## 2.3 Derived Composite Metrics

Beyond raw P and R, KTU expects familiarity with these composite metrics:

- **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of P and R):** Captures balance; penalizes extreme imbalances.
- **Average Precision (AP):** Area under the PR curve (single class).
- **mean Average Precision (mAP):** Mean of AP across all object classes (primary benchmark metric).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Range** | **Engineering Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Precision (P)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Quality of positive predictions — *false alarm control* |
| **Recall (R)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Completeness of detection — *miss control* |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced single-scalar summary |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Not standard for detection (TN ambiguous) |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Background false-alarm rate |
| **Intersection over Union (IoU)** | $\dfrac{\text{Area of Overlap}}{\text{Area of Union}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Geometric box-matching criterion |
| **Average Precision (AP)** | $\int_{0}^{1} P(R)\, dR$ | $[0, 1]$ | Area under PR curve (per class) |
| **mAP** | $\dfrac{1}{N_{c}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{c}} AP_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Mean AP over all classes (primary metric) |
| **Precision-Recall Break-Even** | $P = R$ | scalar | Operating point where they are equal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to compute P, R, F1, and mAP from a *given* set of TP/FP/FN values. Always reduce fractions to lowest terms. Also remember that **mAP@0.5** (PASCAL convention) and **mAP@[0.5:0.95]** (COCO convention) are different and may be asked explicitly.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Recall must be *near-100%* — missing a pedestrian (FN) is catastrophic. Precision can tolerate minor false alarms.
- **Medical Imaging (Tumor Detection):** High Recall is critical — missed tumors are fatal. Radiologists review FP cases.
- **Industrial Defect Detection:** High Precision is preferred — false stops on a production line cost thousands per minute.
- **Surveillance / Retail Analytics:** Balanced F1-score is desirable.
- **Document AI / OCR:** High Precision matters — wrong text insertion is destructive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — From Raw Detections to mAP

**Problem Statement:**
A face-detection model produces the following ranked detections on a single image containing **5 ground-truth faces**. Each row is sorted by **descending confidence**.

| Rank | Confidence | IoU with GT | Predicted Class | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.98 | 0.92 | Face | TP |
| 2 | 0.91 | 0.71 | Face | TP |
| 3 | 0.85 | 0.61 | Face | TP |
| 4 | 0.78 | 0.31 | Face | FP (IoU < 0.5) |
| 5 | 0.72 | 0.88 | Face | TP |
| 6 | 0.65 | 0.45 | Face | FP (IoU < 0.5) |
| 7 | 0.60 | 0.79 | Face | TP |

Total ground-truth faces = **5**.

### Step 1: Cumulative Outcome Counting

We walk through the ranked list. Each detection can only match ONE unique ground-truth (greedy assignment by highest IoU, ties broken by rank).

**Rank 1:** IoU = 0.92 ≥ 0.5, class correct. Matches GT #1. → **TP**
Cumulative: TP = 1, FP = 0, FN_remaining = 4.

**Rank 2:** IoU = 0.71 ≥ 0.5, class correct. Matches GT #2. → **TP**
Cumulative: TP = 2, FP = 0, FN_remaining = 3.

**Rank 3:** IoU = 0.61 ≥ 0.5, class correct. Matches GT #3. → **TP**
Cumulative: TP = 3, FP = 0, FN_remaining = 2.

**Rank 4:** IoU = 0.31 < 0.5 (does not overlap sufficiently). → **FP**
Cumulative: TP = 3, FP = 1, FN_remaining = 2.

**Rank 5:** IoU = 0.88 ≥ 0.5, class correct. Matches GT #4. → **TP**
Cumulative: TP = 4, FP = 1, FN_remaining = 1.

**Rank 6:** IoU = 0.45 < 0.5. → **FP**
Cumulative: TP = 4, FP = 2, FN_remaining = 1.

**Rank 7:** IoU = 0.79 ≥ 0.5, class correct. Matches GT #5. → **TP**
Cumulative: TP = 5, FP = 2, FN_remaining = 0.

Final counts:
$$TP = 5, \quad FP = 2, \quad FN = 0$$

### Step 2: Compute Precision and Recall at Each Rank

Using the cumulative counts, we compute $P_k$ and $R_k$ at each detection rank $k$. Here, the total positives $P_{total} = 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \frac{1}{1+0} = 1.000, & R_1 &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.200 \\
P_2 &= \frac{2}{2+0} = 1.000, & R_2 &= \frac{2}{5} = 0.400 \\
P_3 &= \frac{3}{3+0} = 1.000, & R_3 &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.600 \\
P_4 &= \frac{3}{3+1} = 0.750, & R_4 &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.600 \\
P_5 &= \frac{4}{4+1} = 0.800, & R_5 &= \frac{4}{5} = 0.800 \\
P_6 &= \frac{4}{4+2} = 0.667, & R_6 &= \frac{4}{5} = 0.800 \\
P_7 &= \frac{5}{5+2} = 0.714, & R_7 &= \frac{5}{5} = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Average Precision (AP) — 11-Point Interpolation (PASCAL VOC Legacy)

In the **11-point interpolation** scheme, AP is the mean of the maximum Precision values at Recall levels $r \in \{0.0, 0.1, 0.2, \dots, 1.0\}$:

$$
AP = \frac{1}{11} \sum_{r \in \{0.0,\, 0.1,\, \dots,\, 1.0\}} P_{\text{interp}}(r)
$$

where $P_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max_{r' \geq r} P(r')$.

Computing each interpolated Precision:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{interp}}(0.0) &= \max\{1.000,\, 1.000,\, 1.000,\, 0.750,\, 0.800,\, 0.667,\, 0.714\} = 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.1) &= 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.2) &= \max\{1.000,\, 1.000,\, 1.000,\, 0.750,\, 0.800,\, 0.667,\, 0.714\} = 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.3) &= 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.4) &= \max\{1.000,\, 1.000,\, 1.000,\, 0.800,\, 0.667,\, 0.714\} = 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.5) &= 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.6) &= \max\{0.750,\, 0.800,\, 0.667,\, 0.714\} = 0.800 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.7) &= \max\{0.800,\, 0.667,\, 0.714\} = 0.800 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.8) &= \max\{0.800,\, 0.667,\, 0.714\} = 0.800 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.9) &= \max\{0.667,\, 0.714\} = 0.714 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(1.0) &= 0.714
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \frac{1}{11} \big(1.000 + 1.000 + 1.000 + 1.000 + 1.000 + 1.000 \\
&\quad +\, 0.800 + 0.800 + 0.800 + 0.714 + 0.714\big) \\
&= \frac{1}{11} \big(6.000 + 2.400 + 1.428\big) \\
&= \frac{9.828}{11} \\
&= 0.8935
\end{aligned}
$$

So $AP \approx 0.894$ or **89.35%**.

### Step 4: Compute mAP Across Classes (Hypothetical Extension)

Suppose this face class is one of $N_c = 3$ classes with AP values:
- Faces: $AP_1 = 0.894$
- Cars: $AP_2 = 0.762$
- Traffic Signs: $AP_3 = 0.811$

$$
\begin{aligned}
mAP &= \frac{1}{N_c} \sum_{i=1}^{N_c} AP_i \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \big(0.894 + 0.762 + 0.811\big) \\
&= \frac{2.467}{3} \\
&= 0.8223
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $mAP \approx 0.822$ or **82.23%**.

### Step 5: Compute F1-Score at Final Operating Point

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{final}} &= \frac{5}{7} = 0.714 \\
R_{\text{final}} &= \frac{5}{5} = 1.000 \\
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.714 \cdot 1.000}{0.714 + 1.000} \\
&= \frac{1.428}{1.714} = 0.8333
\end{aligned}
$$

So $F_1 = 0.833$ or **83.33%** at the final operating point.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Scenario** | **High-Precision Regime** | **High-Recall Regime** |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Threshold τ** | High (e.g., 0.9) | Low (e.g., 0.3) |
| **TP** | Lower | Higher |
| **FP** | Very Low | Higher |
| **FN** | Higher | Very Low |
| **Use Case** | Surveillance alarms, OCR | Cancer screening, pedestrian safety |
| **Risk Tolerance** | Misses acceptable; false alarms costly | False alarms acceptable; misses catastrophic |
| **Curve Behavior** | P high near R = 0; falls steeply | R extends to 1.0; P drops at the tail |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Detection → Classification → Confusion Matrix Flow

The following diagram illustrates the full pipeline from raw detector output to metric computation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Object Detector Forward Pass]
    B --> C[Candidate Boxes with Confidence Scores]
    C --> D{Confidence >= Threshold tau}
    D -->|No| E[Discarded - TN equivalent]
    D -->|Yes| F[Compute IoU with Ground Truth]
    F --> G{IoU >= IoU threshold}
    G -->|No| H[False Positive FP]
    G -->|Yes| I{Class Label Matches}
    I -->|No| J[False Positive FP wrong class]
    I -->|Yes| K[True Positive TP]
    L[Unmatched Ground Truths] --> M[False Negative FN]
    H --> N[Aggregate Counts]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Precision and Recall]
    O --> P[Plot PR Curve]
    P --> Q[Compute AP and mAP]
```

## 4.2 Precision vs. Recall Trade-Off Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threshold tau Low] --> B[Many detections survive]
    B --> C[High Recall]
    C --> D[More False Positives]
    D --> E[Low Precision]
    F[Threshold tau High] --> G[Few detections survive]
    G --> H[High Precision]
    H --> I[More False Negatives]
    I --> J[Low Recall]
    E -.->|Trade Off| H
    J -.->|Trade Off| C
```

## 4.3 PR Curve Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Recall axis: 0 to 1] --> Y[Precision axis: 0 to 1]
    Y --> Z1[Ideal Detector: P remains 1.0 across all R]
    Y --> Z2[Realistic Detector: P decreases as R grows]
    Y --> Z3[Random Detector: Diagonal decay toward baseline]
    Z2 --> W1[Compute Area Under Curve]
    W1 --> W2[Average Precision AP per class]
    W2 --> W3[Mean Average Precision mAP across classes]
```

## 4.4 Operating Point Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Application Domain] --> B{Application Type}
    B -->|Safety Critical| C[Maximize Recall]
    B -->|Cost Sensitive| D[Maximize Precision]
    B -->|Balanced| E[Maximize F1 Score]
    C --> F[Set Low Threshold tau]
    D --> G[Set High Threshold tau]
    E --> H[Search tau that maximizes 2PR over P plus R]
    F --> I[Accept More False Alarms]
    G --> J[Accept More Misses]
    H --> K[Trade Off Controlled]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Precision and Recall in the context of object detection. Explain why True Negatives are typically ignored in detection metrics.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Precision** is the fraction of correct positive detections among all detections the model claims as positive, computed as $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$. It quantifies how *trustworthy* a positive prediction is.

**Recall** is the fraction of ground-truth positive objects that the model successfully detected, computed as $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$. It quantifies how *complete* the detection is.

**True Negatives are ignored** because in detection, the background constitutes the vast majority of image pixels/regions. Counting every correctly-rejected background patch as a TN would overwhelm the metric and render Precision/Recall uninformative. Hence, detection metrics operate on a *truncated* confusion matrix considering only TP, FP, and FN.

---

### **Q2. What is the F1-Score and why is the harmonic mean preferred over the arithmetic mean when combining Precision and Recall?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, defined as:
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

The **harmonic mean** is preferred because it **penalizes extreme imbalances** between P and R. For instance, if $P = 0.99$ and $R = 0.01$, the arithmetic mean gives 0.5 (misleadingly high), while the harmonic mean gives:
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.99 \cdot 0.01}{1.00} = 0.0198$$

This correctly reflects that the system is essentially useless despite high precision, because Recall is nearly zero. The harmonic mean only yields a high value when both P and R are simultaneously high, which matches the engineering intuition that a useful detector must be both *accurate* and *complete*.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions with Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define Precision, Recall, F1-Score, IoU, AP, and mAP. State the formulas for each and explain the relationship between the PR curve and Average Precision. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle detector is tested on an image dataset. For the class *"Car"*, the detector produces the following ranked detections (IoU threshold = 0.5). There are **4 ground-truth cars** in the image.

| Rank | Confidence | IoU | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.96 | 0.85 | Car |
| 2 | 0.88 | 0.62 | Car |
| 3 | 0.81 | 0.48 | Car |
| 4 | 0.74 | 0.79 | Car |
| 5 | 0.69 | 0.41 | Car |
| 6 | 0.60 | 0.71 | Car |

Compute the **Precision, Recall, F1-Score at each rank**, and the **Average Precision using 11-point interpolation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**1. Definitions and Formulas (4 marks):**

- **Precision:** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall:** $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score:** $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P + R}$
- **IoU:** $\dfrac{\text{Area of Overlap}}{\text{Area of Union}}$ between predicted and ground-truth box
- **AP:** Area under the Precision–Recall curve for a single class, often computed via 11-point or all-point interpolation
- **mAP:** $\dfrac{1}{N_c} \sum_{i=1}^{N_c} AP_i$ averaged over $N_c$ classes

**2. PR Curve and AP Relationship (3 marks):**

The PR curve plots Precision (y-axis) against Recall (x-axis) as the detection confidence threshold τ varies from 0 to 1. Lowering τ includes more detections, increasing R but generally decreasing P. The **Average Precision** is the area under this curve:

$$AP = \int_0^1 P(R)\, dR$$

In the 11-point PASCAL VOC interpolation:
$$AP = \frac{1}{11}\sum_{r \in \{0, 0.1, \dots, 1\}} P_{\text{interp}}(r)$$

A perfect detector has AP = 1.0 (a rectangle of area 1). A random detector has AP ≈ 0.5 (triangular decay). The PR curve visually summarizes the entire operating envelope, while AP condenses it into a single scalar for benchmark ranking.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Classify each detection using IoU ≥ 0.5 rule:**

| Rank | IoU | IoU ≥ 0.5? | Outcome | TP (cum) | FP (cum) | FN (remaining) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.85 | Yes | TP | 1 | 0 | 3 |
| 2 | 0.62 | Yes | TP | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| 3 | 0.48 | No | FP | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| 4 | 0.79 | Yes | TP | 3 | 1 | 1 |
| 5 | 0.41 | No | FP | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| 6 | 0.71 | Yes | TP | 4 | 2 | 0 |

Total GT = 4 → at the end, all 4 matched → **FN = 0**.

**Step 2: Compute Precision, Recall, F1 at each rank:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank 1: } & P = \tfrac{1}{1} = 1.000, \quad R = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.250, \quad F_1 = 0.400 \\
\text{Rank 2: } & P = \tfrac{2}{2} = 1.000, \quad R = \tfrac{2}{4} = 0.500, \quad F_1 = 0.667 \\
\text{Rank 3: } & P = \tfrac{2}{3} = 0.667, \quad R = \tfrac{2}{4} = 0.500, \quad F_1 = 0.571 \\
\text{Rank 4: } & P = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.750, \quad R = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.750, \quad F_1 = 0.750 \\
\text{Rank 5: } & P = \tfrac{3}{5} = 0.600, \quad R = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.750, \quad F_1 = 0.667 \\
\text{Rank 6: } & P = \tfrac{4}{6} = 0.667, \quad R = \tfrac{4}{4} = 1.000, \quad F_1 = 0.800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: 11-Point Interpolated AP:**

Interpolated Precision at each recall level $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{interp}}(0.0) &= 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.1) &= 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.2) &= 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.3) &= 1.000 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.4) &= \max\{0.667,\, 0.750,\, 0.600,\, 0.667\} = 0.750 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.5) &= 0.750 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.6) &= \max\{0.750,\, 0.600,\, 0.667\} = 0.750 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.7) &= \max\{0.750,\, 0.600,\, 0.667\} = 0.750 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.8) &= \max\{0.667\} = 0.667 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(0.9) &= \max\{0.667\} = 0.667 \\
P_{\text{interp}}(1.0) &= 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \frac{1}{11}\big(1.000 + 1.000 + 1.000 + 1.000 + 0.750 + 0.750 \\
&\quad +\, 0.750 + 0.750 + 0.750 + 0.667 + 0.667\big) \\
&= \frac{9.084}{11} \\
&\approx 0.826
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $AP_{\text{Car}} \approx 0.826$ or **82.6%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the Precision–Recall trade-off in object detection with a real-world engineering analogy. Explain how the **confidence threshold τ** influences both metrics, and describe the role of **IoU threshold** in distinguishing TP from FP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tumor-detection AI produces the following results on a test image batch: **TP = 80, FP = 20, FN = 40**. Calculate **Precision, Recall, F1-Score**, and explain which operating point the hospital should choose given that missing a malignant tumor (FN) is **10 times costlier** than a false alarm (FP). Propose a weighted F-β score that reflects this priority and recompute. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**1. Trade-Off Analogy (3 marks):**

Consider a **TSA security scanner** at an airport. The goal is to detect prohibited items (weapons, explosives). The scanner can be tuned to either:
- **High-Precision Mode (Strict):** Only flag obvious weapons → very few false alarms (low FP), but some concealed weapons slip through (high FN → low Recall).
- **High-Recall Mode (Sensitive):** Flag anything suspicious → almost no weapon slips through (high Recall), but innocent travelers get patted down constantly (high FP → low Precision).

Neither extreme is desirable. The optimal setting balances both, just as in medical diagnosis where missing a cancer (FN) is fatal but over-biopsying benign tissue (FP) is also harmful.

**2. Role of Confidence Threshold τ (2 marks):**

The confidence threshold τ directly controls the Precision–Recall trade-off:
- **Lower τ** → more candidate detections pass the filter → ↑ TP (Recall ↑) but also ↑ FP (Precision ↓).
- **Higher τ** → only highly confident detections survive → ↓ FP (Precision ↑) but also ↓ TP, with missed objects becoming ↑ FN (Recall ↓).

By sweeping τ from 0 to 1, the entire PR curve is traced.

**3. Role of IoU Threshold (2 marks):**

The IoU threshold (typically 0.5) determines whether a predicted bounding box is "geometrically correct enough" to qualify as a TP:
- **IoU ≥ threshold + correct class** → **TP**
- **IoU < threshold** → **FP** (the box is in the right vicinity but poorly localized)
- **Correct box but wrong class** → **FP** (classification error with localization success)

A higher IoU threshold (e.g., 0.75) is stricter and demands tighter localization, generally yielding lower mAP. COCO uses averaged mAP across IoU ∈ [0.5, 0.95] to reward both detection *and* precise localization.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** TP = 80, FP = 20, FN = 40.

**Step 1: Standard Metrics (3 marks):**

$$
P = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 \quad (80\%)
$$

$$
R = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} \approx 0.6667 \quad (66.67\%)
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.6667}{0.80 + 0.6667} = \frac{1.0667}{1.4667} \approx 0.7273
$$

**Step 2: Engineering Decision (2 marks):**

The hospital is in a **safety-critical, asymmetric-cost domain**. Missing a malignant tumor (FN) costs a human life; a false alarm (FP) costs a follow-up biopsy. The cost ratio is:

$$\text{Cost}(FN) : \text{Cost}(FP) = 10 : 1$$

Hence, **Recall must be prioritized** — the system should be tuned to favor detecting every possible tumor, even at the cost of more false positives. A confidence threshold lower than the F1-optimal value should be selected.

**Step 3: Weighted F-β Score (2 marks):**

The general **Fβ score** weights Recall β times more than Precision:

$$
F_\beta = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}
$$

Since **Recall is 10× more important than Precision**, but the β parameter squares the weight, we choose $\beta^2 = 10$, so $\beta = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_\beta &= \frac{(1 + 10) \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.6667}{10 \cdot 0.80 + 0.6667} \\
&= \frac{11 \cdot 0.5334}{8.6667} \\
&= \frac{5.867}{8.667} \\
&\approx 0.6770
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The weighted score $F_\beta \approx 0.677$ is *lower* than $F_1 \approx 0.727$, correctly penalizing the system's moderate Recall. This tells the hospital engineering team that despite decent Precision, Recall must be improved (e.g., by lowering τ or augmenting training data) before deployment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to ignore TN in detection metrics.** Including TN in Precision/Recall formulas is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing IoU threshold (geometric) with confidence threshold τ (statistical).** They serve different purposes; examiners test this distinction.
> 3. **Not computing cumulative TP/FP at each rank.** The PR curve requires *running* cumulative counts, not just the final aggregate.
> 4. **Mistaking arithmetic mean for harmonic mean** when deriving F1.
> 5. **Skipping the $\max_{r' \geq r}$ step** in 11-point interpolation — must take maximum Precision for all Recall ≥ current level.
> 6. **Not stating units or ranges** for P, R, F1 (all dimensionless, range [0, 1]).
> 7. **Incorrectly handling the case P + R = 0** in F1 — must define F1 = 0 in that degenerate case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Precision = TP / (TP + FP)** — measures *false-alarm control*.
- [x] **Recall = TP / (TP + FN)** — measures *miss control*.
- [x] **TN is excluded** in object-detection metrics because background dominates.
- [x] **IoU threshold** (commonly 0.5) gates the TP/FP decision based on box overlap.
- [x] **Confidence threshold τ** sweeps the PR curve; lower τ → ↑ R, ↓ P.
- [x] **PR Curve** plots Precision (y) vs. Recall (x) over τ ∈ [0, 1].
- [x] **Average Precision (AP)** = area under PR curve, single class.
- [x] **mAP** = mean of AP across all classes — primary benchmark metric.
- [x] **F1-Score** = harmonic mean, penalizes imbalance between P and R.
- [x] **Fβ-Score** generalizes F1 by weighting Recall β² times more than Precision — use when false negatives costlier than false positives (medical, safety).
- [x] **11-point interpolation** (PASCAL VOC) vs. **all-point interpolation** (COCO) are two valid AP computation methods.
- [x] **mAP@0.5** is PASCAL convention; **mAP@[0.5:0.95]** is the stricter COCO convention.
- [x] A perfect detector achieves **AP = 1.0**; a random detector achieves **AP ≈ fraction of positives** (low for object detection).
- [x] **Greedy / Hungarian matching** assigns each GT box to at most one detection.
- [x] **Engineering trade-off**: high-stakes safety domains (medical, automotive) → maximize Recall; cost-sensitive domains (OCR, surveillance alerts) → maximize Precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Semantic Segmentation - Basic Understanding Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semantic Segmentation** is a foundational computer vision task in which every single pixel in an input image is assigned a semantic class label drawn from a predefined set of categories (e.g., road, car, pedestrian, building, sky, vegetation). Unlike object detection, semantic segmentation does **not** distinguish between different instances of the same class — all pixels labeled "car" are treated as a single collective category, regardless of how many individual cars are present in the scene.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, semantic segmentation is positioned within the broader family of **dense prediction tasks** in deep learning, where the output spatial resolution matches the input image resolution and every pixel receives a prediction. Formally, given an input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$, the semantic segmentation model $f_{\theta}$ produces an output segmentation map:

$$S = f_{\theta}(I), \quad S \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$$

where $H$ and $W$ are the height and width of the image, and $C$ is the number of semantic classes. Each spatial location $(i, j)$ in $S$ contains a $C$-dimensional probability vector, and the predicted class is $\arg\max_{c} S_{i,j,c}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coloring Book View"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine giving a child a black-and-white photograph of a busy city street and asking them to color it.** The child is told: "All cars are **red**, all roads are **gray**, the sky is **blue**, trees are **green**, and buildings are **brown**." The child doesn't care about which specific car is which — every car simply becomes red. Every road pixel becomes gray. The sky, no matter how many clouds it contains, becomes blue. The output is a fully colored version of the image where the **color** encodes the **semantic class** of every region. This is exactly what semantic segmentation does, only mathematically and with much higher precision.

## 1.3 Hierarchical Position Among Vision Tasks

Semantic segmentation sits at the most granular (pixel-level) end of the image understanding spectrum. It is essential for a KTU student to clearly distinguish the following four canonical vision tasks:

| Task Level | Granularity | Output Type | Distinguishes Instances? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image Classification** | Whole image | Single class label | No |
| **Object Detection** | Object-level | Bounding boxes + class | Yes (per object) |
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Pixel-level | Class label per pixel | **No** |
| **Instance Segmentation** | Pixel-level | Class label + instance ID per pixel | **Yes** |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid:** If two people are standing next to each other, semantic segmentation labels **all** person-pixels as "person" (one color). Instance segmentation labels one person as "person-1" (yellow) and the other as "person-2" (purple).

## 1.4 Standard Evaluation Metrics (High-Yield Definitions)

- **Pixel Accuracy:** The ratio of correctly classified pixels to the total number of pixels. A simple but class-imbalance-sensitive metric.
- **Mean Intersection over Union (mIoU):** The KTU-recommended primary metric. Computes IoU per class and averages across all classes.
- **Dice Coefficient (F1-Score):** A harmonic balance between precision and recall at the pixel level.
- **Boundary F1-Score (BF1):** Specialized for evaluating the quality of predicted object boundaries.

## 1.5 Key Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Driving:** Road, lane, and obstacle understanding for self-driving cars.
- **Medical Imaging:** Tumor, organ, and lesion delineation in CT, MRI, and histopathology slides.
- **Satellite / Aerial Imaging:** Land-cover classification (forest, water, urban, agriculture).
- **Agricultural Robotics:** Weed vs. crop distinction for precision spraying.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Surface defect pixel-level localization on manufactured parts.
- **Augmented Reality:** Real-time background substitution and scene understanding.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for **clear, one-line definitions** and for distinguishing semantic segmentation from instance segmentation. Memorize the column "Distinguishes Instances?" in the table above — it is a **recurring question pattern**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Semantic Segmentation System

The standard semantic segmentation workflow consists of four logical stages:

1. **Input Preprocessing** — The image is resized to a fixed resolution (commonly $224 \times 224$, $256 \times 256$, or $512 \times 512$), normalized, and optionally augmented (random crops, flips, color jitter).
2. **Feature Extraction (Encoder)** — A deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) — typically a backbone such as VGG-16, ResNet-50, or MobileNet — extracts hierarchical features. Early layers capture low-level edges and textures; deeper layers capture high-level semantics.
3. **Decoder / Upsampling** — The compressed feature map is progressively upsampled back to the original spatial resolution. Techniques include transposed convolutions, bilinear interpolation, and unpooling.
4. **Pixel-wise Classification** — A $1 \times 1$ convolution (or equivalent) produces a $C$-channel score map, and a softmax/argmax converts scores to final per-pixel class predictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Encoder–Decoder Paradigm is the KTU High-Yield Keyword.** Every classical segmentation architecture (FCN, U-Net, SegNet, DeepLab) is fundamentally an encoder–decoder network. The encoder "compresses" the image into semantic meaning; the decoder "reconstructs" the spatial layout.

## 2.2 Why Standard CNNs Fail — The "Where vs. What" Problem

Classical classification CNNs end with **fully connected (dense) layers**, which destroy spatial information — they answer *"What is in the image?"* but not *"Where is it?"*. Semantic segmentation needs **both** "what" **and** "where," which is why the fully connected layers are replaced with **fully convolutional** layers. This is the foundational insight of the **Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)** paper, which introduced the modern era of semantic segmentation.

## 2.3 The Three Pillars of Classical Segmentation Architectures

### 2.3.1 Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)
- Replaces all fully connected layers with $1 \times 1$ convolutions.
- Uses **transposed convolutions** (also called deconvolutions) for learnable upsampling.
- Introduces **skip connections** from earlier encoder layers to recover fine spatial detail lost during downsampling.
- Variants: **FCN-32s** (single upsampling), **FCN-16s** (one skip), **FCN-8s** (two skips) — more skips mean finer detail.

### 2.3.2 U-Net
- A symmetric **encoder–decoder** with skip connections at every corresponding level.
- Skip connections concatenate (not sum) encoder features with decoder features.
- Originally designed for biomedical image segmentation, but widely adopted in all domains.
- The **U-shape** is what gives it its name.

### 2.3.3 SegNet
- Uses a **pooling index transfer** mechanism: the max-pooling indices from the encoder are reused in the decoder for non-learnable upsampling.
- Memory-efficient because no feature maps need to be stored — only the indices.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Pay special attention to the **unit** and **range** columns.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Formula | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{PA}$ | Pixel Accuracy | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\text{IoU}_c$ | Intersection over Union (per class $c$) | $\frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\text{mIoU}$ | Mean IoU across $C$ classes | $\frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{IoU}_c$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\text{Dice}_c$ | Dice / F1 Coefficient (per class) | $\frac{2 \cdot TP_c}{2 \cdot TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\text{Loss}_{CE}$ | Per-pixel Cross-Entropy Loss | $-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| $\text{FL}$ | Focal Loss (handles class imbalance) | $-\alpha_c (1 - \hat{y}_{i,c})^{\gamma} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| $\text{Output Size}$ | Conv output spatial size | $\left\lfloor \frac{H - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | — |
| $\text{Transposed Conv}$ | Upsampled output size | $(H - 1) \cdot S - 2P + K$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Collision Avoidance:** Throughout this sheet, the vertical bar `$\vert$` is used in set-membership contexts (e.g., $y \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$), **never** the markdown table pipe `$\mid$` inside a table cell. The KTU Formula Sheet above is markdown-safe.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The encoder–decoder + skip-connection blueprint is the **architectural backbone** of nearly every modern vision system you will encounter in industry: medical AI products (tumor segmentation), ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), satellite monitoring (deforestation tracking), and even smartphone portrait-mode bokeh effects (depth-map estimation). Mastering this blueprint is non-negotiable for any KTU machine-vision engineer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pixel Accuracy from First Principles

Let the ground-truth segmentation mask be $Y \in \{0, 1, \ldots, C-1\}^{H \times W}$ and the predicted mask be $\hat{Y} \in \{0, 1, \ldots, C-1\}^{H \times W}$. The total number of pixels is $N = H \times W$.

**Step 1 — Count of Correctly Classified Pixels.**
Define the per-pixel correctness indicator:

$$\mathbb{1}_{\text{correct}}(i, j) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \hat{Y}_{i,j} = Y_{i,j} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Indicator over All Pixels.**

$$N_{\text{correct}} = \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} \mathbb{1}_{\text{correct}}(i, j)$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by the Total Number of Pixels $N$.**

$$\text{PA} = \frac{N_{\text{correct}}}{N} = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} \mathbb{1}_{\text{correct}}(i, j)$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of the Pixel-Level Confusion Matrix.**
Summing correctly classified pixels across **all** classes yields $TP_1 + TP_2 + \ldots + TP_C$ for the multi-class case, and including true negatives we obtain:

$$\text{PA} = \frac{\sum_{c=1}^{C} TP_c + TN_c}{\sum_{c=1}^{C} \left( TP_c + TN_c + FP_c + FN_c \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a binary segmentation problem (foreground vs. background), this collapses to the familiar form $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index)

Consider one class $c$ (e.g., "car"). Define:
- $TP_c$ = pixels truly class $c$ and predicted class $c$ (**True Positives**).
- $FP_c$ = pixels predicted class $c$ but actually some other class (**False Positives**).
- $FN_c$ = pixels truly class $c$ but predicted as some other class (**False Negatives**).

**Step 1 — Define the Set of Ground-Truth Pixels of Class $c$.**

$$G_c = \{(i, j) \mid Y_{i,j} = c\}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Set of Predicted Pixels of Class $c$.**

$$P_c = \{(i, j) \mid \hat{Y}_{i,j} = c\}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Intersection (Logically, "AND" of the two sets).**

$$G_c \cap P_c \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{size} = TP_c$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Union (Logically, "OR" of the two sets).**

$$G_c \cup P_c \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{size} = TP_c + FP_c + FN_c$$

**Step 5 — Form the Ratio.**

$$\text{IoU}_c = \frac{\vert G_c \cap P_c \vert}{\vert G_c \cup P_c \vert} = \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Mean IoU (mIoU)

**Step 1 — Compute IoU independently for every class.**

For $C$ classes, we obtain $C$ values: $\text{IoU}_1, \text{IoU}_2, \ldots, \text{IoU}_C$.

**Step 2 — Take the Simple Arithmetic Mean.**

$$\text{mIoU} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{IoU}_c = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> mIoU is the **official primary metric** of the major benchmark datasets such as PASCAL VOC, Cityscapes, and ADE20K. KTU examiners regard mIoU as the gold-standard answer when asked "What is the evaluation metric for semantic segmentation?"

## 3.4 Derivation of the Dice Coefficient (F1-Score at the Pixel Level)

**Step 1 — Start with the Generic F1 Formula.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Pixel-Level Precision and Recall.**

$$\text{Precision}_c = \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c}, \qquad \text{Recall}_c = \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FN_c}$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$\text{Dice}_c = \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c} \cdot \dfrac{TP_c}{TP_c + FN_c}}{\dfrac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c} + \dfrac{TP_c}{TP_c + FN_c}}$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification.**

$$\text{Dice}_c = \frac{2 \cdot TP_c}{2 \cdot TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Useful relationship between Dice and IoU:** $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$ and conversely $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{Dice}}{2 - \text{Dice}}$. Both are bounded in $[0, 1]$ and are monotonically related.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Pixel Confusion Matrix for a $4 \times 4$ Binary Segmentation Output (Class "car" vs. background).**

Consider a $4 \times 4$ image (16 pixels total) where 6 pixels are ground-truth "car" and 10 are background. The model produces the following counts:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| True Positives | $TP$ | 4 |
| False Positives | $FP$ | 1 |
| False Negatives | $FN$ | 2 |
| True Negatives | $TN$ | 9 |

**Step 1 — Pixel Accuracy.**

$$\text{PA} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{4 + 9}{4 + 9 + 1 + 2} = \frac{13}{16} = 0.8125$$

**Step 2 — IoU for the "car" class.**

$$\text{IoU}_{\text{car}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{4}{4 + 1 + 2} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714$$

**Step 3 — Dice Coefficient for the "car" class.**

$$\text{Dice}_{\text{car}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 4}{2 \cdot 4 + 1 + 2} = \frac{8}{11} \approx 0.7273$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check the IoU–Dice relationship.**

$$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5714}{1 + 0.5714} = \frac{1.1428}{1.5714} \approx 0.7273 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Verification of Pixel Counts.**

$$TP + TN + FP + FN = 4 + 9 + 1 + 2 = 16 = H \times W \quad \checkmark$$

This example is **highly exam-relevant** — a 14-mark KTU question can ask you to compute PA, IoU, and Dice given such a confusion matrix, and the verification step (Step 5) is a one-line sanity check that examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Semantic Segmentation Pipeline — End-to-End Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input RGB Image H x W x 3] --> B[Preprocessing Resize and Normalize]
    B --> C[Encoder Backbone CNN e.g. ResNet50]
    C --> D[Bottleneck Deep Low Resolution High Semantics Feature Map]
    D --> E[Decoder Upsampling via Transposed Convolution or Interpolation]
    E --> F[Skip Connections from Encoder to Decoder for Spatial Detail Recovery]
    F --> G[One by One Convolution Produces C Channel Score Map]
    G --> H[Softmax and Argmax Produces Per Pixel Class Label]
    H --> I[Final Segmentation Mask H x W x 1]
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Vision Tasks — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Image Classification One label per image] --> B[Object Detection Bounding boxes and class]
    B --> C[Semantic Segmentation Class label per pixel no instance]
    C --> D[Instance Segmentation Class label and instance ID per pixel]
    D --> E[Panoptic Segmentation Union of Semantic and Instance]
    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1a237e,color:#000
    style B fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#283593,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

## 4.3 U-Net Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Input H x W x 3] --> E1[Encoder Block 1 Conv and ReLU and MaxPool H over 2 x W over 2]
    E1 --> E2[Encoder Block 2 Conv and ReLU and MaxPool H over 4 x W over 4]
    E2 --> E3[Encoder Block 3 Conv and ReLU and MaxPool H over 8 x W over 8]
    E3 --> E4[Encoder Block 4 Conv and ReLU and MaxPool H over 16 x W over 16]
    E4 --> BN[Bottleneck Deepest Features H over 16 x W over 16]
    BN --> D4[Decoder Block 4 Upsample and Concat with E4 H over 8 x W over 8]
    D4 --> D3[Decoder Block 3 Upsample and Concat with E3 H over 4 x W over 4]
    D3 --> D2[Decoder Block 2 Upsample and Concat with E2 H over 2 x W over 2]
    D2 --> D1[Decoder Block 1 Upsample and Concat with E1 H x W]
    D1 --> OUT[One by One Conv and Softmax Output Segmentation Map H x W x C]
    style L0 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style BN fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Pixel-Level Confusion Matrix — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pixel Under Evaluation] --> Q{Does Predicted Class Equal True Class}
    Q -- Yes and True Class is Positive --> TP[True Positive Correctly Segmented Target Region]
    Q -- Yes and True Class is Negative --> TN[True Negative Correctly Segmented Background]
    Q -- No and Predicted is Positive --> FP[False Positive Over Segmentation]
    Q -- No and Predicted is Negative --> FN[False Negative Under Segmentation]
    TP --> MET[Used to Compute IoU Dice and Pixel Accuracy]
    TN --> MET
    FP --> MET
    FN --> MET
    style P fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style TP fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style TN fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style FP fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    style FN fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are strictly aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme** ESE pattern: **Part A** carries 3 marks each, **Part B** carries 14 marks each with mandatory **internal choice**. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. The mark distribution and cognitive level mapping follow Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L6).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define semantic segmentation. How does it differ from instance segmentation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Semantic segmentation is the computer vision task of assigning a predefined class label to **every pixel** in an image, producing a dense per-pixel classification map. The key distinguishing feature is that it does **not** differentiate between individual instances of the same class — all pixels belonging to "car" share one label, regardless of whether there are one or ten cars. In contrast, **instance segmentation** goes a step further by assigning a **unique instance ID** to every individual object, so each car in a scene is segmented as a separate entity (Car-1, Car-2, …). In essence, semantic segmentation answers *"What class is each pixel?"* while instance segmentation answers *"What class is each pixel and which object does it belong to?"* — a distinction worth 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for semantic behavior, and 1 mark for the contrast with instance segmentation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**List any three real-world applications of semantic segmentation and state the primary evaluation metric used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Three applications (1.5 marks):**
1. Autonomous driving — road, lane, and obstacle pixel-level understanding.
2. Medical image analysis — tumor and organ delineation in CT/MRI scans.
3. Satellite imagery — land-cover classification (water, forest, urban, agriculture).

**Primary evaluation metric (1 mark):** The standard primary metric is **Mean Intersection over Union (mIoU)**, which averages the per-class IoU across all $C$ classes:

$$\text{mIoU} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}$$

**Why mIoU and not Pixel Accuracy (0.5 mark):** Pixel accuracy is misleading under class imbalance (e.g., a road occupies 70% of a driving scene; predicting "road" everywhere yields high accuracy but zero car-detection). mIoU penalizes missed small classes and is therefore the de-facto standard.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Every Part B question carries an **internal choice** between two alternative questions. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B, not both. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **encoder–decoder architecture** used in semantic segmentation. Discuss the role of **skip connections** in recovering spatial detail.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Define **IoU** and **Dice Coefficient**. For a binary segmentation task on a $5 \times 5$ image, the confusion matrix for the positive class is: $TP = 8$, $FP = 2$, $FN = 3$, $TN = 12$. Compute the **IoU**, **Dice Coefficient**, and **Pixel Accuracy** for the positive class.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
A standard encoder–decoder semantic segmentation network has four stages:
1. **Encoder:** Successive convolution + ReLU + max-pooling blocks progressively reduce spatial resolution while increasing the channel depth. Typical backbones are VGG-16, ResNet-50, or MobileNet.
2. **Bottleneck:** The deepest feature map, with the smallest spatial size and the richest semantic content.
3. **Decoder:** Transposed convolutions (or bilinear upsampling) progressively restore spatial resolution. Each upsampling stage doubles the height and width.
4. **Output head:** A $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the $C$-channel feature map to a per-pixel class score, followed by softmax and argmax.

**Role of skip connections (4 marks):**
- Max-pooling in the encoder discards fine spatial detail (where exactly is the road edge?).
- Pure decoder upsampling produces **coarse, blurry** outputs because the small bottleneck feature map lacks the resolution to reconstruct sharp boundaries.
- **Skip connections** copy feature maps from earlier (high-resolution) encoder layers directly to the corresponding decoder layers and concatenate or sum them.
- This **fuses low-level spatial detail** (edges, textures) with **high-level semantic context** (what the region is), enabling the network to predict **pixel-accurate** boundaries.
- FCN-8s (with two skip connections) outperforms FCN-32s (no skip) by ~3–5% mIoU on PASCAL VOC — a concrete proof of their value (1 mark for the quantitative justification).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**IoU Definition (1.5 marks):**
IoU is the ratio of the intersection area to the union area between the predicted and ground-truth segmentation masks for a given class.

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$$

**Dice Coefficient Definition (1.5 marks):**
The Dice Coefficient (also called F1-score at the pixel level) is twice the ratio of the intersection to the total predicted + ground-truth pixels.

$$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

Given: $TP = 8$, $FP = 2$, $FN = 3$, $TN = 12$.

**Step 1 — IoU.**

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{8}{8 + 2 + 3} = \frac{8}{13} \approx 0.6154$$

**Step 2 — Dice Coefficient.**

$$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot 8}{2 \cdot 8 + 2 + 3} = \frac{16}{21} \approx 0.7619$$

**Step 3 — Pixel Accuracy (Binary Form).**

$$\text{PA} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{8 + 12}{8 + 12 + 2 + 3} = \frac{20}{25} = 0.80$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Verification.**

$$TP + TN + FP + FN = 8 + 12 + 2 + 3 = 25 = 5 \times 5 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Relationship Check.**

$$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.6154}{1 + 0.6154} = \frac{1.2308}{1.6154} \approx 0.7619 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **semantic segmentation, instance segmentation, and panoptic segmentation**. Provide one real-world example where each is the preferred choice and justify why.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)** architecture. How does it differ from a standard classification CNN such as VGG-16? What problem do the FCN-32s, FCN-16s, and FCN-8s variants address?

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Semantic Segmentation | Instance Segmentation | Panoptic Segmentation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output granularity | Per-pixel class | Per-pixel class + instance ID | Per-pixel class + instance ID (unified) |
| Distinguishes instances? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Handles "stuff" classes (sky, road)? | Yes | No (only "things") | Yes (unified) |
| Example use case | Road segmentation in ADAS | Counting individual cells in microscopy | Cityscape scene parsing |

**Examples and Justifications (4 marks):**
1. **Semantic segmentation — Road detection in autonomous driving (1.5 marks):** The vehicle only needs to know *where the road is*, not which specific patch of road corresponds to which instance. A single "road" label is sufficient, and "stuff" classes like road, sky, and sidewalk are inherently non-instanced.
2. **Instance segmentation — Cell counting in biomedical microscopy (1 mark):** A pathologist needs to know *how many individual cells* are present. Semantic segmentation would merge all touching cells into one blob. Instance segmentation provides a unique ID per cell, enabling automated counting.
3. **Panoptic segmentation — Cityscape parsing for AR navigation (1.5 marks):** An AR system in a city needs to label *every* pixel correctly. Cars, pedestrians (things) need instance IDs, while sky, road, buildings (stuff) need only class labels. Panoptic segmentation unifies both into a single coherent output.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**FCN Architecture (3 marks):**
The Fully Convolutional Network replaces every fully connected (FC) layer of a standard classification CNN (e.g., VGG-16) with a $1 \times 1$ convolutional layer. This converts the network from one that produces a single class probability vector into one that produces a spatial heatmap of class scores. The final coarse heatmap is then **upsampled** to the original image resolution using transposed convolutions. The result is a per-pixel class prediction map of size $H \times W \times C$.

**Key Differences from VGG-16 (2 marks):**
- VGG-16 ends with two FC layers of size 4096 and a 1000-way softmax, producing a $1 \times 1 \times 1000$ output (no spatial info).
- FCN replaces these FC layers with $1 \times 1$ convolutions, preserving the $H/32 \times W/32$ spatial grid, which can then be upsampled to a full $H \times W$ prediction map.

**FCN-32s / 16s / 8s Variants (2 marks):**
- **FCN-32s:** Single 32× upsampling at the end. Output is very coarse — fine boundaries are lost.
- **FCN-16s:** Combines the final 32-stride feature map with the 16-stride feature map from an earlier encoder layer via a skip connection, then upsamples 16×. Sharper boundaries.
- **FCN-8s:** Combines 32-stride, 16-stride, and 8-stride feature maps. Two skip connections. Best boundary quality.
- These variants directly address the **loss of spatial detail** caused by repeated pooling. More skip connections = finer detail = higher mIoU.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Semantic and Instance Segmentation:** If asked "Does semantic segmentation distinguish instances?", the answer is **NO** — never write "Yes". One mark is routinely lost here.
> 2. **Confusing IoU and Dice:** IoU divides by $TP+FP+FN$ (the union), Dice divides by $2\cdot TP+FP+FN$ (the sum of sizes). Mixing up these denominators is the most common arithmetic error.
> 3. **Forgetting to Average Across Classes:** When asked for mIoU, you must compute IoU for **each class** first, then take the **arithmetic mean**. Reporting a single class IoU as mIoU loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Pixel-Count Sanity Check:** In numerical problems, always verify $TP + TN + FP + FN = H \times W$. Examiners reward this verification step with 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Writing "Deconvolution" for Transposed Convolution:** Strictly call it a **transposed convolution**. "Deconvolution" is a misnomer and is penalized in rubric-based valuation.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Semantic Segmentation**

- **Core Definition:** Semantic segmentation = dense, per-pixel classification. **Does NOT distinguish instances.**
- **Four-Task Hierarchy:** Classification → Detection → Semantic Segmentation → Instance Segmentation → Panoptic Segmentation. (Increasing granularity.)
- **Architectural Paradigm:** All classical segmentation networks are **encoder–decoder** with **skip connections**.
- **Three Pillars to Know:** **FCN** (replaces FC with $1\times 1$ conv), **U-Net** (symmetric + concatenating skips), **SegNet** (pooling-index transfer).
- **Primary Metric:** **Mean Intersection over Union (mIoU)** — average of per-class IoU across all $C$ classes.
- **Formula Triad:** IoU = $TP / (TP+FP+FN)$ ; Dice = $2\cdot TP / (2\cdot TP+FP+FN)$ ; PA = $(TP+TN) / N$.
- **Dice–IoU Relation:** $\text{Dice} = 2\cdot\text{IoU} / (1+\text{IoU})$ ; $\text{IoU} = \text{Dice} / (2-\text{Dice})$.
- **Loss Function:** Per-pixel **cross-entropy** is the standard; **Focal Loss** is used under class imbalance.
- **Skip Connection Purpose:** Recover fine spatial detail lost during encoder pooling. FCN-8s > FCN-16s > FCN-32s.
- **Upsampling Methods:** **Transposed convolution** (learnable) vs. **bilinear interpolation** (fixed, no parameters).
- **Output Layer:** A $1 \times 1$ convolution + **softmax/argmax** converts the $C$-channel feature map to a class label per pixel.
- **Class Imbalance Caveat:** Pixel accuracy is misleading; mIoU is the trustworthy metric.
- **Top Applications (one-liners):** Autonomous driving, medical imaging, satellite land-cover, agriculture, AR, industrial defect detection.
- **Pitfall #1:** "Instance segmentation = semantic + counting." **Wrong.** It assigns unique instance IDs, not just counts.
- **Pitfall #2:** "Deconvolution = transposed convolution." **Misnomer.** Always use "transposed convolution."
- **Pitfall #3:** "FCN-32s is the best." **Wrong.** FCN-8s (most skips) is the best FCN variant.
- **Final Memory Hook:** **E**ncoder compresses, **D**ecoder reconstructs, **S**kips refine, **mIoU** evaluates. 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Detection and Matching: Points and Patches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT834 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feature Detection and Matching: Points and Patches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Machine Vision, **point features** and **patch features** are localized image structures that exhibit distinctive, repeatable, and robust signatures under variations in illumination, scale, rotation, and viewpoint. Formally, a **point feature** is a salient keypoint whose neighbourhood (patch) carries a unique photometric or geometric signature, while a **patch feature** is the local intensity/gradient pattern surrounding such a point. The combined pipeline of *detection* (locating stable keypoints) and *description* (encoding the patch into a descriptor vector) followed by *matching* (correspondence across images) is the foundational building block of image registration, object recognition, 3-D reconstruction, SLAM, and motion tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A feature is *interesting* only if it satisfies three properties — **Repeatability** (detected under varied conditions), **Distinctiveness** (unique descriptor), and **Efficiency** (computationally tractable). KTU examiners expect students to recall these properties verbatim.

**Key Entities:**

- **Interest Point (Keypoint):** A pixel location $(x, y)$ that is locally unique.
- **Patch:** A square sub-image of size $n \times n$ centred on the keypoint.
- **Descriptor:** An $N$-dimensional feature vector encoding the patch (e.g., 128-D for SIFT, 64-D for SURF, 32-D for ORB).
- **Match:** A pair of descriptors whose distance (Euclidean / Hamming) lies below a threshold $\tau$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **forensic detective** matching fingerprints found at two different crime scenes. Your eye naturally ignores the noise (smeared background), and instead anchors onto the *minutiae* — the ridge endings and bifurcations. These minutiae are analogous to **interest points**: they are stable, sparse, and unique. The small skin patch around each minutia forms a **patch**, and the numerical encoding of ridge orientation/density around it is the **descriptor**. Two fingerprints are matched by comparing the descriptor sets.

In images, points such as **corners, blobs, and T-junctions** play the role of minutiae. Unlike edges (which can shift in appearance drastically with viewpoint) or flat regions (which contain no unique signal), corners encode a 2-D signal change in multiple directions — making them mathematically and perceptually ideal for matching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Corners, not Edges or Flat Regions?** A flat region has zero gradient in every direction (ambiguous). An edge has gradient in only *one* direction (aperture problem — motion along the edge is unobservable). A corner has gradient in *at least two* dominant directions, fully constraining the local geometry.

## 1.3 The Detection–Description–Matching Trinity

| Stage | Goal | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection** | Find stable, repeatable keypoints | $(x, y, \sigma, \theta)$ tuples |
| **Description** | Encode local patch into a vector | Descriptor $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ |
| **Matching** | Establish correspondences across images | Index pairs $(i, j)$ with $d(\mathbf{d}_i, \mathbf{d}_j) < \tau$ |

This trinity is the conceptual backbone of every modern feature pipeline (Harris, SIFT, SURF, ORB, AKAZE).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Local Image Structure — Why "Second Moment"?

The most influential measure of "how corner-like" a neighbourhood is comes from examining how intensity $I$ changes when the window is shifted by $(u, v)$. The **sum of squared differences (SSD)** of intensities under a Gaussian-weighted shift is:

$$E(u,v) = \sum_{x,y} w(x,y)\,[I(x+u, y+v) - I(x,y)]^2$$

Applying a first-order Taylor expansion $I(x+u, y+v) \approx I(x,y) + u\,I_x + v\,I_y$:

$$E(u,v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}$$

where $M$ is the **structure tensor** (second-moment matrix):

$$M = w(x,y) \otimes \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix}$$

Here $w(x,y)$ is a Gaussian window $w(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$, and $I_x = \partial I/\partial x$, $I_y = \partial I/\partial y$ are image gradients (typically obtained via Sobel or Prewitt kernels).

## 2.2 Eigenvalue Analysis — The Heart of Harris

Since $M$ is symmetric positive semi-definite, it has real non-negative eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ corresponding to principal curvatures of $E(u,v)$. Three cases arise:

| Condition on $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ | Region Type | Discriminant |
|---|---|---|
| Both small | Flat region | $\det M \approx 0$, $\text{trace }M \approx 0$ |
| One large, one small | Edge | $\det M \approx 0$, $\text{trace }M$ large |
| Both large | **Corner** | $\det M$ large, $\text{trace }M$ large |

To avoid explicit eigenvalue decomposition (costly), Harris & Stephens (1988) proposed the **Harris corner response**:

$$R = \det(M) - k\,(\text{trace }M)^2 = \lambda_1\lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard empirical value:** $k \in [0.04,\ 0.06]$. KTU questions frequently quote $k = 0.04$ — the original Harris paper value.

**Classification rule:**
- $R > 0$ and large → **Corner**
- $R < 0$ and $|R|$ large → **Edge**
- $|R|$ small → **Flat region**

## 2.3 From Harris to Scale Invariance — Why SIFT?

Harris is *rotation invariant* (because $M$ is rotationally equivariant) but **not scale invariant**. A corner at zoom level $\sigma_1$ may vanish at $\sigma_2$ if the corner is "smoothed away" by Gaussian blur. To achieve scale invariance, **Lowe's SIFT (2004)** introduces a *scale-space* representation.

### 2.3.1 Scale-Space Construction

$$L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)$$

where the 2-D Gaussian is:

$$G(x, y, \sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Scale space is discretized into **octaves** (each octave = doubling of $\sigma$) and **levels** (typically $s = 3$ intermediate levels per octave).

### 2.3.2 Difference of Gaussians (DoG)

$$D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)$$

DoG is a band-pass filter that approximates the **scale-normalized Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG)**:

$$D(x, y, \sigma) \approx (k-1)\,\sigma^2\,\nabla^2 G$$

A scale-space extremum of $D$ (max or min over 26 neighbours: 8 in current level + 9 above + 9 below) is a candidate keypoint.

### 2.3.3 Keypoint Localization (Sub-pixel Refinement)

A 3-D quadratic fit is performed by Taylor-expanding $D(\mathbf{x})$ around the candidate $\mathbf{x}_0$:

$$D(\mathbf{x}) = D(\mathbf{x}_0) + \frac{\partial D^\top}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\,\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^\top \frac{\partial^2 D}{\partial \mathbf{x}^2}\,\mathbf{x}$$

Setting derivative to zero gives the offset:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = -\frac{\partial^2 D^{-1}}{\partial \mathbf{x}^2}\,\frac{\partial D}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$$

If $\lvert \hat{\mathbf{x}} \rvert > 0.5$ in any dimension, the candidate is shifted and interpolation continues. Keypoints with low contrast ($|D(\hat{\mathbf{x}})| < 0.03$) or strong edge response (using a Hessian ratio test, threshold $\approx 10$) are discarded.

## 2.4 Feature Description — SIFT Descriptor

For each surviving keypoint at scale $\sigma$ and dominant orientation $\theta$:

1. Rotate a $16 \times 16$ patch into the canonical $\theta$ frame.
2. Divide into $4 \times 4 = 16$ sub-patches of $4 \times 4$.
3. In each sub-patch, build an **8-bin orientation histogram** weighted by gradient magnitude and a Gaussian falloff.
4. Concatenate: $4 \times 4 \times 8 = \mathbf{128}$-dimensional descriptor.
5. **Normalize** to unit length; cap values at $0.2$, then re-normalize (mitigates illumination non-linearities).

## 2.5 Feature Matching

Given two descriptor sets $\{\mathbf{d}_i^A\}$ and $\{\mathbf{d}_j^B\}$, the **Nearest Neighbour Distance Ratio (NNDR)** test of Lowe is:

$$\text{NNDR} = \frac{\lVert \mathbf{d}_i^A - \mathbf{d}_{1\text{nn}}^B \rVert_2}{\lVert \mathbf{d}_i^A - \mathbf{d}_{2\text{nn}}^B \rVert_2} < \rho$$

A typical threshold is $\rho \in [0.6,\ 0.8]$. Accepting a match requires the *first* nearest neighbour to be significantly closer than the *second*. The search uses **k-d trees** or FLANN for sub-linear complexity.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = w \otimes \begin{bmatrix}I_x^2 & I_x I_y\\ I_x I_y & I_y^2\end{bmatrix}$ | Structure tensor | Harris detector |
| 2 | $R = \det M - k(\text{trace }M)^2$ | Harris response | Corner scoring |
| 3 | $E(u,v) \approx \begin{bmatrix}u & v\end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix}u \\ v\end{bmatrix}$ | SSD approximation | Harris derivation |
| 4 | $G(x,y,\sigma) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Gaussian kernel | Scale-space |
| 5 | $L(x,y,\sigma) = G * I$ | Scale-space representation | Multi-scale detection |
| 6 | $D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma)$ | Difference of Gaussians | SIFT keypoint detection |
| 7 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = -\dfrac{\partial^2 D^{-1}}{\partial \mathbf{x}^2}\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ | Sub-pixel keypoint offset | Localization refinement |
| 8 | $\text{NNDR} = \dfrac{\lVert \mathbf{d}_i - \mathbf{d}_{1\text{nn}} \rVert_2}{\lVert \mathbf{d}_i - \mathbf{d}_{2\text{nn}} \rVert_2} < \rho$ | Match acceptance | Descriptor matching |
| 9 | $\det M = \lambda_1\lambda_2$, $\text{trace }M = \lambda_1+\lambda_2$ | Eigenvalue identities | Region classification |
| 10 | $\text{Tr}(H)^2 / \det(H) < (r+1)^2 / r$ | Edge-rejection (Hessian ratio) | SIFT stability |

**Real-World Utility:** Harris–SIFT pipelines drive panorama stitching in smartphones, autonomous-vehicle SLAM (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono), medical image registration, and forensic biometric matching. The detector–descriptor paradigm is essentially the "eyes" of any modern computer-vision system.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From SSD to Structure Tensor $M$

**Step 1 (Setup).** We begin with the sum of squared differences under a small displacement $(u,v)$:

$$E(u,v) = \sum_{x,y} w(x,y)\,[I(x+u,\,y+v) - I(x,y)]^2$$

**Step 2 (Taylor Expansion).** Expand $I(x+u, y+v)$ around $(x,y)$ using first-order terms only:

$$I(x+u, y+v) \approx I(x,y) + u\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial I}{\partial y} = I + uI_x + vI_y$$

**Step 3 (Substitute the difference).** The bracketed term becomes:

$$I(x+u,y+v) - I(x,y) \approx uI_x + vI_y$$

**Step 4 (Square and distribute).** Substituting back into $E$:

$$E(u,v) \approx \sum_{x,y} w(x,y)\,\bigl[uI_x + vI_y\bigr]^2$$

Expanding the square:

$$E(u,v) \approx \sum_{x,y} w(x,y)\,\bigl[u^2 I_x^2 + 2uv\,I_x I_y + v^2 I_y^2\bigr]$$

**Step 5 (Collect coefficients).** Treating $u, v$ as constants wrt the summation:

$$E(u,v) \approx u^2 \sum w\,I_x^2 \;+\; 2uv \sum w\,I_x I_y \;+\; v^2 \sum w\,I_y^2$$

Define the Gaussian-weighted averages $\langle \cdot \rangle = \sum w(\cdot)$:

$$E(u,v) \approx u^2 \langle I_x^2\rangle + 2uv\,\langle I_x I_y\rangle + v^2 \langle I_y^2\rangle$$

**Step 6 (Matrix form).** Recognizing the quadratic form:

$$E(u,v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2\rangle & \langle I_x I_y\rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y\rangle & \langle I_y^2\rangle \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the celebrated quadratic approximation that localizes corner response. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Harris Response from Eigenvalues

Let $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ be the eigenvalues of $M$. Recall the standard identities:

$$\text{trace}(M) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2, \qquad \det(M) = \lambda_1\lambda_2$$

**Step 1.** Define $R = \lambda_1\lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$.

**Step 2.** Substitute identities:

$$R = \det(M) - k\,(\text{trace}(M))^2$$

**Step 3 (Region classification).** Using $\alpha = \lambda_1 / \lambda_2$:

$$R = \lambda_1\lambda_2\bigl[1 - k(\alpha + 2 + 1/\alpha)\bigr]$$

- If both small → $R$ small (flat).
- If $\alpha \to 0$ or $\infty$ (edge) → bracket $\to -\infty$ → $R < 0$.
- If $\alpha \to 1$ (corner) and both large → $R > 0$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DoG Approximates Scale-Normalized LoG

**Step 1 (Heat diffusion relation).** The Gaussian satisfies the diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial G}{\partial \sigma} = \sigma \nabla^2 G$$

**Step 2 (Finite difference).** Approximating the LHS by a forward difference between scales $k\sigma$ and $\sigma$:

$$L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma) \approx (k\sigma - \sigma)\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial \sigma} = (k-1)\sigma^2 \nabla^2 L$$

**Step 3 (Conclude).** Since $L = G * I$ and $\nabla^2 L = \nabla^2 G * I$:

$$D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma) \approx (k-1)\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G * I$$

Hence, extrema of $D$ correspond to extrema of the **scale-normalized Laplacian** $\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G$, which provides scale-invariance. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sub-pixel Keypoint Offset

Let $\mathbf{x}_0 = (x_0, y_0, \sigma_0)^T$ be a DoG extremum. The Taylor expansion of $D$ around $\mathbf{x}_0$ is:

$$D(\mathbf{x}) = D(\mathbf{x}_0) + \frac{\partial D^\top}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\,\boldsymbol{\delta} + \frac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\delta}^\top \frac{\partial^2 D}{\partial \mathbf{x}^2}\,\boldsymbol{\delta}$$

**Step 1.** Differentiate with respect to $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial D}{\partial \boldsymbol{\delta}} = \frac{\partial D^\top}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + \frac{\partial^2 D}{\partial \mathbf{x}^2}\,\boldsymbol{\delta} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\delta}}$:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\delta}} = -\left(\frac{\partial^2 D}{\partial \mathbf{x}^2}\right)^{-1}\frac{\partial D}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$$

**Step 3.** Re-evaluate at the refined location:

$$D(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = D(\mathbf{x}_0) + \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\partial D^\top}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\delta}}$$

If $|D(\hat{\mathbf{x}})| < 0.03$, the keypoint is rejected as low-contrast. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Detectors

| Detector | Year | Invariance | Detector Type | Descriptor | Key Advantage | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Harris** | 1988 | Rotation | Corner (eigenvalue) | Optional patch SSD | Fast, robust in scale | Not scale invariant |
| **Harris-Laplace** | 2004 | Rotation + Scale | Corner + scale selection | Patch gradient histogram | Combines both | Manual scale selection |
| **SIFT** | 2004 | Rotation + Scale + Illumination | DoG blob | 128-D float | Gold-standard accuracy | Patent, slow |
| **SURF** | 2006 | Rotation + Scale | Hessian-blob | 64-D float | Faster than SIFT | Lower accuracy in extreme cases |
| **ORB** | 2011 | Rotation + Scale (approx) | FAST corner | 32-B binary BRIEF | Very fast, free | Less robust to large viewpoint |
| **AKAZE** | 2013 | Rotation + Scale | Non-linear diffusion | M-LDB binary | Nonlinear scale-space, fast | Newer, less widespread |

## 3.6 Edge-Response Rejection — Hessian Ratio

For a candidate keypoint, compute the $2 \times 2$ Hessian of $D$ at its location. Let $\alpha, \beta$ be its eigenvalues with $\alpha > \beta$. The keypoint is rejected as an edge if:

$$\frac{\text{Tr}(H)^2}{\det(H)} = \frac{(\alpha+\beta)^2}{\alpha\beta} \geq \frac{(r+1)^2}{r}$$

Lowe suggests $r = 10$. This eliminates unstable keypoints lying along edges where $\det$ is small but $\text{Tr}$ is large. $\blacksquare$
